title: design: external OAuth 2.0 / OIDC provider — third-party app integration via standard authorization-code flow
description:
## Why now

Cloudflare's 2026-06 "OAuth for All" announcement (https://blog.cloudflare.com/oauth-for-all/) opened OAuth delegation to any Worker — every developer on CF can now ship an OAuth-server surface. That raises a substrate-trust question for ART: should notme expose a CF-compatible OAuth dance so third-party apps that already speak OAuth (and may be deployed on CF) can use notme as their identity provider, the way they'd OAuth into Google / GitHub today?

## What's already shipped vs missing

notme's identity-bridge surface (per cloister.capnp `wellKnownIdentityBridge` route kind) already ships: `/.well-known/openid-configuration`, `/.well-known/jwks.json`, `/.well-known/webfinger`, `/.well-known/nostr.json`, `POST /oauth/token` (client_credentials grant).

Missing for a real third-party-app OAuth provider: `GET /oauth/authorize` (auth-code flow entrypoint), consent UI, apps registry (`client_id`/`client_secret`/`redirect_uri`), auth-code → token exchange on `/oauth/token`, PKCE (RFC 7636), token introspection (RFC 7662), revoke (RFC 7009), refresh-token grant.

## Decision matrix

A. **Wrap Ory Hydra as a sidecar** — battle-tested (CF runs Hydra), full OAuth/OIDC; cost: new substrate dep, new SQL backend, duplicates trust-root concerns.

B. **Implement the spec slice in-stack** (~2-3k LOC TypeScript inside the existing notme Worker) — reuses notme's Ed25519 master for JWT issuance; trust root stays inside Interlace pipeline; ART takes responsibility for spec compliance. **Recommended.**

## Scope shape

`worker/src/oauth/` — authorize.ts, consent.ts, token.ts (extend existing), introspect.ts, revoke.ts, apps-registry.ts, auth-code-state.ts, pkce.ts. Cloister-side: extend `cloister.capnp` `identity-bridge` route paths under the same `wellKnownIdentityBridge` route kind (no new tenant; same trust boundary).

## Open decisions before implementation

1. Consent UI hosting: same Worker, or R2-hosted page + state callback? (Recommend: same Worker.)
2. Apps registry storage: new DO class, or extend notme's DO? (Recommend: extend existing.)
3. Token-lifetime defaults: align with CF's "multiple hours" or with Interlace's short-lease posture? (Recommend: short-lease Interlace-aligned.)
4. OAuth scopes: 1:1 mirror of Interlace scope vocabulary, or curated subset? (Recommend: 1:1 initially; add curation when external devs ask.)

## Why P2

Product-direction question, not substrate gap. ART works today with Interlace-only authn. The OAuth surface unlocks external developer adoption — ecosystem play, not a reliability fix. Land after substrate-trust layer is stable but before first external developer onboarding push.

## Full design

Staged at `docs/design/011-external-oauth-provider.md` (commit pending) — covers context, decision matrix, scope shape, file tree, all four open questions, and out-of-scope (SSO/SAML federation; centralized hosting; refresh-token leaked-token detection).

## Out of scope

- Replacing Interlace cert-based authn for internal substrate trust. Interlace stays the substrate-tier mechanism.
- SSO / SAML / corporate-IdP federation.
- Centralized hosting (à la Auth0 / Clerk). notme stays self-hosted by the cluster operator.

## Related

- https://blog.cloudflare.com/oauth-for-all/
- cloister.capnp identity-bridge tenant + ADR-0007 (Interlace), ADR-0018 (notme co-location)
- `~/remotes/art/notme/docs/design/011-external-oauth-provider.md` (staged)

comments.text:
Deprioritized to P3 (2026-06-25): parked pending real external-integrator demand — matches the doc's own "P2 ecosystem play, not a substrate gap" rationale. ART works today on Interlace-only authn.

The design doc undercounts what's already built. The JWT-minting primitive it would consume is notme-d87ef2 (dispatched standalone).

title: P1: /authorize redirect mints DPoP-bound token — rejected by cnf check
description: /authorize page calls POST /token which mints a DPoP-bound token (cnf.jkt). After the cnf downgrade fix, verifyAccessToken correctly rejects tokens with cnf. The redirect flow is broken.\n\nFix: mint an unbound redirect token inside the DO (no cnf claim). Short-lived, single-use, EdDSA-signed. The /authorize page calls this instead of /token.

title: Expose JSON CABundle endpoint at /internal/ca-bundle for cloister consumption
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-09 from the cloister-side fetcher work (cloister-b89fdb).

## Background

cloister's lease middleware (cloister-bd7770) consumes a signed JSON `CABundle` to verify Signet ephemeral certs. The bundle shape is what notme already uses internally (`notme/worker/src/revocation.ts:CABundle`):

```
interface CABundle {
  epoch: number;
  seqno: number;
  keys: Record<string, string>;        // kid → b64-std raw Ed25519 pubkey
  keyId: string;
  prevKeyId?: string;
  issuedAt: number;                    // Unix-seconds
  signature: string;                   // Ed25519 over bundleCanonical, b64-std
}
```

Today notme exposes `/.well-known/ca-bundle.pem` (PEM-formatted X.509). cloister needs the raw JSON bundle (not PEM) because:
- The Interlace cert chain is Ed25519 raw-key, not X.509 hierarchies
- cloister's `bundleCanonical` (lifted from notme/revocation.ts) produces the canonical bytes that signet's Go side signs

## What to do

Add a service-binding-only endpoint at `/internal/ca-bundle` that:
1. Returns `Content-Type: application/json` with the current `CABundle` JSON
2. Calls `SIGNING_AUTHORITY.getCurrentCABundle()` (or whatever the existing internal accessor is named) to fetch the latest signed bundle
3. Sets `Cache-Control: public, max-age=240` (4 min — must stay strictly inside notme's `BUNDLE_MAX_AGE_MS = 5min` so cloister's 4-min refresh interval always sees a non-stale bundle; per cloister-e195ea audit amendment)
4. Returns 503 if no bundle is currently signed (cloister fails closed, then retries)

The endpoint MUST NOT be reachable from external HTTP (no Authorization-by-default-routes; restrict to service-binding callers via the existing internal-only path policy).

## Contract with cloister

cloister's fetcher: `cloister/src/storage/notme-bundle-fetcher.ts:notmeBundleFetcher`
- Calls `env.NOTME.fetch(new Request("https://notme-bot/internal/ca-bundle", { method: "GET" }))`
- Expects `200` + JSON body matching the CABundle shape
- Treats anything else as `null` (fail-closed via cloister's getCABundle)

cloister verifies the bundle's Ed25519 signature against `INTERLACE_ROOT_PUBKEY` after fetching, so this endpoint doesn't need to gate on the caller's identity — the signature is the trust anchor. But adding the service-binding restriction is defense-in-depth.

## Acceptance

- `/internal/ca-bundle` returns the current signed bundle as JSON
- The endpoint is unreachable from public HTTP (service-binding-only)
- A test in notme demonstrates signing the bundle once + serving it via the endpoint
- Cross-repo smoke: cloister's `task smoke` (when it runs against a notme replica) successfully fetches + verifies the bundle

## Files (notme-side)

- `notme/worker/worker.ts` — add the `/internal/ca-bundle` route
- `notme/worker/src/revocation.ts` — likely already has the bundle accessor; expose if needed
- `notme/worker/src/__tests__/internal-ca-bundle.test.ts` (NEW) — round-trip via the service binding

## Refs

- cloister-bd7770 — lease middleware (consumer)
- cloister-c614ae — bundle signature verification
- cloister-e195ea — CA bundle cache + 4-min refresh interval
- ADR-0007 audit amendment 2026-05-08 (cloister)

Working on branch `notme-049e5f-internal-ca-bundle` from current `main`.

title: bug(dpop): enforce proof alg/key pairing and reject private JWK material
description: RFC 9449 section 4.3 requires an acceptable asymmetric alg and requires the embedded proof JWK to contain no private key. The shared verifier currently ignores pHeader.alg, selects verification from kty/crv, and does not reject `d`/other private members. Pin ES256 to EC P-256 and EdDSA to OKP Ed25519, reject mismatches/unsupported algorithms with stable codes, and reject private JWK material before import. Mirror the private-key rejection in worker token-endpoint validation.

comments.text: Implemented test-first: proof alg is now pinned to ES256/EC P-256 or EdDSA/OKP Ed25519, private JWK members are rejected before import, malformed imports and verify failures map to stable DPoPVerificationError codes, and worker token-endpoint validation rejects private JWK material. Fresh `task worker:check` passes (371 tests + 6 todo; 18 worker-pool tests), package build/format pass. Awaiting final reviewer verdict and commit.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

acceptance_criteria: `task worker:check` passes with regression tests for alg/key mismatch, unsupported/symmetric/none alg values, and embedded private JWK material.

title: Audit + ship OCI image `notme:0.1.0` consumable by cloister cluster.capnp
description:
Filed 2026-05-10 from cloister's deployment-readiness audit.

## What needs to happen

cloister's `cluster.capnp` (the multi-bundle deployment manifest at `~/remotes/art/cloister/cluster.capnp`) expects an OCI image tagged **`notme:0.1.0`** for the `notme-identity` bundle. cloister's `task cluster:up` invokes a compose runtime (nerdctl/podman/docker) and assumes this image is locally available (or pullable from a registry).

Today notme has apko + melange configs at:
- `packages/apko-notme.yaml`
- `packages/melange-workerd.yaml`
- `packages/melange-notme-app.yaml`

But it's not clear from outside notme whether `task image` (or equivalent) actually:
1. Produces an image tagged `notme:0.1.0`.
2. Bundles the worker (`worker/dist/`) into the image at a path workerd can boot from.
3. Embeds `worker/wrangler.toml`-equivalent config that lets it serve on port 8788 (the port cluster.capnp expects).
4. Survives `docker load < notme.tar` + `docker run -p 8788:8788 notme:0.1.0` → 200 on `/health`.

## Acceptance

- `task image` (or whatever the build target is — name it if different) produces `notme.tar` or pushes to a local registry.
- `docker load < notme.tar` (or pull) makes `notme:0.1.0` available locally.
- `docker run -p 8788:8788 notme:0.1.0` boots and responds 200 on `/health` (or notme's equivalent liveness endpoint).
- Document the build invocation in notme's README so a cloister operator can reproduce it.
- If the apko configs need updates (version pin, port, entrypoint), update them.
- If notme has its OWN cluster.capnp / equivalent topology, mention how the two compose (cross-link).

## Cross-repo context

- **cloister** at `~/remotes/art/cloister/cluster.capnp` declares `image = "notme:0.1.0"` for the `notme-identity` bundle.
- The wire from cloister-router to notme is the workerd service binding (named `NOTME` in cloister-router's bindings, pointing at notme-identity via `serviceBindingProxy` for `/identity/*`).
- For the simple-deploy "docker run cloister-bundled" story, see cloister-side bead `cloister-db99cd` (whether to co-locate notme inside cloister's workerd as a single image — different question; this bead is the separate-container path).

## Refs

- cloister's `~/remotes/art/cloister/cluster.capnp` (the consuming manifest)
- cloister's `~/remotes/art/cloister/docs/deployment/cluster-in-a-pod.md` (operator guide that calls into this)
- cloister-db99cd (sibling: should notme co-locate instead of ship separately?)

## Out of scope

- Co-location with cloister (that's `cloister-db99cd`).
- Multi-tenant scaling of notme (separate concern).
- Anything beyond producing a runnable, tagged image.

title: feat(dpop): extract gen/ts/dpop.ts to packages/dpop (@notme/dpop) + expose clockTolerance
description: Two consumers (cloister src/vendor/notme-dpop.ts, canonical-hours agent/lib/vendor/notme-dpop.ts) each vendor a byte-copy of gen/ts/dpop.ts because it is not a package. notme is already a pnpm workspace (packages/*) and ships @notme/contract, so the pattern exists. gen/README.md itself carves dpop.ts out as hand-written rather than generated, so it is misfiled in gen/ anyway. Extract to packages/dpop as @notme/dpop so consumers take a versioned dependency instead of re-deriving. Requires a registry publish decision (npm public vs GitHub Packages) since npm/pnpm cannot consume a git subdirectory. Second, same trip: VerifyDPoPOptions does not expose clockTolerance and verifyDPoPToken calls validateClaims without one, pinning verifiers to zero skew tolerance. notme mints nbf: iat on every access token (worker/src/auth/token.ts), so any negative issuer/verifier clock skew denies a legitimate token. cloister hit this on re-vendor (cloister-ed4460): it previously allowed 60s and silently lost it. Expose clockTolerance and forward it to validateClaims.
acceptance_criteria: Resolved when the linked PR merges — rosary's default close signal (the GitHub merge webhook advances the bead). Set a specific acceptance_criteria to override.

title: sec: threat-model test file + contract assertions for consumer↔server boundary
description:
From the 2026-05-16 /security-review pass on the notme consumer/server.

Encodes as executable tests:

1. Regression guard for the AuthService module-scope credentials Finding 1 (already fixed in commit 33ebe24, this is the test that catches future regressions). Includes an in-test sanity mirror that pins the buggy module-scope pattern so the regression block's failure mode is visible.

2. 11 cross-repo contract assumptions surfaced by the review:
   - OIDC audience identity (verifyOIDC rejects mismatch / aud-array mismatch / accepts match)
   - TRUSTED_ISSUERS allowlist (rejects untrusted iss; accepts GHA and auth.notme.bot past the trust check)
   - CABundle CBOR canonical round-trip + mutation rejection (epoch/seqno/keyId tamper detection)
   - Revocation seqno monotonicity (advance / no-op / rollback / non-positive)
   - DPoP proof shape (typ=dpop+jwt, alg=ES256, jwk=EC P-256, jti required)
   - Scope vocabulary single-source (CertScope enum pins bridgeCert / authorityManage / certMint)
   - AuthService RPC method surface (pin the public method names for consumer compatibility)
   - Redirect-URI normalization (rejects file:/javascript:/data:, rejects wildcard subdomain abuse)

3. Cross-repo invariants needing server-produced fixtures, marked it.todo with the fixture filename:
   - TRUSTED_ISSUERS snapshot (fixtures/trusted-issuers.snapshot.json)
   - CBOR canonical byte-equality vs signet's Go encoder (fixtures/cabundle.canonical.bin)
   - /cert/gha and /join error-shape distinction (fixtures/server-errors.json)
   - AAD binding (moved to cloister/vault threat-model.test.ts)
   - GHA OIDC fork-PR boundary (needs handleCertGHA event_name filter + Action guard)

Linked from worker/THREAT_MODEL.md (new rows in §7 AuthService RPC) and SECURITY.md (new "Threat Model & Tests" section).

Follow-ups not in this scope:
- Wire @notme/contract as workspace dep in worker/package.json so the test imports from the SSoT instead of gen/ts/identity. (Package exists from 33ebe24 but isn't a worker dep.)
- Land server-produced fixtures and promote the it.todo entries.
- Add server-side event_name filter for GHA OIDC fork-PR boundary (separate bead).

title: P1: make /token DPoP JTI replay consumption atomic across edge nodes
description: The token endpoint and handleToken currently split `checkJtiReplay`/KV get from `storeJti`/KV put. Comments claim storing before mint prevents TOCTOU, but concurrent edge requests can both observe absence before either eventually-consistent write lands. Replace the split contract with one atomic check-and-record operation backed by an appropriate strongly consistent primitive (for example a Durable Object), wire the production route and test seam to it, and retain validate-before-consume ordering.

comments.text: Implementing the reviewed minimal fix test-first: atomically consume proof JTI in the singleton SigningAuthority Durable Object SQLite transaction boundary, then mint. Updating the pure handler seam to a single consumeJti callback. Planned regression coverage: two concurrent same-JTI calls yield exactly one token, invalid proofs never consume, distinct JTIs succeed.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text: Implementation complete and independently re-reviewed with no P0/P1 findings. Focused real-DO tests prove exactly one concurrent same-JTI mint succeeds, later replay rejects, distinct JTI succeeds, and mint failure leaves JTI consumed. Full worker gate previously passed; rerunning final gate after the last clock-default adjustment.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 008: /cert/gha accepts public keys + PoP proofs, returns cert pair
description:
Implement the 008 wire protocol. Currently /cert/gha returns a DPoP token. Change to: accept two public keys (P-256 + Ed25519) + nonce signatures proving possession, return two signed bridge certs. No private key in response.

The binding_payload = SHA-256(mtls_spki || signing_spki || SHA-256(oidc_jwt)). Each proof is a signature over this payload.

See docs/design/008-bridge-cert-csr-wimse.md for the full spec.

title: 008: WIMSE identity URI in bridge cert SAN
description: Add SubjectAltName URI extension to bridge certs: wimse://notme.bot/{context}/{identity}. The same URI appears in the cert SAN, git signatures, and APAS attestations.\n\nRequires updating cert-authority.ts X.509 generation to include SAN URI extension.

title: 008: Cert pair binding extension (SHA-256 of both SPKIs)
description: Embed SHA-256(P-256_SPKI || Ed25519_SPKI) as custom X.509 extension (OID .1.6) in both certs. Prevents mixing certs from different exchanges. Math friend review item #2.

title: 008: Fix CA pathlen=0 → pathlen=1 for 3-level hierarchy
description: CA cert has BasicConstraints pathlen=0 which blocks orchestrator→agent delegation. Need pathlen>=1 for CA→orchestrator→agent chain. Math friend review item #1.\n\nFile: signing-authority.ts getCACertificatePem() line ~286.

title: 008: Scope encoding as ASN.1 SEQUENCE OF UTF8String
description: Replace comma-delimited UTF-8 string scope encoding with proper ASN.1 SEQUENCE OF UTF8String in X.509 extensions. Enables unambiguous parsing by third-party verifiers. Math friend review item #9.

comments.text: ASN.1 SEQUENCE OF UTF8String encoding implemented in mintBridgeCertPair (cert-authority.ts derScopeSequence). The legacy mintGHABridgeCert path doesn't use it but that path will be deprecated once all callers use the cert pair exchange.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: 008: Action generates dual keypair, sends PoP, outputs certs not tokens
description: Rewrite action/src/index.ts: generate P-256 + Ed25519 keypairs (extractable:false), compute binding_payload, sign with both keys, POST to /cert/gha, output notme_cert + notme_signing_cert + notme_identity. Remove all token/DPoP output.\n\nThis is the final step that kills bridge_key AND DPoP tokens from the action.

title: 008: /cert endpoint accepts PoP for session/bootstrap/OIDC proofs
description: Update cert-exchange.ts: accept public_keys + proofs in request body alongside proof.type. Return cert pair instead of token. DPoP fallback for browser-only callers via /token endpoint.\n\nMirrors the /cert/gha changes but for the generalized proof types.

title: 008: Scope attenuation verification + adversarial tests
description: Implement scope subset verification: cert[n].scopes ⊆ cert[n-1].scopes. Add verification pseudocode as a function. Add adversarial tests: agent cert cannot grant wider scopes than orchestrator cert.\n\nMath friend review item #7: this is cooperative enforcement (verifier MUST check), not intrinsic.

title: 009: AuthService.proxy() — mTLS proxy via service binding
description: Add proxy() method to AuthService WorkerEntrypoint. Agent Worker calls env.NOTME.proxy({url, method, headers, body}). notme Worker establishes mTLS with P-256 bridge cert, checks destination allowlist + scope, returns response.\n\nCloud metadata endpoints hard-denied. Audit log for every proxied request.

title: 009: AuthService.sign() — Ed25519 signing via service binding
description: Add sign(payload, format) method to AuthService. Supports 'raw', 'dsse', 'git-commit' formats. Signs with Ed25519 bridge cert key. Returns signature + cert PEM + WIMSE identity.\n\nScope check: requires sign:git or sign:attestation in cert scopes.

title: 009: Agent Worker config — no globalOutbound, service binding only
description: Create example agent Worker config.capnp that demonstrates the co-located model: agent Worker has no globalOutbound (fetch disabled), communicates exclusively via NOTME service binding. Include a minimal agent.js example.\n\nThis proves the V8 isolate enforcement model works in practice.

title: 009: Destination allowlist + cloud metadata hard-deny

title: 008: Evolve /token to WIMSE WIT+WPT (browser fallback)
description: Evolve the existing DPoP /token endpoint to produce WIMSE-conformant WITs (media type wimse-id+jwt, cnf.jwk, correct claims). WPT validation replaces DPoP proof validation — same ES256 pattern, standardized format.\n\nThis is the browser-only path. CLI/agents use bridge certs (mTLS).

comments.text: WIMSE is still an IETF draft (not finalized). The /token endpoint currently produces DPoP-bound JWTs which are structurally similar to WIMSE WITs (both use cnf for key binding). When WIMSE finalizes, alignment is a media type + header name change, not an architecture change.\n\nDowngrading from P2 to P3. The bridge cert path (mTLS) is the primary mechanism. WIMSE WIT+WPT is browser fallback only. Keeping this bead open as future alignment work.

title: Replace keyIdFromSpki with the canonical BLAKE3 kid; epoch-rotate JWKS; keep opaque-match
description:
notme currently derives its JWK `kid` via `keyIdFromSpki` (RFC-7638-ish truncated SHA-256 over SPKI DER — worker/src/key-id.ts, per worker/README.md:182). Switch to the canonical derivation defined by signet (BLAKE3 over the raw Ed25519 public key, 128-bit) so an LLO signed head's signerKid and a cloister verifier resolve against notme's JWKS.

Scope:
1. Replace keyIdFromSpki with the canonical kid. Publish it in both `/.well-known/jwks.json` (keys[].kid) AND the JWT header kid — they must move together.
2. Because kid is opaque-matched by consumers (worker.js:37085 `keys.find(k => k.kid === header.kid)`), the change is safe as a key-rotation/epoch event: bump the epoch so caches (1hr TTL) refresh. No external consumer recomputes the kid (Cloudflare Access is upstream of notme, not a downstream kid consumer — verified 2026-07-21).
3. Add a test gating notme's kid against signet's pinned vector fixture.

Note: `keyIdFromSpki` may still be needed anywhere an X.509 SubjectKeyId is expected — do not delete it blindly; scope its remaining callers first. This bead is about the PROTOCOL kid (JWKS/JWT), not the cert SubjectKeyId.

Close condition: `cd ~/remotes/art/notme && <the JS test runner, e.g. pnpm -C worker test>` green including the new vector-conformance test.

comments.text: keyIdFromSpki conformance LANDED — PR #32 merged. Canonical (import->export re-encode) + 128-bit, ADR-012 conformance vector 9408457aefd071cec127c1f985399308 pinned + verified IN WORKERD, NULL-params rejected, full suite 325 green. The 'epoch-rotate JWKS' half: new keys get 128-bit kids via the existing rotation mechanism (no new code); retiring 64-bit prevKeyId comparators is R3 (signet-3723b6). Also implements the notme half of signet-371778 (R2).
comments.author: rsry-cli

acceptance_criteria: notme's JWKS kid and JWT-header kid are the canonical BLAKE3(raw key) 128-bit value and move together; epoch bumped so caches refresh; a test asserts notme kid == signet pinned vectors; worker test suite green.

title: cross-repo: cloister state re-survey before any operadic-substrate composition design lands
description:
User flagged at end of 010-workerd-as-rope conversation (2026-04-28/29) that **cloister has changed substantially** since the conversation that generated `ley-line-3278b4` (substrate ADR) and `notme-e005a8` (mTLS-injector chunk-spec). The math-friend's load-bearing recommendation: re-survey cloister BEFORE writing any cross-repo composition design.

Filed in notme because cloister has no `.beads/` directory; tracking from the consumer side.

## What to do

Fresh seam-discovery pass on cloister. Use the `seam-discovery` skill. Answer:

1. **Current shape.** Cloister at `~/remotes/art/cloister`. What does its src/, manifest/, capnp surfaces look like as of today (commit hash + last-touched dates)? It currently has: `cloister.capnp`, `config.capnp`, `src/`, `manifest/`, melange/apko packaging.
2. **Capabilities exposed.** What does cloister currently expose to consumers? What runtime contracts? Where is the seam to ley-line / workerd / notme?
3. **Relationship to workerd.** Math-friend flagged this specifically. Is cloister still the planned host for content-addressed isolate composition, or has its scope drifted?
4. **What's parked there.** The `notme-e005a8` description says "ley-line substrate ADR (currently parked in cloister as `[destined for ley-line]` until ley-line's `.beads/dolt` schema migration completes)" — verify whether that parking is still in place or has moved.
5. **Math-friend's critique exposure.** Does cloister's current shape change which of the 5 falsifiable predictions in `ley-line-3278b4` are easy/hard to test?

## Why this blocks the substrate ADR

Bead `ley-line-3278b4` (substrate ADR) is the cross-repo design pivot. If cloister has drifted substantially since the conversation, the substrate ADR risks being written against a phantom — math-friend specifically flagged this as a load-bearing call.

## Artifacts

## Blocks

- `ley-line-3278b4` (substrate ADR) — should not land without this survey first.
- `notme-e005a8` (mTLS-injector chunk-spec) — depends on substrate ADR transitively.

## Anchors

title: POC: load tree-root hash → instantiate notme-proxy + tiny HTTP echo Worker as a unit (math-friend's smallest viable end-to-end)
description:
Math-friend's recommended smallest-viable POC for the operadic-Merkle-DAG substrate (`ley-line-3278b4`). End-to-end before any schema decisions land. If it works, the rest is fleshing out the picture; if it doesn't, the whole frame needs rethinking.

## What to build

- Existing notme-proxy (Rust, holds in-memory mTLS key — never extracted, never shared).
- 50-line HTTP echo Worker (workerd-runnable; trivial).
- Hash both as chunks per the substrate ADR's chunk schema.
- 200-line Rust loader that takes a single tree-root hash and instantiates them as a unit.
- Verify: HTTP request through the loader → echo Worker sees mTLS-attached request from notme-proxy.

## Why it matters

This is the empirical falsifier for the entire substrate frame. Either the loader-from-tree-root model produces a working composed deployment OR it doesn't. No amount of ADR refinement substitutes for this measurement.

## DO NOT START until

- `ley-line-3278b4` (substrate ADR) has answers — chunk schema, capability resolution, identity model picked.
- `notme-32c72f` (cloister re-survey) is done — POC may need to consume cloister output, depending on what re-survey reveals.

If you start before those answers, you'll bake in arbitrary choices the substrate ADR is supposed to derive.

## Hard constraint (architectural decision 2026-04-29)

notme-proxy stays where it is, NOT extracted to a generic `cloister-companion`. The mTLS key lives in notme-proxy memory and never appears in any chunk content. The chunk is a *manifest* describing how a deployment binds notme-proxy as a capability; the key crosses no chunk boundary.

## Acceptance

- HTTP echo Worker receives a request bearing the mTLS identity from notme-proxy.
- The composed unit is loadable from a single tree-root hash.
- Loader is ≤300 LOC of Rust (math-friend's 200-LOC target + 50% slack).
- Spike branch only — DO NOT merge the loader into a notme/ley-line crate without ADR sign-off.

## Anchors

- `ley-line-3278b4` (substrate ADR — blocks this)
- `notme-32c72f` (cloister re-survey — blocks this)
- `notme-e005a8` (mTLS-injector chunk spec — companion)
- `notme/docs/design/010-handoff.md` — handoff summary
- `notme/worker/_agent_log/theoretical-foundations-analyst_2026-04-28_agent_log.md` — math-friend trail

title: notme: GitHub Actions disabled at repo level — 7 open PRs have no CI signal
description:
Discovered while checking why PR #2-#8 (opened in this session) have no CI status. The workflow file at `.github/workflows/ci.yml` looks fine and lists as "active" via `gh workflow list`, but `gh api repos/agentic-research/notme/actions/permissions` returns:

```json
{"enabled":false,"sha_pinning_required":false}
```

That's an org/repo-level kill switch. Workflows can't fire while it's set. Confirms why:
- "Test Identity Exchange" workflow only ran via `workflow_dispatch` (manual trigger that sometimes ignores the disable, depending on org policy)
- CI workflow has `total_count: 0` historical runs since 7aa843d added it 4 weeks ago
- All 7 of this session's PRs (PR #2 through #8) have empty `statusCheckRollup`

**Fix:** enable Actions on the repo. UI path: Settings → Actions → General → "Allow all actions and reusable workflows" (or your preferred policy). API path:
```
gh api -X PUT repos/agentic-research/notme/actions/permissions \
  --input - <<<'{"enabled":true,"allowed_actions":"all"}'
```

Once enabled, push/PR triggers fire and CI signal returns. The 7 open PRs would re-run by closing+reopening or pushing an empty commit.

**Why it was disabled is the operator question** — possibly turned off during the security audit work (rosary-b22de6 hardening pass) or org-level cost control. Re-enabling needs explicit operator decision.

This is the operator action; an agent shouldn't flip the kill switch.

Side note: even with this disabled, I've been verifying tsc + vitest locally on every commit — but external CI would catch environmental drift (Node version, lockfile mismatch, etc.) that local doesn't.

title: Add stable error codes to dpop verifiers — cloister/canonical-hours match on fragile message substrings
description: cloister's bundle-auth.ts (post notme-dffc5c re-vendor) pattern-matches verifyDPoPToken's thrown Error messages back to internal audit reason codes via string substring matching — its own comment flags this as fragile ('COUPLING WARNING: these are the SDK's thrown message strings, which are not an API. If notme rewords one, this silently degrades... The real fix is a stable code field on the SDK's errors'). Add a DPoPVerificationError class with a stable, machine-readable VerifyErrorCode on every throw site in packages/dpop/src/index.ts, message text UNCHANGED (cloister's existing substring-matching must keep working — additive, not breaking), then update cloister's mapVerifyFailureReason to match on .code instead.

comments.text: This work completes the stable-error-code contract in the published package: verifier failures use DPoPVerificationError with typed VerifyErrorCode values, new ath/alg/JWK/audience/config paths have stable codes, WebCrypto import/verify exceptions are normalized, and tests assert class/code behavior. Will close after final verification and commit.

title: schema-bridge: handle Go-annotation declarations so task gen:zod runs on notme/schema/identity.capnp
description:
**Re-filed from rosary-8d2c78** (reconciler-busted; re-filed in target repo).

Surfaced during B.3 (schema-bridge lift in PR #21 — merged).

# Issue
`task gen:zod` against `notme/schema/identity.capnp` errors with `unmapped capnp construct annotation at node id=bea97f1023792be0`. Root cause: `identity.capnp` uses `$Go.package`/`$Go.import` for the existing `schema:go` task; capnp emits the imported `/go.capnp`'s annotation **declarations** as `Node.Annotation` nodes, which schema-bridge rejects at `packages/schema-bridge/src/inputs/capnp.rs:128-130`. Zero impact on zod output (the annotations describe Go codegen) but trips schema-bridge's fail-fast policy.

# Three fix options (recommend option 2)

**Option 1**: Allow ALL annotation declarations to be skipped (loosens the fail-fast policy across the board — fragile).

**Option 2 (RECOMMENDED)**: Allowlist the Go annotation file id `@0xd12a1c51fedd6c88` alongside the existing `ANN_JSON_*` constants in `packages/schema-bridge/src/inputs/capnp.rs`. Surgical, preserves fail-fast for everything else.

**Option 3**: Strip Go annotations from notme/schema/identity.capnp at codegen time (preprocessor). Doesn't scale to other annotation systems.

# Done when
- `task gen:zod` succeeds against notme/schema/identity.capnp
- Committed baseline `notme/gen/ts/identity.zod.ts`
- `task gen:zod:check-drift` works as designed

# Depends on
- nothing (PR #21 already landed schema-bridge in notme/packages/)

title: worker: revive revocation.test.ts (currently dormant)
description:
worker/src/revocation.test.ts is checked in but neither runs nor typechecks:
- vitest.config.ts only globs `src/__tests__/**/*.test.ts` — file is at the wrong path
- The file imports from `cloudflare:test` (vitest-pool-workers) and uses `env.REVOCATION` DO binding — neither is wired up in the project's TS config or vitest pool

Currently excluded from tsconfig.json so `tsc --noEmit` passes. Documented in the file's header.

To revive:
1. Move the file to `worker/src/__tests__/revocation.test.ts`
2. Update `vitest.config.ts` to set `pool: '@cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers'` and `poolOptions.workers.wrangler.configPath: './wrangler.toml'`

4. Drop `src/revocation.test.ts` from tsconfig `exclude`
5. Confirm the test runs under vitest and the assertions match current revocation.ts behavior

This adds DO-level coverage for the revocation flow which is currently only covered by adversarial integration tests at the worker level.

comments.text:
Investigated during the security/completeness loop. Deferring with sharper scope:

**Blockers found in the path:**
1. `wrangler.toml` has `[[vpc_services]] binding = "VPC_AUTH"` — miniflare (which vitest-pool-workers uses) does not natively support vpc_services bindings. Pointing the pool at wrangler.toml directly will likely fail at miniflare init.
2. `vitest 4.1.2` + `@cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers ^0.13.5` — the codemod package included is `vitest-v3-to-v4`, suggesting v0.13+ targets vitest 4 but the migration is recent. README is one paragraph, no inline config example.

**Recommended path (next agent):**
- Don't touch the existing `vitest.config.ts` — it owns the 137-test suite that runs in default node pool. Keep that path stable.
- Add a separate `vitest.workers.config.ts` with `pool: '@cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers'` and an inline `miniflare: { ... }` object that declares ONLY the bindings the test needs (REVOCATION DO + CA_BUNDLE_CACHE KV — no ASSETS, no VPC, no SIGNING_AUTHORITY).
- Add a `test:workers` npm script: `vitest run --config vitest.workers.config.ts`.
- Move `src/revocation.test.ts` → `src/__tests__/revocation.test.ts`.
- Add a top-level `declare module "cloudflare:test" { interface ProvidedEnv { REVOCATION: DurableObjectNamespace; CA_BUNDLE_CACHE: KVNamespace; } }` in the test file or a shared types file.
- Drop the `src/revocation.test.ts` exclude from `tsconfig.json`.
- Ensure CI runs both `npm test` and `npm run test:workers` separately.

**Why not now:** the failure mode is that `vitest run` would error out before any test runs if miniflare can't initialize. That breaks the loop's invariant of "137/137 vitest still green" on every commit. A dedicated session can verify miniflare's binding tolerance and adjust without that constraint.

title: SigningAuthority.generateBundle() drops prevKeyId — rotation grace period broken
description:
Discovered 2026-05-10 during Copilot review on PR #10 (CBOR canonical). Pre-existing bug; not caused by the CBOR work, but adjacent enough that the review caught it.

## The hole

`worker/src/signing-authority.ts:418-451` `generateBundle()`:

```ts
const bundle: Omit<CABundle, "signature"> & { signature?: string } = {
  epoch,
  seqno,
  keys: { [keyId]: pubKeyB64 },
  keyId,
  issuedAt: Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000),
};
```

The bundle is built without a `prevKeyId` field.

`rotate()` at line 455-475 explicitly stores prevKeyId for the rotation grace window:

```ts
// Store prevKeyId for the transition window
await this.ctx.storage.put("prevKeyId", oldKeyId);
```

But `generateBundle()` never reads it back. Consequence:

- After a CA key rotation, the freshly signed CABundle has only the new keyId, not the previous one.
- Tokens issued under the old key get rejected with `unknown_key` immediately after rotation, even though the rotation grace window is supposed to keep them valid until they expire.
- The grace-window code in `revocation.ts::checkRevocation` (which checks `bundle.prevKeyId` for grace acceptance) has nothing to find.

## Why CBOR work didn't break this

The CBOR canonical encoder in `bundleCanonical()` correctly handles `bundle.prevKeyId ?? ""` — emitting the empty-string CBOR value when prevKeyId is absent. Cross-runtime byte equality with signet's Go-side `bundle.PrevKeyID string` (zero-value `""`) holds. So this bug is invisible to CBOR alignment tests; it's a separate functional bug.

## Fix

`generateBundle()` should read prevKeyId from DO storage (or from the same SQL state row) and include it:

```ts
const prevKeyId = (await this.ctx.storage.get<string>("prevKeyId")) ?? "";
const bundle: Omit<CABundle, "signature"> & { signature?: string } = {
  epoch,
  seqno,
  keys: { [keyId]: pubKeyB64, ...(prevKeyId ? { /* if grace-period public key still trusted */ } : {}) },
  keyId,
  prevKeyId,
  issuedAt: Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000),
};
```

Question for the implementer: should the `keys` map also include the previous key's public bytes during the grace window? Currently `keys` is single-key (current keyId only). For grace-period token verification to work, the verifier needs the previous public key in the bundle's `keys` map too. Need to look at `revocation.ts::checkRevocation` flow to confirm.

## Acceptance

- After `rotate()`, the next `generateBundle()` includes `prevKeyId` (and possibly the previous public key in `keys`).
- A test that rotates the key, regenerates the bundle, then verifies a token signed under the OLD key passes (during grace period).
- Worker test suite stays green.

## Anchors

- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:418-451` — generateBundle (the hole)
- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:455-475` — rotate (stores prevKeyId)
- `worker/src/revocation.ts::checkRevocation` — verifier that reads bundle.prevKeyId
- Copilot review on PR #10 (now closed; finding to be re-surfaced in the replacement PR)

comments.text: FIXED — PR #33 merged. rotate() now captures the old PUBLIC key before deleting it (stores prevKeyId + prevPubKey); generateBundle() republishes both (prev key in the signed keys map + prevKeyId), so old-key tokens verify through their grace window. Verified by typecheck + full suite (329). GAP: no dedicated rotation-grace regression test — SigningAuthority lacks a real-DO (runInDurableObject) test harness; filed as a follow-up.
comments.author: rsry-cli

title: notme: HIGH — /connections missing OIDC audience check enables account-link hijacking
description:
worker/worker.ts:1269 calls `verifyProof(body.proof, caKey)` with no `expectedAudience`. Sister endpoints `/join` (line 1380) and `/auth/oidc/login` (line 1423) both pass `"notme.bot"`. The omission breaks the same confused-deputy defense that verify-proof.ts:80-82 documents.

**Attack:**
1. Attacker authenticates to notme via passkey (principal-A).
2. Attacker obtains a Google OIDC token signed for the legitimate user `victim@gmail.com`, audience=evil-app.com (acquired via any means — public service exposure, shared XSS, etc.).
3. Attacker POSTs to /connections with their notme session + the stolen token. notme verifies signature OK, issuer trusted, **no audience check**, and stores `(google, victim@gmail.com) → principal-A` in the federated_identities table.
4. When the victim later tries to sign in to notme via Google for the first time (audience=notme.bot), `findPrincipalByOIDC` resolves to **principal-A** (the attacker's), not a fresh principal. The victim is silently routed into the attacker's notme account.

**Fix:** pass `"notme.bot"` as the `expectedAudience` argument to `verifyProof` at worker.ts:1269. Behavior matches /join and /auth/oidc/login.

**Test:** add a /connections test that submits an OIDC token with audience != notme.bot and asserts 401.

Threat model: belongs in the `auth.notme.bot` table as `oidc.connections.audience-binding`.

comments.text:
Fixed in commit eb70e78 (pushed to origin/main).

worker.ts:1269 now passes "notme.bot" as the expectedAudience argument to verifyProof, matching the existing pattern at /auth/oidc/login (line 1423) and /join (line 1380).

6 new tests in worker/src/__tests__/verify-proof.test.ts cover both the direct verifyOIDC path and the verifyProof dispatcher. THREAT_MODEL.md gained a new section 1b "OIDC connection / sign-in" with three rows (audience confused-deputy, token expiry ordering, issuer spoofing).

137/137 vitest green.

title: notme-proxy: add UDS-dial path on X-Cloister-Transport: uds header (companion role for cloister)
description:
Filed 2026-05-10 from `cloister-46fc1a` (closed) which wired cloister's `udsForward` backend to call out via the companion-proxy seam.

## Context

`notme-proxy` is the Rust mTLS forward proxy at `notme/proxy/`. Today it holds bridge cert + private key in process memory and presents mTLS on outbound TLS handshakes to external upstreams. Workerd Workers `fetch()` it via plain HTTP-on-loopback because workerd can't do TLS client auth from JS.

cloister also calls notme-proxy "cloister-companion" (per ADR-0005 in cloister) — same binary, two names. ADR-0005's design has the companion as the IPC seam for any wire workerd can't do directly: outbound mTLS today, **UDS-to-sibling-bundle** is the new use case as of `cloister-46fc1a`.

## What needs to happen

cloister-router now POSTs capnp ToolCall messages to notme-proxy with two new transport-indicator headers:

```
X-Cloister-Transport: uds
X-Cloister-Socket-Path: /run/cloister-uds/<bundle>.sock
```

The proxy should detect those headers and route via `AF_UNIX` connect → socket read/write → return the response bytes — instead of the existing mTLS-outbound path. Default (no header) keeps the existing mTLS behavior.

## Reference implementation

`scripts/stub-companion.mjs` in cloister already implements this proxy shape in Node for local dev:

- Reads `X-Cloister-Transport` header
- If `uds`: connects `AF_UNIX` to `X-Cloister-Socket-Path`, writes the request body, half-closes the write side, reads response bytes, returns verbatim
- If unset / `mtls`: keeps the existing mTLS-outbound behavior

The Rust impl should mirror that shape. tokio's `UnixStream` is the equivalent of node's `net.createConnection({path})`.

## Acceptance

- `notme-proxy` recognizes `X-Cloister-Transport: uds` + `X-Cloister-Socket-Path: <path>` headers.
- For UDS requests: opens `UnixStream`, write-half-closes after the request body, reads the response bytes, returns them in the HTTP response body. Status code mirrors success (200) / failure (502).
- Mounting: cloister's `cluster.compose.yaml` already declares the shared `cloister-uds:/run/cloister-uds` volume on the existing bundles. notme-proxy (when added to the topology as a sibling bundle in Phase 2B) inherits the same mount via `transport = (uds = void)` wire declarations; `scripts/emit-compose.mjs` handles it.
- Round-trip tests against the same fixtures cloister's `scripts/stub-companion.mjs` uses (or equivalent — TypeScript fixtures live in `cloister/test/manifest/uds-forward-backend.test.ts`).
- README updated to note the new transport path.

## Cross-repo cleanup

- cloister's `cloister-4cdba5` (closed; was filed pointing at a phantom future repo) → this bead.
- cloister's `docs/adr/0005-internal-wire-leyline-net.md` references "cloister-companion" — keep that name in cloister docs to match its terminology; just note in notme-proxy's README that the two names refer to the same binary.
- cloister's `docs/deployment/cluster-in-a-pod.md` already has the operator-facing explanation of the companion proxy role (per `cloister-46fc1a`); should still be accurate once this bead lands.

## Refs

- `cloister-46fc1a` (closed) — cloister side, wired the udsForward backend
- cloister `docs/adr/0005-internal-wire-leyline-net.md` — wire contract
- `notme/proxy/README.md` — existing mTLS forward proxy design
- cloister `scripts/stub-companion.mjs` — Node stub the Rust impl should mirror

comments.text:
Landed locally on main (3 commits, not pushed — awaiting human review):

- `52d8505` — handler + validation + tests
- `23a0f15` — apko `/run/cloister-uds/` mount point
- `45b7f42` — README docs (transport modes, sequence diagram, two-plane update)

## Implementation notes

**Framework**: notme-proxy is hyper 1 directly (no axum/tower) — service_fn + http1::Builder::serve_connection. Wired the header-based dispatch into a new `dispatch()` fn that branches on `X-Cloister-Transport` before either `handle_proxy` (existing mTLS, default) or `handle_uds` (new). Unknown transport values are rejected with 400 rather than silently falling through to mTLS, so a typo can't send a request across the wrong wire.

**Path validation** (security-critical): rejects on prefix mismatch (default `/run/cloister-uds/`, configurable via `NOTME_UDS_PREFIX`), any `..` path component, control characters (NUL/CR/LF/etc), and basenames that don't match `^[a-z0-9_-]+\.sock$`. Deliberately did NOT canonicalize via `fs::canonicalize` — that resolves symlinks and could expose paths the operator wired up intentionally as indirection. Literal prefix-match on the header value is the stronger contract.

**No tricky unicode cases**: `char::is_control()` covers the standard set; the lowercase `[a-z0-9_-]` regex on the basename closes the door on unicode lookalikes in the sock name. The header is UTF-8 (per HTTP), but the validation rules never look at the visual form — only bytes.

**Half-close**: `tokio::net::UnixStream` + `AsyncWriteExt::shutdown()` worked cleanly — sends FIN on the write half exactly as Node's `socket.end()` does. Mirrors the contract `proxyToUds` in cloister's `scripts/stub-companion.mjs`.

**Tests**: 11 → 26. New coverage:
- 9 unit tests for `validate_uds_path` and `is_valid_sock_name` (each rule has a positive + negative test)
- 1 test for `uds_prefix()` env-var handling
- 5 integration tests: spin up a real `UnixListener` + a real HTTP server on a per-test UDS, send raw HTTP/1.1 over an `AF_UNIX` client, assert proxy returns upstream bytes verbatim / 400 on malformed path / 502 on connect failure

**Cross-repo note**: the stub-companion's contract was a clean mirror — no divergence. One small thing: the stub doesn't time out on a stuck peer, but the Rust impl does (30s). That's an improvement, not a contract divergence. The header names + dispatch shape match exactly.

`task proxy:check` green (cargo build + 26 tests + clippy -D warnings). `task worker:check` still 23/338 (unchanged).

Bead remains open per instructions. Pre-push review-ready.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
Landed and pushed (`52d8505`, `23a0f15`, `45b7f42` on origin/main).

**Validation gate**:
- `task proxy:check`: 26 tests pass (was 11; +15 across unit + integration), clippy `-D warnings` clean
- `task worker:check`: 23 files / 338 tests, no regression

**Notable design choices the agent made that are worth knowing**:

1. **No `fs::canonicalize`** — symlink resolution would mask operator-intended indirection. Instead: literal prefix-match on header bytes + `Path::Component::ParentDir` check anywhere in the path. Catches `/run/cloister-uds/../etc/passwd` even though it starts with the right prefix.
2. **Fail-closed on unknown `X-Cloister-Transport` values** — 400, not silent fallthrough to mTLS. A typo'd header doesn't accidentally route to the existing path.
3. **30s read timeout on upstream** — additive safety over the cloister `stub-companion.mjs` reference: if a peer accepts but never writes back, the proxy returns 502 instead of hanging the request. The stub just hangs.

**Cloister-side unblock**: `cloister-b23309` (the Tier 2 integration bead for end-to-end `bead_create` through the cluster topology) had `depends_on: notme-58cc3e` and now becomes actionable. The companion is the real Rust proxy with UDS dial; cluster.compose.yaml's topology can grow companion as a fifth sibling bundle and the integration matrix can exercise the full chain.

**Closing** — the work matches the bead's scope + acceptance, tests pass, docs landed.

title: notme: P2 — /authorize/token missing audience allowlist (consistency)
description:
worker/worker.ts:1620 (/authorize/token) accepts any audience string from the request body and mints a Bearer token with it. /token at line 1666 enforces an `ALLOWED_AUDIENCES` set (rosary.bot, mcp.rosary.bot, auth.notme.bot, notme.bot, mache.rosary.bot); /authorize/token only checks `!audience`.

Practical impact is limited:
1. Session cookie is SameSite=Strict so cross-site POST is blocked
2. The /authorize redirect endpoint enforces ALLOWED_REDIRECT_HOSTS so the issued token can't be exfiltrated to attacker domains via that flow
3. Resource servers MUST validate audience anyway

But the inconsistency is real defense-in-depth gap: a session-authenticated user can request tokens for arbitrary audiences. Fix is to extract the allowlist constant and check it in both places.

Fix:
- Hoist ALLOWED_AUDIENCES out of the /token handler into a module-scope constant
- Apply the same `if (!ALLOWED_AUDIENCES.has(audience))` guard in /authorize/token
- Add a route test that confirms /authorize/token rejects audience="evil.example"

title: EPIC: DO alarm-loop hardening — post 'Cautionary tale' incident review (2026-05-10)
description:
Triggered by reddit cautionary tale (r/CloudFlare, 24 days before 2026-05-10) — operator hit 20 trillion DO reads on a single invoice from a runaway alarm loop. Their pattern:
- `onStart()` called `setAlarm()` without first checking `getAlarm()` → alarms compounded across instances
- 60+ preview deployments each spinning up their own DO populations multiplied the blast radius
- No spending cap on DOs in CF; the issue surfaced at invoice time

## notme audit verdict (2026-05-10)

**Not vulnerable to the redditor's specific bug.** `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:755-758` `scheduleNextRefresh()` correctly checks `getAlarm()` before `setAlarm()` — the exact fix the redditor wished he'd had. Single DO instance per deploy (`idFromName("default")`). No `onStart()` override. `RevocationAuthority` has no alarm at all.

**But 4 hardening opportunities stand:**

1. **(P1) `notme-XXX` — `alarm()` re-arm doesn't check `getAlarm()` first** (`signing-authority.ts:774`). CF's contract says one fire = one execution, so this is the canonical pattern, but defense-in-depth costs nothing and protects against runtime quirks.
2. **(P1) `notme-XXX` — No circuit breaker on consecutive failures.** The `try/catch` swallows errors and re-arms anyway. If `generateBundle()` fails repeatedly, we fire forever, each time doing real DO work. Need: track consecutive failures in storage; stop re-arming after N (loud log + escalation).
3. **(P2) `notme-XXX` — No fire counter / DO-internal observability.** Worker-level metrics (per `[observability] enabled = true`) don't show DO alarm fire counts.
4. **(P3) `notme-XXX` — Preview deployment exposure.** Currently `workers_dev = false` so no preview-deploys exist. But IF preview infrastructure ever lands, each preview = its own SigningAuthority DO = its own 4-min loop. 60+ previews = 900 fires/hour. The redditor's exact escalation mode.

## Constellation scope

**Audited: only notme has this exposure.** cloister has DOs (BlobStore, BeadStore) but no `setAlarm`/`getAlarm`/`alarm()` anywhere. cloister/vault has no DOs at all (the lifted vault code from notme didn't include the alarm-bearing SigningAuthority). No cross-repo beads needed.

## Implementation sequence

After PR #11 (CBOR canonical) merges → checkout main → pull → branch → fix the P1 + P2 beads in one mass-fix PR. Tier-1 patch sketched in conversation 2026-05-10 (defense-in-depth getAlarm + alarm_health table tracking failure_count + circuit breaker at 5 consecutive failures + reset RPC + getAlarmHealth RPC).

## Anchors

- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:31-79` — DO class definition (no onStart override; constructor inherits from base)
- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:197` — `scheduleNextRefresh()` call in getOrCreateSigningKey (✓ idempotent via getAlarm check)
- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:754-759` — `scheduleNextRefresh()` (the protective pattern)
- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:761-775` — `alarm()` (the unconditional re-arm at line 774)
- `worker/wrangler.toml.example` — `[observability] enabled = true`, `workers_dev = false`
- Reddit: r/CloudFlare cautionary tale (referenced in conversation 2026-05-10)
- Adjacent: `notme-942d63` (action dist drift, similar "discovered via review" pattern)
- Adjacent: `notme-ae81c9` (passkey_challenges grows unbounded — another DO unbounded-growth concern; same defense-in-depth class)

Defense-in-depth against runtime quirks (manual `.alarm()` calls in tests, future CF runtime changes, parallel-execution edge cases in workerd-local mode).

title: DO hardening 2/4: circuit breaker — stop re-arming after N consecutive alarm() failures

comments.text:
**Done in PR #12 (commit `cf4901f` on main).**

Circuit breaker shipped. After `MAX_CONSECUTIVE_ALARM_FAILURES = 5` consecutive `generateBundle()` failures, `alarm()` stops re-arming. Manual recovery via the new `resetAlarmHealth()` RPC. State persisted in the new `alarm_health` SQLite table.

Off-by-one detection: post-update re-read of `failure_count` catches the case where THIS fire's failure pushed us to/past the threshold (without the re-read, breaker would only trip on the NEXT fire — leaking one extra cycle past policy).

Atomic counter writes: all `failure_count` / `total_fires` updates use SQL `field = field + 1` instead of TS-computed read-modify-write, so concurrent fires can't lose increments.

title: DO hardening 3/4: alarm fire counter + getAlarmHealth RPC for observability
description:
Part of the DO alarm-loop hardening thread (parent epic filed alongside).

## Why

CF Workers have `[observability] enabled = true` which gives Worker-level metrics (request count, CPU time, errors). But it does NOT show DO-internal alarm fire counts. If a runaway alarm loop ever started — say, from a future CF runtime quirk or an unforeseen interaction with new code — the only signal would be the CF invoice (the redditor's situation, which cost 20 trillion DO reads).

Goal: a cheap, queryable counter of alarm fires + last-fire timestamp + last-outcome that operators can poll without scraping logs.

## Fix

Builds on the `alarm_health` table introduced by the circuit-breaker bead (sibling). Add columns:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS alarm_health (
  id              TEXT PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT 'authority',
  failure_count   INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  total_fires     INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  last_fire_at    INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- Unix ms
  last_outcome    TEXT    NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  first_fire_at   INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0   -- when this DO started firing
);
```

Increment `total_fires` at top of `alarm()`. Set `first_fire_at` if 0.

Add a DO RPC method:

```ts
async getAlarmHealth(): Promise<{
  totalFires: number;
  failureCount: number;
  lastFireAt: number;
  lastOutcome: string;
  firstFireAt: number;
  firesPerHour: number;  // total_fires / ((now - first_fire_at) / 3_600_000)
  expectedFiresPerHour: 15;  // 60min / 4min refresh
  driftRatio: number;        // firesPerHour / expectedFiresPerHour
}> { … }
```

Optionally expose via a Worker route (auth-gated, e.g. requires `authorityManage` scope):

```
GET /admin/do-health → { signingAuthority: getAlarmHealth() }
```

The `driftRatio` is the runaway detector. Expected = 15 fires/hour. If we see 30+/hour, something is double-firing.

## Acceptance

- `alarm_health.total_fires` increments on every fire.
- `getAlarmHealth()` returns the current state including `driftRatio`.
- A test that fires alarm() 10 times in succession asserts `total_fires == 10` and `driftRatio` is bounded.
- Optional: route `/admin/do-health` returns the health JSON, gated by `authorityManage`.

## Out of scope

- External-system alerting (PagerDuty, Slack webhook, CF email-on-spike) — operator concern, separate bead if needed.
- Cross-DO health aggregation (just SigningAuthority for now; RevocationAuthority has no alarm).

## Anchors

- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:761-775` — alarm() (where the counter lives)
- Sibling bead: circuit breaker (introduces alarm_health table; this bead extends the schema)
- Parent epic: filed alongside this
- `worker/wrangler.toml.example` — `[observability] enabled = true` (CF-level metrics; this bead complements at DO level)

comments.text:
**Done in PR #12 (commit `cf4901f` on main).**

`alarm_health` table tracks `failure_count`, `total_fires`, `last_fire_at`, `last_outcome`, `first_fire_at`. New `getAlarmHealth()` RPC returns the row plus derived `firesPerHour`, `expectedFiresPerHour`, `driftRatio`, `warmupComplete`, `circuitBreakerOpen`.

driftRatio is the runaway smoking gun — but only after warmup. Round-2 Copilot review caught that startup noise made `driftRatio > 1` ambiguous; fixed by adding `warmupComplete: boolean` (true when `total_fires >= DRIFT_RATIO_WARMUP_FIRES = 3`, ~12 minutes of cadence-paced data). Consumers must gate runaway alerts on `warmupComplete === true` before treating elevated driftRatio as an anomaly. After warmup, sustained `driftRatio > 1.2` indicates the alarm is firing faster than the cadence prescribes.

THREAT_MODEL.md §3 row updated to specify the warmup-gated interpretation. worker/README.md `signing-authority.ts` row mentions the new RPCs.

title: DO hardening 4/4: preview-deployment policy review (operator concern; trigger-based)
description:
Part of the DO alarm-loop hardening thread (parent epic filed alongside).

**Trigger-based, not a current task.** Currently `workers_dev = false` in `worker/wrangler.toml.example` — no preview deployments exist.

## When this matters

If the project ever enables ANY of:

1. `wrangler dev --preview` per-PR previews
2. `workers_dev = true` (default-on previews)
3. Per-environment deployments (`[env.preview]` blocks)
4. CF Pages deployments that mount the SigningAuthority DO

…each preview will provision its own SigningAuthority DO with its own 4-minute alarm loop. The reddit cautionary tale escalated specifically because of this:

> "I had 60+ previews, and each preview could create its own Durable Object instance. … 20 trillion DO reads on my most recent invoice."

For a 4-minute alarm cycle that does ~10 storage ops + 1 KV put per fire, 60 previews ≈ 9000 ops/hour ≈ 6.5M ops/month. Still small but non-zero. Now imagine a future bug doubling the fire rate — and now the redditor's escalation curve.

## Policy options (when triggered)

**(A) No-preview-DO policy.** Preview deployments use a different DO class (no-op stub) or omit DO bindings entirely. SigningAuthority only runs in prod. Rationale: previews don't need to mint real bridge certs.

**(B) Bounded preview lifetime.** Use CF deploy-bot or `wrangler deployments delete` on PR close. Cap concurrent previews. Cleanup runs on a cron.

**(C) Disable alarms in preview environments.** Read `env.NOTME_DEPLOYMENT_TIER` (or similar); skip `scheduleNextRefresh()` when `tier === "preview"`. The preview DO exists but never alarm-fires. Bundle generation moves to lazy-on-demand for previews.

(A) is cleanest. (C) is most flexible. (B) is operational toil.

## Pre-flight checks (before enabling any previews)

1. Decide policy (A/B/C above).
2. Wire chosen policy in `wrangler.toml` + `signing-authority.ts`.
3. Add a CI gate: deploy preview, wait 30 minutes, query `getAlarmHealth()` (sibling bead 3/4), assert fire count matches expected.
4. Set CF spending cap if available (per cautionary-tale reddit comments — no DO-specific cap exists today; track this as upstream feature request).

## Acceptance

- BEFORE this directory enables preview deployments: this bead is read, a policy chosen, and the policy implemented in code.
- AFTER preview deployments are live: `wrangler deployments list` shows ≤ N concurrent previews; alarm fire count per preview is bounded.

## Anchors

- `worker/wrangler.toml.example` — `workers_dev = false` (current state)
- Reddit cautionary tale (referenced in epic)
- Parent epic: filed alongside this
- Sibling bead 3/4 — getAlarmHealth RPC, the verification mechanism for this policy

title: notme: P3 — /cert legacy mints audience="notme.bot" (no scheme), inconsistent with ALLOWED_AUDIENCES
description:
worker/src/cert-exchange.ts:232 mints a Bearer token with `audience: "notme.bot"` (no scheme). Outgoing token convention elsewhere uses `https://<host>` form (worker.ts ALLOWED_AUDIENCES: `https://notme.bot`, `https://rosary.bot`, etc.). Resource servers that validate `aud` against the https-form would reject these tokens.

Two audience formats live in this codebase:
1. Incoming OIDC tokens TO notme — `"notme.bot"` (bare hostname, e.g. ghaCertAudience default at worker.ts:283, /connections/login/join verifyOIDC calls)
2. Outgoing tokens FROM notme — `"https://<host>"` (ALLOWED_AUDIENCES set)

The legacy /cert path uses the incoming convention for an outgoing token — category error.

Practical impact is bounded: no internal caller, /cert legacy is not documented public API. But future callers would receive tokens that don't match resource-server expectations.

Fix:
- Change `audience: "notme.bot"` → `audience: "https://notme.bot"` in cert-exchange.ts:232
- Confirm "https://notme.bot" is already in ALLOWED_AUDIENCES (it is, post notme-5aa0a1)
- Optional: also add an allowlist check, in case the audience becomes caller-controlled

OR (cleaner first-principles): drop the legacy bearer-token path entirely and require public_keys + proofs (008 cert-pair flow) for /cert. Browsers that can't manage the keypair flow already use /authorize/token. Saves the dual-audience-convention drift.

title: @notme/contract has no publish-time supply-chain pin — fork-and-publish hijack lane
description:
red-team:trust-root

## Trust assumption
`@notme/contract` is the single source of truth for `TRUSTED_ISSUERS`, `OIDC_ALLOWED_ALGS`, `GHA_REJECTED_EVENTS`, and `ERROR_STATUS`. The consumer-side `verifyOIDC` at worker/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:58 imports `TRUSTED_ISSUERS` and builds its enforcement Set from it. The contract README states a "future move to a published @notme/contract will replace the mirror with an npm install" (packages/contract/README.md:20).

## What breaks it
1. packages/contract/package.json has no `publishConfig`, no `repository` field, no `provenance: true`. Today it's `"private": true` — fine — but the moment someone runs `pnpm publish --access public` (the README forecasts this), it ships unsigned.
2. No npm 2FA / trusted-publisher / sigstore-provenance gate referenced in .github/workflows. The two workflows that exist (gha-identity.yml, test-identity.yml, ci.yml) don't publish anything.
3. The `@notme` scope is not reserved in the repo's docs. If `@notme` is unowned on npmjs.com, an attacker can register it pre-emptively and publish their own `@notme/contract@0.1.0` with a TRUSTED_ISSUERS that includes their attacker-controlled IdP. Any third-party that follows the README's forecasted "npm install" guidance pulls in the attacker's allowlist.
4. Even without scope-squat: the package has no `files: ["dist"]` build artifact gate; it exports `.main: "./src/index.ts"` raw, meaning any consumer that compiles their own TS reads the raw source. A typosquat (`@notmebot/contract`, `notme-contract`, `@notme-bot/contract`) gets the same effect.

## Attacker capability
- npm account + ability to publish a scope.
- No filesystem write to the target repo.

## Compromise path
1. Register `@notme` on npm before the maintainer (or take over via an expired-account vector).
2. Publish `@notme/contract@0.1.0` with:
   ```
   export const TRUSTED_ISSUERS = [
     "https://auth.notme.bot",
     "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com",
     "https://auth.attacker.example",
   ] as const;
   ```
3. Wait for the first third-party SDK consumer to follow the README and run `npm install @notme/contract`. That consumer's `verifyOIDC` now trusts the attacker's IdP. Attacker mints a JWT with `aud=notme.bot` (matches expected), `iss=https://auth.attacker.example` (matches the poisoned allowlist), and sub=any. The consumer's `verifyOIDC` then calls `fetchJWKS("https://auth.attacker.example")` → attacker controls the verifying key. Signature verifies. Identity claim is accepted.

The CRITICAL secondary risk: notme.bot is the SERVER side. Today the server's `OIDC_ALLOWED_ISSUERS` defaults to empty (worker.ts:38) — so the server itself is safe from this. But a third-party consumer SDK that uses worker/src/auth/verify-proof.ts to verify tokens HANDED TO THE CLIENT will accept attacker-issued tokens.

## Detection
None at install-time. Lockfile (pnpm-lock.yaml) would pin a hash for the legitimate package once published, but the first install is TOFU.

## Recovery
1. Reserve @notme scope on npm IMMEDIATELY (before publishing or otherwise — defensive squat).
2. Reserve common typosquats (@notme-bot, @notme.bot — though npm rejects dots).
3. Add `provenance: true` to publish config when going public. Sigstore-attest the build.
4. Add `npm-audit-signatures` to CI on consumer side.

## Closing playbook
- packages/contract/package.json: add `"publishConfig": { "access": "public", "provenance": true }` BEFORE the first publish.
- Add a GH workflow that publishes via OIDC-authed trusted-publisher (npm now supports this; no NPM_TOKEN secret needed). 
- Add `repository` field pointing to agentic-research/notme so npm UI shows the canonical source.
- Document SRI/integrity pin: the contract values should ALSO be in the server's CI snapshot test (it is — threat-model.test.ts:298) and consumers should be advised to snapshot them.
- Better: define a single canonical SHA over the contract module, surface as CONTRACT_DIGEST const, and have notme.bot fail-closed if it ever sees a token from a consumer whose CONTRACT_DIGEST mismatches what notme.bot has cached as known-good.

## Confidence
High on the surface, Medium on real-world yield (depends on whether @notme has been reserved on npm — I cannot check from this environment).

## Refs
- packages/contract/package.json
- packages/contract/README.md:20 (forecasts npm publish)
- worker/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:4,58 (consumer trust)
- Prior art: nx, ua-parser-js, event-stream — all scope/typosquat campaigns

title: [red-team:replay] vault DPoP path skips audience pinning — token minted for any RS replays at vault
description:
## Vector
`vault/src/worker.ts:201-247` (`resolveIdentity`) — DPoP-bound access token verification.

## Captured message
A valid DPoP-bound access token issued by auth.notme.bot with `aud` claim referring to a DIFFERENT resource server (e.g. `rosary.bot`, `mache.rosary.bot`, anything else in the notme ecosystem), plus a fresh DPoP proof crafted by the holder (same key).

## Replay condition
The DPoP branch (line 211-226) calls:
```
verifyDPoPToken({ token, proof, method, url, jwksUrl, ... })
```
NO `audience` parameter is passed. Inside `gen/ts/dpop.ts:128-277`, `verifyDPoPToken` validates exp/iat/sub/htm/htu/cnf.jkt but contains NO `aud` enforcement — line 273 simply copies `tPayload.aud ?? ""` into the return value without checking it against an expected resource server.

The bearer path immediately below (line 229-244) DOES pass `audience: env.VAULT_AUDIENCE` to `verifyAccessToken`, and the comment at line 235-238 explicitly claims audience pinning is enforced. **That claim is true for Bearer but false for DPoP — the same token-type the comment was protecting against.**

## Resulting state
A holder of any auth.notme.bot-minted DPoP token (rosary scope, mache scope, anything) can compose a fresh DPoP proof and call vault successfully. Vault accepts the token's `sub` as the caller's identity, charges the rate bucket against that sub, and serves credentials whose `allowedSubs` glob matches. A token minted for a totally separate resource is laundered into vault credential access — the confused-deputy attack the bearer-path comment describes.

## Detection difficulty
Hard to detect offline. The signed access token is valid, the DPoP proof is fresh and well-formed, and the cnf.jkt binding holds — the attestation chain looks perfect to any auditor reading only the verified-claims output. The only evidence is the `aud` field inside the access token, which is preserved but never compared. A holistic audit reading the access token's claims AND vault's intended audience could detect, but the substrate itself does not.

## Closing playbook
1. In `vault/src/worker.ts:212`, pass `audience: env.VAULT_AUDIENCE` to `verifyDPoPToken` (mirror the bearer-path call at line 232-239).
2. In `gen/ts/dpop.ts:128`, add `audience` to `VerifyDPoPTokenOptions` and run the same aud check the bearer path uses (`gen/ts/dpop.ts:579-590`). Make it REQUIRED — refuse to verify without an expected audience, matching the bearer path's stance.
3. Add a structural test that asserts DPoP path and Bearer path produce identical aud-rejection behavior for a cross-audience token.

## Confidence
High. Verified by reading `verifyDPoPToken` and confirming the absence of any `aud` validation, and that the call site does not supply an audience option.

## Files
- vault/src/worker.ts:201-247 — call site missing audience
- gen/ts/dpop.ts:55-126, 128-277 — VerifyDPoPTokenOptions has no audience field

title: verifyOIDC: silent kid-omitted → keys[0] fallback (key-confusion on multi-key JWKS)
description:
red-team:trust-root

## Trust assumption
JWT key selection is bound by the protected `kid` header — the verifier looks up exactly the key the issuer claims to have signed with. notme.bot/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:191-195 was explicitly hardened on this ("Threat 7: with a multi-key JWKS, kid is required. No silent fallback to keys[0] (lets an attacker who controls JWKS ordering promote their key to the verification slot)").

## What breaks it
The CONSUMER side at notme/worker/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:139-142 has NOT been hardened the same way:

```
const jwk = header.kid
  ? keys.find((k) => k.kid === header.kid)
  : keys[0];        // <-- silent fallback
if (!jwk) throw new Error(`unknown key id: ${header.kid}`);
```

When the JWT header omits `kid` entirely, the consumer trusts `keys[0]` — the first key in JWKS order. This is exactly the bug-class the server-side comment calls out.

## Attacker capability
- A trusted-issuer IdP that the consumer talks to (one of TRUSTED_ISSUERS — auth.notme.bot or token.actions.githubusercontent.com).
- The ability to influence JWKS ordering OR to mint a JWT without a kid header.

GHA's JWKS is provider-controlled (cannot be influenced by an attacker without GitHub compromise). auth.notme.bot is single-key today (signing-authority.ts emits one entry in `keys`). So this isn't immediately exploitable on the canonical issuers.

The risk surfaces if/when:
1. A deployer extends OIDC_ALLOWED_ISSUERS to include an IdP whose JWKS the deployer doesn't fully control (e.g. an org's Okta tenant), AND
2. That IdP rotates keys and a stale-cache window causes JWKS[0] to be the older key, AND
3. An attacker mints a kid-less token signed by the older key.

The classic kid-confusion is also possible: attacker creates a kid-less JWT signed with one of THEIR keys, then influences the JWKS endpoint at the CDN cache layer to return their key first. Easier in deployments that use shared CDN cache keys (host:port collapsing).

## Compromise path
1. Identify a TRUSTED_ISSUER whose JWKS the attacker can influence (multi-key, cache-poisoning surface, or self-controlled tenant).
2. Mint a token with `header.kid` UNSET, payload `iss=<that issuer>`, `aud=notme.bot`, `sub=victim-principal`.
3. Position attacker key as keys[0] in JWKS response (race the cache, or — for self-tenant — declare the key first in tenant config).
4. Send token to consumer-side verify endpoint. Consumer fetches JWKS, takes keys[0], verifies, accepts identity.

## Detection
None — successful verify is indistinguishable from a legitimate verify.

## Recovery
Tighten to match the server: reject any token whose header omits `kid` UNLESS the JWKS has exactly one key. This is what notme.bot:191-195 does.

## Closing playbook
At worker/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:139-142, replace with:

```
let jwk: JWK | undefined;
if (header.kid) {
  jwk = keys.find((k) => k.kid === header.kid);
} else if (keys.length === 1) {
  jwk = keys[0];
} else {
  throw new Error("missing kid: JWKS has multiple keys");
}
if (!jwk) throw new Error(`unknown key id: ${header.kid}`);
```

Bonus: also add the force-refresh-once pattern from the server (notme.bot/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:186-190) so legitimate key rotation doesn't fail for the JWKS-cache TTL window.

Also: add a contract test that the consumer's verifyOIDC and the server's verifyOIDC have IDENTICAL kid-handling. Today the test surface is `TRUSTED_ISSUERS` byte-equivalence; it should be extended to "kid policy" behavioral equivalence.

## Confidence
High on the bug (it's a clear divergence from the server's hardened path). Medium on near-term exploitability (depends on which IdPs deployers configure).

## Refs
- worker/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:139-142 (consumer — vulnerable)
- /Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/notme.bot/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:191-195 (server — hardened)

comments.text: FIXED — PR #33 merged. verifyOIDC no longer silently falls back to keys[0]; extracted selectVerifyingKey requires an explicit kid on any multi-key JWKS (live during rotation grace), single-key fallback kept for back-compat. 4 unit tests cover it (multi-key+omitted THROWS). ADR-012 R1.

title: red-team:dos — vault rate-bucket charges only AFTER full JWT/DPoP verification + JTI replay RPC; pre-auth budget gap
description:
Location: vault/src/worker.ts:128-191 (fetch handler), vault/src/worker.ts:201-247 (resolveIdentity), vault/src/worker.ts:451-470 (consumeBudget inside DO).

Attack seam: The worker's request lifecycle is:
1. preValidateRoute() — cheap sync route shape check.
2. resolveIdentity() — full JWT verification:
   - verifyDPoPToken: parse JWT, fetch JWKS, importKey, subtle.verify, then vault.checkAndStoreJti() (a DO RPC + SQL insert).
   - OR verifyAccessToken: parse JWT, fetch JWKS, importKey, subtle.verify, audience check.
3. THEN vault.consumeBudget(sub, ...) — the token-bucket gate.
4. handleRequest.

The token-bucket protects `proxyRequest`/`putCredential` from a noisy authenticated caller (the documented intent in rate-bucket.ts:5-13). It does NOT protect the JWT-verify path. The DO RPC `checkAndStoreJti` itself is unconditional — every valid-looking DPoP request lands on the single CredentialVault DO (idFromName("default") at worker.ts:130) BEFORE the rate bucket is consulted, doing an SQL INSERT/DELETE pair every time.

Specifically `checkAndStoreJti` (vault.ts:383-393) does:
- DELETE FROM seen_jti WHERE expires_at < ? (purge on every call)
- SELECT 1 FROM seen_jti WHERE jti = ? (the actual replay check)
- INSERT INTO seen_jti (jti, expires_at) (if fresh)

That's 3 SQL ops per pre-budget request. A flood of well-formed DPoP requests with valid signatures (e.g., from a compromised but unrevoked client) all consume DO time before the budget gate runs.

Amplification: 1 attacker request with a valid (or carefully crafted) DPoP : 1 JWKS fetch (cached) + 1 importKey + 1 subtle.verify + 1 DO RPC for JTI + 3 SQL ops INSIDE the DO. Only THEN is consumeBudget called — which itself is another DO RPC.

Also, on `claims.jti = null` from verifyDPoPToken (unlikely but possible if the verifier doesn't enforce jti), this short-circuits to anonymous (line 222: `if (replayed) return null`).

Blast radius: shared-namespace — every authenticated caller queues at the single vault DO. The DO's MAX_INFLIGHT comment at rate-bucket.ts:23 says "16; workerd serializes handlers but proxy's await yields" — but checkAndStoreJti is a sync write path, so it's bounded by SQLite per-DO write throughput. A burst of distinct JTIs from N attacker tokens all hit the same DO instance.

Suggested defense:
1. Combine JTI check + budget consumption into ONE DO RPC method. Currently they are two round trips per request (`checkAndStoreJti` then `consumeBudget`). Halves the queue depth.
2. Charge a budget BEFORE the JTI store. The current order is "verify token (worker-local) → JTI replay (DO) → budget (DO)". Reorder to "verify token → budget (DO, same RPC) → JTI replay (same RPC if budget passes)". The cost: an attacker with a stolen token can probe burn rate, but they already have to expend a valid JWT/DPoP per probe so the budget bound is meaningful.
3. Document the pre-auth attack surface in vault/THREAT_MODEL.md or here. The rate-bucket math is excellent for proxy-time amplification but doesn't cover the JWT-verify amplification.

Confidence: Medium. The compromise model (attacker has valid DPoP signing keys) is narrow, but the JTI table growth and per-request SQL cost are real. The DO RPC ordering is unambiguous from worker.ts:147-165.
