title: VAULT_KEK_SOURCE is re-resolved at runtime — config-write attacker can switch keychain:// to env://CONTROLLED
description:
red-team:trust-root

## Trust assumption
The KEK source URL (env://, file://, keychain://, http(s)://) is a deployment-time decision. The deployer chose "keychain://my-service" because they wanted hardware-backed key storage, not a plaintext env var.

## What breaks it
vault/src/worker.ts:404-436 (`#resolveAndDeriveKEK`) reads `this.env.VAULT_KEK_SOURCE` at DO instantiation, builds a KekSource via `buildKekSource(spec, env)`, resolves, derives the AES-GCM KEK. The spec is NEVER cross-checked against an out-of-band manifest. There is no signed manifest of "this deployment is supposed to use keychain://X". 

The cached promise (kekPromise at worker.ts:347, 404) caches the DERIVED KEY for the DO instance lifetime — good for one DO. But:

1. **Per-DO bootstrap is per-request-on-first-call.** If an attacker with config-write capability (modifies wrangler.toml / config.capnp before deployment) changes VAULT_KEK_SOURCE from `keychain://prod` to `env://ATTACKER_VAR` AND sets ATTACKER_VAR=<known-value>, then the DO derives a KEK from a KNOWN-TO-ATTACKER secret. All wrapped DEKs in SQLite are then attacker-decryptable.

2. **No pinning between deployments.** Two DOs (or one DO across hibernation cycles) can be re-instantiated with different VAULT_KEK_SOURCE values. The SQLite-stored sealed credentials are wrapped with whatever KEK the CURRENT spec resolves. An attacker who flips spec, lets the DO seal new credentials, then flips back, has effectively forged the encryption boundary.

3. **kek-helper has no binary verification.** vault/src/kek-source.ts:189-211 calls `helper.fetch(...)` via KEK_HELPER service binding. The helper is documented as `scripts/kek-helper.mjs` (vault/README.md:50) but no signed-binary check is documented in this repo. ADR-0019 covers "sign-only helper protocol" but the implementation here doesn't verify a manifest digest before calling the helper. The helper IS the trust root for keychain:// — there is no "double-check" layer.

4. **EnvKekSource accepts any env var matching `/^[A-Z0-9_]+$/i`.** If the attacker can set VAULT_KEK_SOURCE=`env://CF_VERSION_METADATA` or similar runtime-injected vars (some of CF's binding-injected vars are stable across deploys), the KEK becomes predictable.

## Attacker capability
- Config-write (wrangler.toml or capnp config or workerd CLI flags). Not full filesystem-root.

## Compromise path
1. Compromise CI/CD or any config bind point.
2. Set `VAULT_KEK_SOURCE=env://KEK_OVERRIDE` and `KEK_OVERRIDE=AAAA...`.
3. On next DO re-instantiation (eviction is normal, ~10min idle on CF), the DO derives KEK from "AAAA...".
4. Existing sealed credentials in SQLite are NOT re-encryptable to the new KEK — they unwrap-fail (good detection lane). BUT new credentials added after this point are sealed with the attacker's KEK. Attacker reads SQLite (via any other channel) and decrypts.
5. Even WITHOUT new writes: if the deployer EVER had `VAULT_KEK_SECRET=AAAA...` historically and the KEK_OVERRIDE happens to match, the attacker can decrypt all historical sealed credentials.

The vault DO is the secondary-trust-root in this stack (it holds the API keys to upstream services). KEK compromise = blast-radius is "every credential in the vault".

## Detection
- Unwrap failures spike (legitimate caller flow returns "unwrap failed" errors).
- No alarm wired today. The vault doesn't bind KEK provenance to an attestation.

## Recovery
- Rotate all vault DEKs: requires deleting every credential and re-uploading.
- No automated DEK rotation today (worker.ts has no rotateKEK method).

## Closing playbook
1. **Pin KEK source at first call.** On first `#resolveAndDeriveKEK`, write the resolved spec + a fingerprint of the derived key to DO storage. On subsequent calls, REQUIRE spec equality with the stored value. Refuse to resolve if VAULT_KEK_SOURCE has changed without an explicit `rotateKEK()` admin call that drops the SQLite credentials table.
2. **Manifest-pin the helper.** kek-helper.mjs (or compiled equivalent) gets a SHA-256 burned into the worker bundle at build time. The helper exposes a `/health` endpoint that signs back the digest of its own binary; vault refuses to use a helper whose self-reported digest != burned-in expected.
3. **Drop EnvKekSource by default in prod.** Make it opt-in via a separate feature flag. The legacy VAULT_KEK_SECRET fallback (worker.ts:426-435) is the obvious downgrade lane.
4. **Surface kek-id in audit log.** Each putCredential emits an audit entry — add the KEK fingerprint to it. Auditor sees the fingerprint flip in real time if an attacker swaps the spec.

## Confidence
High on the architectural gap (no pinning, no manifest). Medium on direct exploitability — depends on whether the attacker has config-write but not direct SQLite read (CF DO storage is local to a colo, less exposed than e.g. an S3 bucket — but `wrangler tail` + a hot DO + arbitrary RPC gives plenty of surface).

## Refs
- vault/src/worker.ts:404-436 (#resolveAndDeriveKEK — no pinning)
- vault/src/kek-source.ts:58-92 (buildKekSource — accepts any scheme at runtime)
- vault/src/kek-source.ts:96-118 (EnvKekSource — accepts attacker-set var)
- ADR-0014 (pluggable KEK source — does the ADR's claim of "pinned at deploy" actually hold? No.)
- ADR-0019 (sign-only helper protocol — implementation gap on the consumer side)

title: [red-team:isolation] vault DO proxyRequest bypasses allowedSubs — confused-deputy if any caller binds the DO directly
description:
## Boundary that should hold
The CredentialVault DO is the security kernel — `vault/src/worker.ts:294-304` says so: "The Worker is just a routing/auth shell. It never sees decrypted credentials." The same kernel contract should imply: any RPC caller into the DO is treated as untrusted; the DO enforces `allowedSubs` itself.

## What crosses it
The DO method `CredentialVault.proxyRequest(service, incomingRequest)` (vault/src/worker.ts:525-546) decrypts headers and performs the upstream fetch using the credentials of whichever `service` the caller names — without re-checking `allowedSubs` against any caller identity. The only `allowedSubs` enforcement is in `vault/src/handler.ts:129` (Worker side, before the DO call) using the `sub` resolved at the Worker's HTTP boundary.

This is a textbook confused-deputy:
  - DO RPC arg shape: `(service: string, request: Request)`  — no `sub` parameter at all
  - DO trusts the calling Worker to have done access control
  - The DO is named `idFromName("default")` (single global instance) and exposed via the `VAULT` binding within the vault Worker process

Today, only the vault Worker binds to `VAULT`. The bypass is latent — but the moment ANY second Worker is given a `[[services]] VAULT` binding (or the vault Worker grows a second route family that calls `vault.proxyRequest` without first calling `checkAccess`), every credential in the vault is reachable by `sub`-name alone. A bundle that compromises any other Worker on the same wrangler account/group can mount this attack just by binding the DO namespace.

## Evidence
- `vault/src/worker.ts:525-546` — `proxyRequest(service, incomingRequest)` decrypts + fetches without a `sub` arg. The `allowed_subs_json` column is read out only as `cred.allowedSubs` returned by `getCredential()` (line 484), but proxyRequest reads the row directly and ignores the column.
- `vault/src/handler.ts:129-131` — `checkAccess(cred.allowedSubs, sub)` runs at the Worker boundary, then dispatches to `proxyViaVault(service, request)` (line 144) which forwards to `vault.proxyRequest`. No `sub` is propagated.
- `vault/src/worker.ts:189` — wiring: `proxyViaVault: async (service, req) => vault.proxyRequest(service, req)` — the proof that `sub` is dropped at the syscall.
- Compare to PR #3 hotfix: "DO RPC arg-validation is the trust boundary, not just instance state." That principle is violated here.

## Required preconditions for exploit
1. A second Worker (or a future route in the vault Worker) gains the `VAULT` DO binding without re-implementing the `checkAccess` gate.
2. The attacker can reach that Worker with a request that names a `service`.

## Blast radius
Every credential stored in the vault: third-party API keys, OAuth client secrets, anything `putCredential` accepted. Plaintext upstream proxy with the credential's headers — the DO returns the upstream response to the caller. `allowedSubs` becomes documentation, not enforcement.

## Closing playbook
Push the `sub` into the DO at the syscall:
  - Widen `CredentialVaultRpc.proxyRequest` signature to `(sub: string, service: string, incomingRequest: Request)`.
  - In `proxyRequest`, after reading the row, call the same `checkAccess(allowedSubs, sub)` from `vault.ts`. Return 403 from the DO on mismatch.
  - At the Worker handler, pass the resolved `sub` down: `proxyViaVault: (service, req) => vault.proxyRequest(sub, service, req)`.
  - Add a test: a stub Worker that calls `vault.proxyRequest("nobody-allowed", "openai", req)` must get a 403 regardless of any Worker-side state.

This is "defense in depth = the security kernel re-checks". Cheap (one glob match), eliminates the latent confused-deputy, and matches the principle stated in the file header.

## Lint gap / new invariant proposal
Inv-6 candidate: "Any DO RPC method that consumes a stored credential (decrypt, sign, proxy) must accept a caller-identity arg and re-check the ACL inside the DO. The Worker may pre-check, but the DO is the final gate."

## Confidence
High — this is mechanical, the call sites are clear, and the test would fail today.

title: [red-team:isolation] AuthService.authenticate accepts caller-asserted identity + scopes — RPC peer becomes any principal
description:
## Boundary that should hold
`AuthService` is exposed as a service-binding entrypoint (`worker/worker.ts:13-17` example: `binding = "AUTH"`, `entrypoint = "AuthService"`). The contract should be: the consumer presents a proof; the entrypoint verifies and binds the resulting identity/scopes/expiry to the per-RPC-session `this.heldCerts`. The consumer is NOT trusted to assert its own identity.

## What crosses it
`AuthService.authenticate(creds)` at `worker/worker.ts:119-129` accepts a caller-supplied struct containing `identity: string`, `scopes: string[]`, `expiresAt: number`, `mtlsCert: string`, `signingCert: string`, `mtlsKey: CryptoKey`, `signingKey: CryptoKey`. There is no verification at all — the body of the method is literally:

```
async authenticate(creds: {...}) {
  this.heldCerts = creds;
}
```

The subsequent `proxy()` and `sign()` methods then trust `this.heldCerts.identity` and `this.heldCerts.scopes` for audit log identity, scope gating (`scopes.includes("bridgeCert")`, `scopes.includes("sign:git")`, etc.), and TTL checks. The CryptoKey objects are non-extractable but the SUBJECT/SCOPES are not the keys — they are caller-asserted strings.

A consumer Worker bound via `[[services]] AUTH` can call:
```
await env.AUTH.authenticate({
  mtlsCert: stolenCert,         // any PEM blob — only used in audit, never verified
  signingCert: stolenCert,
  mtlsKey: anyImportedKey,      // is verified-cryptographically only when signing
  signingKey: anyImportedKey,
  identity: "wimse://notme.bot/gha/agentic-research/cloister",  // arbitrary
  scopes: ["bridgeCert", "sign:git", "sign:attestation"],       // arbitrary
  expiresAt: Date.now()/1000 + 3600,                            // arbitrary
});
await env.AUTH.proxy({url: "https://api.openai.com/...", ...});  // audited as the asserted identity
await env.AUTH.sign(payload, "git-commit");                       // signs with caller's key, but claims any identity in the audit log
```

The signing key's authenticity is partially saved by the fact that `sign()` uses `this.heldCerts.signingKey` which the caller supplied — so the *signature* is over the caller's key, not the CA's. But the audit log claims `identity` and `scope_checked` per the asserted struct (`worker/worker.ts:175-185`, `worker/worker.ts:225-233`). For a `proxy()` call, the dangerous claim is `identity` — any audit downstream consumes a caller-fabricated identity for a Worker-emitted egress fetch.

Note: this is by design ONLY if every Worker that binds `AUTH` is part of the same trust domain as `AuthService`. The wrangler.toml example doesn't restrict the binding; there's no contract test guarding it; and the PR-#19 vault lift consolidates multiple Workers into one repo, which makes cross-Worker bindings strictly more likely.

## Evidence
- `worker/worker.ts:119-129` — `authenticate` body has zero verification.
- `worker/worker.ts:135-188` — `proxy()` uses `this.heldCerts.identity` for audit + `this.heldCerts.scopes` for the scope check.
- `worker/worker.ts:194-240` — `sign()` likewise.
- `worker/worker.ts:13-17` — the documented usage pattern says the consumer calls `mintBridgeCert(subject, publicKeyPem)`. There's no documented "first prove who you are" call before `authenticate`. The closest signal is `authenticate` is described as "Called after a successful 008 PoP exchange" — but the PoP exchange happens elsewhere (presumably in the CONSUMER Worker), and the result is then handed back to `AuthService` by the consumer. That is the seam: the consumer can lie.

## Required preconditions
- Any consumer Worker with `[[services]] AUTH` binding to `notme-bot` entrypoint `AuthService`.
- No additional creds needed.

## Blast radius
- Audit log forgery (claim to be any WIMSE identity for any egress through `proxy()`).
- Scope elevation (claim `sign:git` and `sign:attestation` without ever holding them).
- Signed-data identity confusion: the signature is over the caller's signingKey, but downstream verifiers that consume the returned `certificate` and `identity` will see a fake binding if the caller also fabricated `signingCert`.

Mitigating factor: signed artifacts include the cert, which is signed by the CA. If the CA never minted a cert for the fake identity, downstream verifiers reject. So the worst case is: caller obtained a cert legitimately for identity X, then `authenticate(identity=Y, signingCert=X-cert, signingKey=X-key)` — the cert's CN says X, the audit log says Y. Identity-audit divergence is still the cross-tenant blast.

## Closing playbook
Replace `authenticate(creds)` with a verifying entrypoint. Two options:

1. **Verify in-band**: `authenticate(mtlsCertPem, signingCertPem, mtlsKeyHandle, signingKeyHandle)` — entrypoint parses both certs, verifies signatures against the AuthorityDO public key, extracts CN + ext-scopes from the certs, validates `notAfter`, and ONLY THEN assigns `this.heldCerts` with values DERIVED from the verified cert (not from caller args). Caller-supplied identity/scopes/expiresAt fields are removed from the input type.

2. **Verify out-of-band**: keep `authenticate(creds)` shape but require the caller to present a fresh authentication proof (e.g., a session/DPoP token whose `sub` becomes `heldCerts.identity`). Verify at the entrypoint boundary.

Either way, the invariant is: `heldCerts.identity` and `heldCerts.scopes` are entrypoint-DERIVED, never entrypoint-RECEIVED.

## Lint gap / new invariant proposal
Inv-7 candidate: "A WorkerEntrypoint method that mutates session-scoped identity state (`this.held*`) must NOT take `identity`, `scopes`, `expiresAt`, or any other principal-asserting field as caller input. These are derived from verifiable proofs (certs, tokens, signatures) inside the entrypoint."

## Confidence
High — code is explicit, no verification is present, the method-body is one assignment statement.

Tag: red-team:isolation

title: [red-team:silence] CI has no runtime contract tests — DO RPC, deploy-preview smoke, or post-deploy verification; PR #3 reproduces
description:
## Gap

`.github/workflows/ci.yml` covers only:
- `sha-pin-check` (good — addresses March 2026 Trivy supply-chain class)
- `check`: `task worker:install`, `worker:audit`, `worker:check`, `docs:check` — all static (tsc, prettier, audit)
- `proxy`: cargo clippy on the Rust crate

There is **no test that exercises a real Worker against a real DO**. The PR #3 incident (CryptoKey-in-DO-state crashing /me + /connections + /cert/gha with CF 1101) passed `task worker:check` cleanly because the bug only manifests at RPC serialization time in the workerd runtime — never in tsc. The hotfix added `src/auth/do-rpc-shape.test.ts` (notme.bot), which is **static AST analysis of the source file**. That catches the specific bug (CryptoKey-in-instance-field), but doesn't catch the broader class: "tests pass while prod contract no longer holds."

## Other runtime contracts that deserve the same treatment but don't have it

1. **DO storage migration on cold-start.** `signing-authority.ts:37-67` runs schema migrations on first request. If a migration throws on existing-prod-data shapes that tests don't have, every request post-deploy 500s. No CI step deploys to a preview environment with prod-shaped data.
2. **KV bundle freshness alarm.** `signing-authority.ts:649` re-arms the alarm in a catch — if `generateBundle()` throws (e.g. key import fails post-rotation), bundle goes stale, revocation verifier hits BUNDLE_MAX_AGE_MS, every cert verify silently fails. No test asserts the alarm produces a fresh bundle E2E.
3. **OIDC verify-proof against real JWKS.** Tests stub JWKS (threat-model.test.ts patterns). If `auth.notme.bot/.well-known/jwks.json` shape changes, vault rejects every DPoP token in prod. No cross-repo runtime test.
4. **CA bundle KV shape.** `worker.ts:82-86` lazy-inits `bundle:current`; revocation.ts reads it. Schema drift between writer and reader is undetected statically.
5. **Cookie session signing secret rotation.** `signing-authority.ts:381` reads from DO SQLite; if the row is missing or shape changes, every session verify fails. Not exercised in CI.

## Failure mode that would hide

PR #3 reproductions for each contract above — tests pass, prod is broken on first request. Operator only finds out via manual curl.

Distinguishability question: "code is correct" vs "code passes tsc but breaks the DO RPC contract" — currently identical from CI's perspective.

## Attack relevance

Indirect but compounding. Every load-bearing contract that lacks a runtime test is a candidate for the next PR #3-class regression. An attacker doesn't engineer this directly — but the next refactor PR might. Without CI catching the drift, the only safety net is the operator-driven smoke test (`task verify`) which is run *manually after* deploy.

## Existing signal

- `task verify` (Taskfile.yml:41) — manual post-deploy smoke. Doesn't run in CI. Doesn't hit /me, /connections, or /cert/gha (the three PR #3 endpoints).
- `do-rpc-shape.test.ts` — static AST, narrow scope (CryptoKey only).
- No preview-deploy CI job.
- No cross-repo contract test (notme/vault DPoP token consumer vs notme.bot auth.notme.bot token producer).

## Closing playbook

1. **Preview deploy + smoke job in ci.yml.** Add a CI job that deploys to a preview Worker (`wrangler deploy --env=preview`) on every PR and curls all PR #3 endpoints (`/me`, `/connections`, `/cert/gha`, `/auth/passkey/status`, `/.well-known/ca-bundle.pem`). Fails if any return 5xx. Cost: small (CF preview deploys are free per request).
2. **Cross-repo runtime contract test.** notme/vault DPoP verifier must run against a live JWKS endpoint shape (snapshot of `auth.notme.bot/.well-known/jwks.json`). Add a CI step that fetches the prod JWKS and validates the shape matches the test fixture. Drift fails CI.
3. **Workerd in-CI test.** Add `vitest run` with `@cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers` that actually instantiates SigningAuthority + a worker fetch handler and asserts a real `/cert/gha` POST returns 200. This is the layer that catches CryptoKey-RPC and schema-migration bugs.
4. **Post-deploy verify automation.** Currently `task verify` is manual. Wire it into CI as a post-deploy step that runs against prod after `wrangler deploy`, fails the deploy job if smoke fails. Even without preview deploys, a failed verify would have caught PR #3 within the same CI run.
5. **Pattern: extend do-rpc-shape.test.ts** to assert "no class field has type X" for every non-structured-cloneable type CF documents: `CryptoKey`, `ReadableStream`, `WebSocket`, `Request`/`Response` (some forms).

## Files
- `/Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/notme/.github/workflows/ci.yml` (add preview-deploy + smoke job, cross-repo JWKS contract step)
- `/Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/notme.bot/Taskfile.yml` (already has `task verify`; extend coverage)
- `/Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/notme.bot/src/auth/do-rpc-shape.test.ts` (extend forbidden-types list)
- `/Users/jamesgardner/remotes/art/notme.bot/vitest.config.mts` (add @cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers integration test config)

Tag: red-team:silence
Confidence: Medium

## How-long-could-the-next-incident-hide

**Days** — same window as PR #3 (gold standard for this class). A preview-deploy smoke + post-deploy verify gate would have caught PR #3 in <5 minutes. Without it, the failure window is bounded only by "how long until someone manually curls."

title: [red-team:isolation] vault KEK derived from env://VAULT_KEK — single global key across all stored services; no per-service or per-tenant separation
description:
## Boundary that should hold
The vault's encryption-at-rest design (vault/src/worker.ts:294-304) places the KEK as the security-kernel root: "Resolves the KEK ... Non-extractable in Web Crypto." The boundary that should hold is: compromise of one credential's wrap context should not yield other credentials' plaintext.

## What crosses it
`CredentialVault.#getKEK()` (vault/src/worker.ts:404-412) resolves ONE KEK per DO instance via `buildKekSource(spec, env).resolve()`. The DO uses this KEK for every `encrypt(cred.headers, kek)` (line 491) and every `decrypt(sealed, kek)` (line 535) — across ALL stored services and ALL `allowedSubs`.

Combined with `idFromName("default")` (vault/src/worker.ts:130), the construction is:
  - ONE vault DO per deployment
  - ONE KEK per DO
  - N services × M allowedSubs sharing that ONE KEK

Two consequences:

1. **KEK source compromise = total compromise.** A `keychain://` lookup, a `file:///` disk-service read, an `env://VAULT_KEK` plaintext binding — any of these returning the same secret to an attacker reveals every credential in vault. There is no per-service salt/nonce that limits the blast.

2. **No per-tenant or per-service KEK separation.** The handler/DO contract does not pass any per-service context into `deriveKEK(secret)` (vault/src/crypto.ts). The KEK is derived ONCE per DO lifetime. Different services (OpenAI, Anthropic, Stripe, GitHub PAT) all live behind the same key.

Today this is the INTENDED model for a personal vault. The bead is filed because:
  - The kek-source comment (vault/src/kek-source.ts:5-25) explicitly cites "self-hosted on macOS" — a single-user pattern. But the AdminSub gate (vault/src/handler.ts:50, 76, 108) means there is exactly ONE admin who can store credentials. The vault is single-admin by gate, single-KEK by construction. There is no rail for adding a second admin without giving them keys to everything.
  - The threat-model in vault/src/worker.ts doesn't state this explicitly. A consumer who reads the comment thinks "non-extractable Web Crypto" + "URL-driven KEK source" sounds like defense-in-depth, but it's the same one secret either way.

## Evidence
- `vault/src/worker.ts:404-412` — `#getKEK()` returns one Promise<CryptoKey>, cached at field level.
- `vault/src/worker.ts:489-506` — every putCredential calls `await this.#getKEK()`, never differentiates by service or allowedSubs.
- `vault/src/worker.ts:525-546` — every proxyRequest calls `await this.#getKEK()`, same.
- `vault/src/kek-source.ts:53-55` — `KekSource.resolve()` returns a single string for the entire vault instance.

## Required preconditions
- Operator stores credentials for >1 distinct service.
- Attacker obtains KEK plaintext via any KEK-source-side path (e.g., reads `env.VAULT_KEK` from a leaked wrangler.toml; reads `file:///kek.bin` from a disk-service compromise; reads `keychain://notme-vault-kek` from a compromised kek-helper process; intercepts the helper's HTTP response).

## Blast radius
Every credential in the vault: third-party API keys, OAuth secrets, anything `putCredential` accepted. The audit log emits service name + sub on every proxy, so the attacker also has the catalog of what to decrypt.

## Closing playbook
Two paths, both small:

1. **Per-service HKDF info**: change `encrypt(headers, kek, info=service)` and `decrypt(sealed, kek, info=service)` to derive a per-service subkey via HKDF. Stored row already includes service name, so it's the natural info. This bounds compromise of any single decrypt context to that service alone. The KEK still rotates as one unit, but a single AES-GCM nonce/key recovery doesn't generalize.

2. **Per-tenant KEK source**: extend `VaultStorage` so `putCredential(service, cred)` can declare a kek-source-URL alongside the cred. The DO resolves and caches per-source. Multi-admin / multi-tenant deployments can then have admin-A's secrets behind kek-source-A and admin-B's behind kek-source-B. Slower to implement; bigger refactor.

Path 1 is the minimum honest "defense in depth" upgrade. Path 2 is the only one that supports multi-admin.

## Lint gap / new invariant proposal
Inv-9 candidate: "Any DO that encrypts heterogeneous records under one KEK must bind a per-record context (service, allowedSubs, row-id) into the KDF. Single-key, multi-record decryption is forbidden unless the records form a single trust class."

## Confidence
Medium — the construction is intentional for v1, but the gap is real and worth a regression-test / doc update.

Tag: red-team:isolation

So this is NOT greenfield design: the fix is to port kek-scope.ts from cloister into notme/vault and derive per-service/per-tenant KEKs from the global root instead of using env://VAULT_KEK directly. title: Local credential proxy — secretless bridge cert holder
description:
The bridge cert private key currently gets written to $GITHUB_OUTPUT (a file on the runner). The fix is a local proxy process that holds the cert+key in memory and performs authenticated requests on behalf of agents. No key output, no files, no wire transmission of private keys.

Architecture (two enforcement planes):
- **Local plane**: Proxy generates ephemeral keypair in-process, sends only public key to server (CSR pattern), receives signed cert. Holds cert+key in memory. Agents talk to localhost:PORT. Proxy attaches mTLS on outbound, enforces scope before requests leave.
- **Edge plane**: CF WAF / signing authority DO validates cert signature, epoch, TTL, scope. Independent validation — doesn't trust local.
- **Seam**: The bridge cert itself is the shared contract between planes.

Design influences:
- Sigstore/Fulcio: ephemeral keypair, never persisted, cert from OIDC exchange
- ssh-agent: background process holds key in memory, exposes via socket
- SPIFFE/SPIRE: workload API over UDS, key delivered in-memory
- Cloud KMS: signing operation happens remotely, key never leaves origin

Sequencing:
1. PR #1 (done): shared JWT/crypto SDK primitives
2. This bead: local proxy that holds bridge cert, does mTLS
3. Action starts proxy instead of outputting bridge_key
4. Edge plane validates independently (already exists in signing authority DO)

comments.text:
Functionally complete — closing.

The "local proxy process that holds the cert+key in memory and performs authenticated requests on behalf of agents" landed across three commits:

1. **notme-214a20** (closed) — action/src/index.ts rewritten to generate ephemeral keypairs (`extractable: false`) and output only certs + identity. Lines 165-169: `notme_cert`, `notme_signing_cert`, `notme_identity`, `expires_at`. No private keys written to `$GITHUB_OUTPUT`. Comment at line 28 documents the invariant.

2. **5bfcd63 (notme-ac2941)** — `proxy/src/main.rs` Rust mTLS forward proxy. The bridge cert private key lives only in this process's memory; workerd Workers call `fetch()` against it and the proxy attaches the client cert during the upstream TLS handshake. Listens on TCP or unix:/path (owner-only 0600).

3. **07c00d3 (notme-22fc09)** — leaf certs now declare BasicConstraints + KeyUsage + EKU so strict X.509 validators (including any rustls-based receiver) accept them.

The original bead description is fulfilled. notme-d82673 (decade) covers the broader "identity-aware network gateway" vision; this one was specifically about the secretless bridge-cert-holder process.

title: CRITICAL: X.509 cert chain never verified — caPublicKeyPem unused
description:
src/auth/verify-proof.ts ~100-121: verifyX509() accepts caPublicKeyPem but never calls X509Certificate.verify(). Only checks notBefore/notAfter. Any self-signed cert with valid dates passes. Attacker who knows a valid principal UUID can craft CN=uuid cert and get a DPoP token.

Fix: call cert.verify({ publicKey: caPublicKey }) using @peculiar/x509 (already in deps).

TDD: write test that crafts a cert signed by a DIFFERENT key, verify it's rejected.

title: CRITICAL: OIDC proof path in /cert always 500s
description:
src/cert-exchange.ts ~88-112: oidc branch passes null as any to findPrincipalByFederated() and getCapabilities(). Runtime TypeError on any OIDC token submission. JTI may be partially consumed before crash.

Fix: either wire via DO or return 501 with clear error instead of crashing.

TDD: write test that submits OIDC proof type, verify clean 501 not 500.

title: HIGH: Scope escalation via client-controlled body in passkey registration
description:
worker.ts ~217-238: body.scopes in register/verify POST taken at face value. Non-admin user can include authorityManage in request body and get privileged session. Server never validates scopes match what was issued in register/options.

Fix: ignore client-supplied scopes. Derive scopes server-side based on is_admin flag from DB.

TDD: write test that submits registration with authorityManage scope as non-admin, verify scope is NOT granted.

title: HIGH: Open redirect + no client registration in /authorize
description:
worker.ts 1252-1265: localhost allowed with no port/protocol restriction. No per-client redirect_uri pre-registration. Any caller can use any *.notme.bot or *.rosary.bot subdomain. http: allowed despite comment saying https required.

Fix: strict redirect_uri validation — allowlist of registered URIs. localhost only on non-production. Enforce https for all non-localhost.

TDD: write test that tries http://evil.notme.bot, http://localhost:9999, verify rejected.

title: HIGH: No rate limiting on /token endpoint
description:
POST /token has zero throttling. JTI replay only blocks reuse of specific proof. Continuous minting from valid session unconstrained. Acknowledged in SECURITY.md.

Fix: rate limit per principal (e.g. 20 tokens/hour via KV counter).

TDD: write test that mints 21 tokens in quick succession, verify 429 on 21st.

title: MEDIUM: SSRF via unconstrained JWKS fetch from token iss claim
description:
src/auth/verify-proof.ts ~43-65: JWKS URL constructed from unverified iss claim. No issuer allowlist. CF Workers sandbox limits blast radius but real SSRF vector.

Fix: allowlist of trusted issuers (e.g. token.actions.githubusercontent.com, auth.notme.bot). Reject any iss not in list.

TDD: write test with iss pointing to attacker-controlled URL, verify rejected.

title: MEDIUM: Bootstrap code 32-bit entropy + various medium/low fixes
description:
Bundled medium/low issues from security audit:

- Bootstrap code: crypto.randomUUID().slice(0,8) = 32 bits. Use full UUID (128 bits).
- exp truthiness: if (exp && exp < now) skips exp:0. Use typeof check.
- WebAuthn challenge not session-bound: race condition on concurrent auth.
- JTI enforcement optional for GHA: if (claims.jti) silently skips. Hard-require.
- htu hardcoded: auth.notme.bot in client JS. Derive from window.location.origin.
- Weak 32-bit key ID: djb2 hash. Use SHA-256 of SPKI.
- /auth/passkey/status unauthenticated: leaks user/admin counts.
- Reset doesn't consume bootstrap code: reusable across resets.
- Error messages leak internals: userId, issuer, subject in 403 responses.

Each should have a test.

title: CRITICAL: TS-side capnp is hand-rolled; bundleCanonical() emits JSON, not capnp canonical bytes
description:
Discovered 2026-05-09 during cross-repo capnp audit (RTFM at `ley-line-open/docs/decades/T8/capnp-rtfm-findings.md`).

## The hole

`schema/identity.capnp` is the declared single source of truth for cross-language types. Its CABundle docstring claims:

> "CABundle canonical encoding must produce identical bytes across all languages. Cap'n Proto's deterministic binary format guarantees this."

This is a security-relevant claim about cross-language byte equality.

**It is not true in this codebase.** Two distinct paths exist:

1. **Go side** (`gen/go/identity.capnp.go`): real `capnpc-go` output. `using Go = import "/go.capnp"` directives in the schema. Real capnp wire format.
2. **TS side** (`gen/ts/identity.ts`): output of `schema/codegen/capnp-to-ts.ts`, a hand-rolled lightweight parser that emits TS interfaces + Zod schemas. **No capnp wire format involvement at all.** No `set_root_canonical` equivalent. No segment table. No ordinals on the wire.

Then in `worker/src/revocation.ts:159`:

```ts
export function bundleCanonical(bundle: CABundle): Uint8Array {
  const { signature: _sig, ...rest } = bundle;
  const sorted: Record<string, unknown> = {};
  for (const k of Object.keys(rest).sort()) {
    sorted[k] = rest[k as keyof typeof rest];
  }
  return new TextEncoder().encode(JSON.stringify(sorted));
}
```

The "canonical bundle" the TS edge verifies signatures against is **JSON-of-sorted-keys**, not capnp canonical bytes. Whatever signet (Go) signs over capnp canonical wire format will not match what notme TS recomputes for verification — except by coincidence (i.e., if both sides happen to produce byte-identical output for a particular bundle).

## Why this isn't blowing up today

The notme TS verifier is reading bundles that signet writes. If signet ALSO writes the JSON-shaped form (rather than capnp canonical), the round-trip works because both sides agree on a *non-capnp* canonicalization. **That means the schema is documentation, not protocol.** Need to confirm by reading signet's CABundle write path.

Either way, the schema's central claim ("capnp's deterministic binary format guarantees this") is wrong as written — they're getting determinism from JSON.stringify + key sort, not from capnp.

## Anti-pattern flagged in the RTFM

`ley-line-open/docs/decades/T8/capnp-rtfm-findings.md` §7.c quotes protobuf's official position:

> "Users who need canonical serialization, e.g. persistent storage in a canonical form, fingerprinting, etc, should define their own canonicalization specification..."

This is exactly what notme is doing — defining its own canonicalization (JSON sorted keys) — but claiming to use capnp's. The RTFM's recommendation is the opposite: use capnp's actual canonical form via `Builder::set_root_canonical` (Rust) / `Canonicalize` (Go) / equivalent in TS.

## Fix paths (security-sensitive — pick one before coding)

**(A) Make TS use real capnp wire format.**
- Replace `schema/codegen/capnp-to-ts.ts` with a real capnp→TS toolchain. Options: `capnp-ts` library, `node-capnp` (if it works on Workers runtime), or compile Go capnp library to WASM.
- Replace `bundleCanonical()` with the capnp canonical-bytes output.
- Verify against signet's Go canonical-bytes path.
- Adds a Workers-runtime constraint (whatever capnp lib is chosen must run on V8/workerd, not just Node).

**(B) Make the schema honest about JSON.**
- Drop the "Cap'n Proto's deterministic binary format guarantees this" claim from the docstring.
- Document the actual canonicalization (JSON sorted keys, signature field excluded) in the schema as a comment.
- Verify signet uses the same JSON canonicalization.
- Cheaper but architecturally backwards — it admits the wire is JSON, then keeps the .capnp file as schema-only documentation.

**(C) WASM bridge — strategic, longer.**
- Compile signet's CABundle marshaler to WASM (similar to leyline-sign's wasm32 emit per `ley-line-c764c6`).
- Both Go side and TS side call the same WASM module for canonical-bytes. Single audit surface.
- This is the cloister ADR-0007 §"Single shared crypto artifact" pattern applied to bundle canonicalization.

Options A or C are the architecturally correct paths; B is the honest local fix.

## Acceptance

- The CABundle signature verification path on the TS edge produces the same canonical bytes as whatever signet (Go) signs over.
- A cross-runtime fixture suite at `schema/fixtures/cabundle-*.bin` + `*.expected.hex` is consumed by both Go and TS test suites.
- The schema docstring matches reality.

## Related

- RTFM: `ley-line-open/docs/decades/T8/capnp-rtfm-findings.md` (§1, §7)
- ADR seed: cloister ADR-0007 amendment 2026-05-08 ("single shared crypto artifact")
- Wasm gate: `ley-line-c764c6` (option C couples here)
- Adjacent: `notme-bd2a72` (lease endpoint reshape — uses cert-authority.ts which doesn't currently touch CABundle, but if certs grow scope/peer extensions, the same canonicalization question applies)

## Anchors

- `schema/identity.capnp` (claim site)
- `worker/src/revocation.ts:159` (bundleCanonical — the actual canonicalization)
- `gen/ts/identity.ts` (hand-rolled TS shape)
- `gen/go/identity.capnp.go` (real capnp output)
- `schema/codegen/capnp-to-ts.ts` (the home-rolled parser, ~60 LOC)
- `signet/pkg/revocation/types.go` (the Go-side CABundle the TS mirrors — needs cross-check on its canonicalization path)

comments.text:
**Closing in favor of three properly-scoped beads** — see `docs/design/010-cross-language-canonical-encoding.md` (notme ADR-010) for the full decision.

This bead conflated two distinct problems:

1. **Wire format drift** (TS uses JSON-of-sorted-keys; signet protocol says canonical CBOR per RFC 8949 §4.2)
2. **Schema sync drift** (`capnp-to-ts.ts` is hand-rolled, silently degrades on unknown capnp features)

Plus a duplicate-code refactor that fell out during the audit.

**Restructured into:**

- (P0) `notme-XXX` — TS adopts canonical CBOR for CABundle signing canonicalization. Implements ADR-010.
- (P1) `notme-YYY` — schema sync drift, capnp-to-ts.ts silent degradation. Independent of wire-format.
- (P3) `notme-ZZZ` — dedupe `bundleCanonical()` (currently in two places).

Also corrected the original framing here:
- The "TS canpn is fake" framing was misdirection — capnp is correctly type-sync-only in this stack; the wire format was always supposed to be elsewhere.
- Signet's actual production architecture is **JSON for transport / storage / API, canonical CBOR for signing input only**. notme's closed loop works in isolation; the gap is interop with other signet impls.
- The scope of the fix is much smaller than originally framed (single function changes its body, plus a fixture suite).

Branch: `notme-803923-cbor-canonical`. ADR-010 + schema docstring fix landing in commit 1.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

title: README sweep across worker-esque subdirs (worker/, proxy/, action/, wasm/, schema/, gen/, scripts/, packages/, docs/)
description:
Repo currently has exactly one README.md (top-level). Several subdirectories serve distinct runtime/purpose roles and need their own.

Skip `vault/` (being moved to cloister per session 2026-05-09).

## What lives where (audit 2026-05-09)

| Folder | Has README? | What it is | Why it needs one |
|---|---|---|---|
| `worker/` | ❌ | Cloudflare Worker — TS, runs at CF edge AND locally via workerd. DOs: SigningAuthority, RevocationAuthority. cert-authority.ts, cert-exchange.ts, revocation.ts. Includes `config.capnp` and `examples/config-colocated.capnp` for local workerd. | Same code, two runtimes (CF prod + local workerd). Newcomers can't tell which entrypoint maps to which. |
| `proxy/` | ❌ | Local proxy holding bridge cert in memory (per `project_secretless_proxy.md` memory). Distinct language/runtime from `worker/`. | Two-plane model: proxy is the local plane; worker is the edge plane. Relationship needs documenting. |
| `action/` | ❌ | GitHub Action surface. Third runtime path (GHA). | Reused by other repos via `notme/action@<sha>`; consumers need to know calling convention. |
| `wasm/` | ❌ | Empty slot for `leyline-sign` wasm32 artifact (per cloister ADR-0007 + `ley-line-c764c6`). | Empty-with-purpose; needs to say "what lands here, when, from where, why empty." |
| `gen/` | ❌ | Generated TS + Go from `schema/identity.capnp`. | "Don't edit; run codegen" — currently only the file header says this, not the directory. |
| `schema/` | ❌ | `.capnp` files — declared single source of truth for cross-lang types. Has `codegen/capnp-to-ts.ts`. | Most-misunderstood folder; needs schema-evolution rules + codegen invocation + the wire-format caveat from `notme-XXXXXX` (capnp canonical-encoding bug). |
| `scripts/` | ❌ | `doc-check.ts` etc. | Conventional. |
| `packages/` | ❌ | melange/apko output (sbom .spdx.json, .tar bundles). git status currently shows `packages/out/`, `packages/sbom-aarch64.spdx.json`, `packages/notme.tar` etc. — build artifacts. | What consumes these? What's gitignored vs committed? |
| `docs/` | ❌ | ADRs (`design/005-…009`). | Index of ADRs. |

## What each README should answer

(Tier-1 sections — keep tight, skim-friendly)

1. **What this directory is.** One sentence.
2. **What runtime / language.** Disambiguates from sibling dirs.
3. **Entry points.** Which file is `main`? Which is the deploy target?
4. **How to run / build locally.** Concrete commands.
5. **What it depends on.** Service bindings, env vars, sibling dirs, external repos.
6. **Where to look next.** Pointer to relevant ADR or design doc.

## Out of scope

- `vault/` — moving to cloister.
- `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, `_agent_log/` — generated/transient.
- Top-level `README.md` — already exists; touch only to add a "Repo layout" section if missing.

## Acceptance

- 9 new READMEs landed, one per subdir in the table above.
- Top-level README has a "Repo layout" section listing the 10 subdirs (+ vault flagged as moving).
- Cross-links work: schema/README.md links to the capnp wire-format bead; wasm/README.md links to ley-line-c764c6.

title: Signing oracle protocol in identity.capnp + OpenAPI projection
description:
Add SignRequest/SignResponse/PublicKeyResponse structs to identity.capnp for the signing oracle protocol (the wire format between agent and key holder).

Once mache-845144 lands, this schema projects to OpenAPI automatically. Until then, hand-maintain a minimal OpenAPI spec for /sign and /public-key.

The Go crypto.Signer wrapper is ~10 lines on top of the generated client. The signing oracle IS the unix primitive (ssh-agent pattern) that unifies the CA and proxy models.

title: SDK: Go verifier middleware — mTLS client cert → WIMSE identity
description:
Light verifier SDK for Go services. Loads CA cert from /.well-known/ca-bundle.pem, validates client certs on incoming mTLS, extracts WIMSE identity from SAN URI, makes identity available via context. ~100 lines.

Three deployment classes:
1. Library/middleware (this bead): import a package, 5 lines to integrate
2. Local user proxy: workerd sidecar holds certs, proxies with mTLS
3. Bastion/gateway: shared reverse proxy validates certs, forwards with identity headers

This is Class 3 from 008 spec — the service needs zero notme-specific code beyond the CA cert.

title: SDK: gRPC UnaryServerInterceptor for notme cert verification
description: gRPC interceptor that extracts client cert from TLS peer, validates against notme CA, extracts WIMSE identity. Same pattern as Go HTTP middleware but for gRPC.\n\nUsage: grpc.NewServer(grpc.UnaryInterceptor(notme.VerifyInterceptor(caCert)))

title: workerd: add mTlsCertificate binding for local mTLS outbound
description:
Root cause (5 why): workerd has no binding type for client certs in open-source. CF has mtls_certificates as a platform feature. The fix: add it to workerd's capnp schema + C++ implementation.

Scope:
1. Add mTlsCertificate to Worker.Binding union in workerd.capnp
2. Parse into kj::TlsContext with keypair
3. When fetch() sees cf.tlsClientAuth, pass keypair to KJ HTTP client
4. KJ uses it in SSL_CTX_use_certificate_chain + SSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey

For dynamic certs: file-watch (reload PEM on change) or JS API (env.CERT.update(pem, key)).

This is a C++ change to cloudflare/workerd. Could be upstream PR (benefits all standalone workerd users) or fork.

Makes local workerd + CF production use identical code: fetch(url, { cf: { tlsClientAuth: env.MY_CERT } })

title: Security: review remaining hypothesized vulnerabilities
description:
From session hypothesis list — not yet verified by either audit pass:

1. WebAuthn origin validation — does verifyRegistration/verifyAuthentication check exact origin or just hostname? RP ID mismatch attacks.
2. parseCookie defensiveness — malformed cookie headers, cookie injection via header manipulation
3. DPoP audience parameter — can caller mint tokens with arbitrary audience claims? Should audience be validated against an allowlist.
4. Clock skew on cert notBefore — window between DO signing time and verifier check time. Should allow small skew (30s).
5. DO SQLite parameterized queries — audit every .exec() call for string interpolation vs parameterized binding
6. Session HMAC entropy — btoa(String.fromCharCode(...crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(32)))) — is this actually 256 bits after base64 encoding?
7. No CORS policy on /token or /cert — any origin can POST
8. return_to parameter reflection — user-controlled path reflected in login redirect
9. Session revocation — no server-side store, stolen cookie = 24h access
10. Admission control — recovery key, multi-passkey requirement, time-delayed destructive ops (from step-ca research)

Each should be TDD: write the test that proves the vulnerability exists, then fix.

7 of 10 items fixed. Remaining 3 are design decisions, not code bugs:
- Clock skew on cert notBefore: acceptable (certs are 5-min TTL, 30s skew is noise)
- Session revocation: no server-side store by design (tradeoff documented in SECURITY.md)
- Admission control / recovery key: needs ceremony design (step-ca pattern, separate bead)

Closing — remaining items tracked elsewhere or accepted as design tradeoffs.

title: action/: dist/index.js stale — src has HTTPS enforcement that committed bundle is missing
description:
Discovered 2026-05-09 during README sweep agent dispatch.

## The drift

GitHub Actions consumers run `action/dist/index.js` directly (per `runs: node20, main: dist/index.js` in `action.yml`). GitHub does NOT execute `npm install` for action consumers — only the committed bundle.

`action/src/index.ts` was modified at commit `779ef60` to enforce HTTPS on `authority_url` (rejects `http://` unless localhost). This is a defensive guard against accidentally transmitting OIDC JWTs in plaintext.

**`action/dist/index.js` was last rebuilt at commit `f171e8d`, BEFORE `779ef60`.**

Consumers pinned to any tag/SHA after `779ef60` but before a fresh `dist/` rebuild are running an action that:
- Accepts non-HTTPS `authority_url`
- Will silently transmit the OIDC bearer token in plaintext if `authority_url` is `http://...` and not localhost

The pinned-by-SHA model assumes `dist/index.js` reflects `src/index.ts`. Drift breaks that assumption. Anyone reading the code thinks the guard is shipped; consumers are not actually getting it.

## Fix

```bash
cd action
npm install
npm run build      # esbuild src/index.ts → dist/index.js
git add dist/
git commit -m "[notme-XXXXXX] build(action): rebuild dist after HTTPS-enforcement change in src"
```

Then verify by reading the bundled output for the HTTPS check string.

## Prevention

Add a CI step that:
1. Runs `npm run build` in `action/`.
2. Fails if `git diff --exit-code dist/` shows any change post-build.

This is the standard "dist must match src" CI gate that GitHub's `actions/typescript-action` template uses. Without it, the next dist drift will land the same way.

## Adjacent finding (not a bead, just noting)

`octo_sts_scope` is declared as an input but the TS action just emits `core.warning` redirecting users to a separate reusable workflow — `github_token` output is always empty in the TS code path. This IS communicated via the warning, so it's a documented punt rather than a stealth bug. If you want to remove the redirect: separate bead.

## Anchors

- `action/src/index.ts` — current source
- `action/dist/index.js` — stale bundle
- `action/action.yml` — declares the entry point
- Commits: `779ef60` (src change), `f171e8d` (last dist rebuild)
- `.github/workflows/` — needs the dist-drift CI gate

comments.text:
**FALSE POSITIVE — closing.**

Verified 2026-05-09: ran `cd action && npm install && npm run build`. Output: `git diff --stat dist/index.js` returned empty. The committed `dist/index.js` is current.

The HTTPS guard string from `src/index.ts:66` ("authority_url must be HTTPS — an HTTP URL would transmit the OIDC token in plaintext") **IS** present in `dist/index.js` (grep -c = 1).

The action sub-agent that surfaced this finding compared `git log` timestamps incorrectly — both `f171e8d` shows up as the latest commit for src AND dist. The HTTPS-enforcement commit `779ef60` was committed alongside the dist rebuild, not after.

**Lesson:** verify agent findings with the actual rebuild, not just `git log` reading. Agents can read git correctly but mis-conclude.

The CI gate this bead recommended (fail PR if `git diff --exit-code dist/` after rebuild) is still a good idea — would have caught this verification dance and prevented the false alarm next time.

title: worker/ discovery drift: signet-authority.json advertises endpoints that don't exist (/exchange-token, /api/cert/register, /healthz)
description:
Discovered 2026-05-09 during README sweep agent dispatch.

## The drift

`worker/worker.ts` `/.well-known/signet-authority.json` returns a discovery doc that advertises:

- `exchange_token_endpoint = /exchange-token` — **route does not exist** in `worker.ts`
- `registration_endpoint = /api/cert/register` — **route does not exist** in `worker.ts`
- `endpoints./healthz` (in JSON landing's `endpoints` map) — **route does not exist** in `worker.ts`

A client (resource server, partner integration, third-party auditor) following the discovery doc to integrate against notme will hit 404s. This is the kind of mismatch that makes "well-known"-style discovery worse than no discovery at all — the consumer trusts the published doc.

## Two fixes (pick one)

**(A) Remove the dead advertisements.**
- Drop `exchange_token_endpoint` and `registration_endpoint` from the discovery JSON if those flows aren't shipping.
- Drop `/healthz` from the landing endpoints map.
- Cheaper. Honest about what's actually wired.

**(B) Wire up the advertised endpoints.**
- Implement `/exchange-token` (likely a pre-008 single-cert flow that was renamed to `/cert/gha`?)
- Implement `/api/cert/register` (purpose unclear from the doc alone — read git history for intent)
- Implement `/healthz` (trivial — just return `{"ok": true}`)
- More work; only justified if these endpoints have real consumers somewhere.

Most likely answer: (A). The discovery doc looks like it was written ahead of implementation and never trimmed.

## Acceptance

- `cd worker && npx tsx ../scripts/doc-check.ts` (or whatever the validation flow is) does NOT advertise endpoints that aren't implemented.
- Discovery JSON only lists routes that actually return non-404.
- Optional CI gate: a test that fetches every endpoint in `signet-authority.json` and asserts response status != 404.

## Anchors

- `worker/worker.ts` — discovery handler + the live route table
- `worker/public/` — landing JSON if relevant
- `scripts/doc-check.ts` — currently validates `@endpoints` claims in `worker.ts` only via `pathname === "..."` regex; the discovery JSON is a separate surface that this script doesn't check (per the `scripts/README.md` audit, doc-check has false-negative risk for non-inline route checks)

comments.text:
**Fixed 2026-05-09 (Option A — remove dead advertisements).**

`worker/worker.ts` discovery handler (line ~1814) and JSON landing (line ~1875) trimmed:

- Removed `exchange_token_endpoint: /exchange-token` from `signet-authority.json` — route doesn't exist.
- Removed `registration_endpoint: /api/cert/register` from `signet-authority.json` — route doesn't exist.
- Added `cert_endpoint: /cert` to `signet-authority.json` — IS shipped, was missing from discovery.
- Removed `endpoints.exchange_token`, `endpoints.register`, `endpoints.healthz` from JSON landing.
- Added `endpoints.cert` and `endpoints.token` — both shipped, were missing.

Verification: `npx tsc --noEmit` clean. `npx vitest run` 332/332 pass.

Out-of-scope follow-up (NOT fixed here, file separately if you want it):

- `signet-authority.json.grant_types_supported` still lists `oidc_token_exchange` even though `/cert` returns 501 for OIDC proofs (per `notme-adae8f`). Either wire OIDC or drop the grant type from discovery.
- The CI gate proposed in this bead (test that fetches every advertised endpoint and asserts != 404) is still a good idea; would prevent re-drift. Separate bead if you want it.

title: worker/: PASSKEY_LIMITER binding declared in wrangler but never read in code
description:
Discovered 2026-05-09 during README sweep agent dispatch.

## The drift

`worker/wrangler.toml.example` declares a rate-limit binding:

```toml
[[unsafe.bindings]]
name = "PASSKEY_LIMITER"
type = "ratelimit"
namespace_id = "..."
simple = { limit = 5, period = 60 }
```

(5 requests/minute cap.) But `env.PASSKEY_LIMITER` is **never accessed** in `worker.ts` or any `worker/src/*.ts` file. The binding is allocated but dormant.

## What this means in practice

Passkey register + login endpoints currently have no rate limit:
- `/auth/passkey/register/options`
- `/auth/passkey/register/verify`
- `/auth/passkey/login/options`
- `/auth/passkey/login/verify`

`CERT_LIMITER` and `TOKEN_LIMITER` ARE wired (used in `/cert/gha` and `/token` paths); only `PASSKEY_LIMITER` is dead. Either:

1. Wire it — wrap the four passkey routes with a `PASSKEY_LIMITER.limit({key: ...})` check before invoking the DO. The DO call is the expensive operation; rate-limiting at the edge protects the DO from passkey-flood DoS.
2. Remove it — drop the binding from wrangler.toml.example. Document the decision in worker/README.md.

(1) is the safer answer — the binding was clearly intended to throttle passkey enumeration / DoS attempts. Removing it leaves an attack vector open that the original author thought was worth addressing.

## Acceptance

- Either: `env.PASSKEY_LIMITER.limit({key})` is called in all four passkey routes before they hit the DO.
- Or: the binding is removed from `wrangler.toml.example` AND the rationale is captured in worker/README.md (so a future reader doesn't re-add it).
- Tests: a unit test that sends >5 requests/min to a passkey route gets 429s on requests 6+.

## Anchors

- `worker/wrangler.toml.example` — declares the binding
- `worker/worker.ts` `handlePasskey` — the callsite that should consume the limiter
- `worker/src/auth/dpop-handler.ts` and `worker/src/cert-exchange.ts` — examples of how the other limiters are wired (CERT_LIMITER, TOKEN_LIMITER)

title: vault: extend tsc scope to include vault/src — surfaces 8 hidden type errors
description:
Companion to ce2dbf4 (proxy CI integration) and the dormant-test activations. Vault tests run in vitest now, but `vault/src/*.ts` is still NOT included in any tsconfig. Worker's tsconfig only globs worker code; the SDK was added in this iteration's commit.

Attempting to extend `worker/tsconfig.json` to also include `../vault/src/**/*.ts` surfaces 8 real type errors that runtime tests don't catch:

1. `vault/src/crypto.ts:76, 84` — argument typed as `CryptoKey | CryptoKeyPair` passed where only `CryptoKey` accepted; needs narrowing or correct type at the callsite.
2. `vault/src/worker.ts:27, 31, 34, 37, 56, 82` — `DurableObjectStub<undefined>` doesn't know vault's RPC methods (`getCredential`, `putCredential`, `deleteCredential`, `listServices`, `checkAndStoreJti`, `proxyRequest`). Needs a typed binding (e.g. `DurableObjectNamespace<CredentialVault>` so the stub picks up the class's RPC surface).

Fix path:
- Type the env binding in vault/src/worker.ts as `DurableObjectNamespace<CredentialVault>` (TS 5.6+ supports the parameterized form per CF docs).
- In vault/src/crypto.ts, narrow the input type at the boundary so subtle.encrypt/decrypt sees `CryptoKey`.
- Re-extend `worker/tsconfig.json` include with `../vault/src/**/*.ts`.

Once green, vault is under the same tsc + vitest CI signal as the worker. This was deferred from this loop because fixing all 8 is more than the iteration's risk budget; tests still pass at runtime.

comments.text:
Partial progress in commit a879729 — the simpler of the two error categories is fixed in isolation:

- ✅ `vault/src/crypto.ts:65` — `subtle.generateKey({name:"AES-GCM",...})` cast to CryptoKey. Eliminates 2 of the 8 errors.

The DO-binding typing turned out trickier than the bead's recommendation suggested. Tried two paths:

1. **`Env.VAULT: DurableObjectNamespace<CredentialVault>` + `class CredentialVault extends DurableObject<Env>`** — TS rejects with `TS2589: Type instantiation is excessively deep and possibly infinite`. The cycle is Env → VAULT → CredentialVault → DurableObject<Env> → Env.

2. **`extends DurableObject` (unparameterized) + `DurableObjectNamespace<CredentialVault>`** — same TS2589. The cycle is shorter but still recursive (CredentialVault references Env via `private env: Env` for its constructor and methods that read env props).

Working approach for whoever takes this next:

- Extract a separate RPC-only interface (no Env reference): `interface CredentialVaultRpc extends Rpc.DurableObjectBranded { getCredential(...): ..; putCredential(...): ..; ... }`. Use it as the type parameter on DurableObjectNamespace.
- The class itself can `extends DurableObject` (if `cloudflare:workers` import works for vault's tsc context — needs verification by adding vault to tsconfig include).
- Or alternatively, wrap each `vault.<method>(...)` call site in a typed shim that the worker.ts uses, isolating the cast to one place.

There's also a Uint8Array<ArrayBufferLike> vs ArrayBuffer mismatch at crypto.ts:137 surfaced when vault is included in tsconfig — not present in the original 8 because tsc stops counting after a certain depth. Likely a `lib` config issue or an explicit `as ArrayBuffer` cast site.

Bead remains open. Risk budget for this loop iteration was the limiter — the recursion fight is worth a clean session, not a 5-minute slice.

title: DEFERRED: Remove notme/vault/ once cloister's copy is proven (no urgency)
description:
**Scope changed 2026-05-09**: deferred indefinitely. cloister-9ad9eb is now copy-not-move — cloister gets a self-contained AGPL-3 copy of vault/, notme keeps its Apache-2.0 copy unchanged. Consolidation only happens when there's a concrete reason (e.g. notme starts using cloister's vault via service binding, or vault grows in a way that makes maintaining two copies painful).

## When to revisit this bead

Trigger conditions (any one is sufficient):

1. **notme starts depending on cloister's vault** — service binding, npm dependency, or path-shared. At that point notme's local copy becomes redundant.
2. **Active divergence between the two copies** — bug fixes or features land in one but not the other. Either consolidate or accept the fork explicitly.
3. **External user asks** — "where's the canonical vault implementation?" If the answer is "either, but they might drift," that's a signal to pick one.
4. **License-strategy change** — if notme's Apache-2.0 posture changes, or if cloister's AGPL-3 needs to fan out to notme too.

## When NOT to do this

- "It feels duplicative." Two copies with clear ownership is fine; the cost is low and the coupling cost of a service binding is non-zero.
- "OSS readiness needs notme cleaned up too." cloister's OSS readiness doesn't depend on notme being touched. The cloister-side lift gives cloister everything it needs.

## Original scope (kept for reference, not active work)

If/when this bead reactivates, the original migration plan was:

- Delete `notme/vault/` directory.
- Remove `../vault/src/**/*.ts` from `notme/worker/tsconfig.json` `include`.
- Remove `../vault/src/__tests__/**/*.test.ts` from `notme/worker/vitest.config.ts` test paths.
- Decide on `notme/worker/THREAT_MODEL.md` (22 vault references) — move with the code OR leave a redirect.
- Update notme's top-level README.md if it references the vault sibling.

## Files (when reactivated)
- `vault/` (deletion)
- `worker/tsconfig.json` (edit)
- `worker/vitest.config.ts` (edit)
- `worker/THREAT_MODEL.md` (move or split)
- `README.md` (if applicable)

title: Design: credential mint for bd Dolt sql-server MySQL auth (cloister-side substrate adoption)
description:
## Context

The rosary LLM (2026-06-23 session) flipped ADR-0012's read: `bd` ships with a socket-mode `dolt sql-server` that handles concurrent writers + is sandbox-friendly. The corrected substrate plan is for ALL bead consumers (rosary, cloister, claude code sessions) to speak MySQL to a shared bd-managed Dolt sql-server.

Cloister-side design lives in `cloister-9d19e3` (filed in cloister tracker 2026-06-23): cloister-router gets a service binding → external dolt-sql-server, with cloister mediating credentials via the existing ADR-0024 `cloister/credential-isolation/v1` path. This bead captures what notme has to ship for the credential mint.

## Question

Does the existing GHA-OIDC bridge cert path (`notme/action`, `pkg/oidc`, `LocalCA.IssueBridgeCert`) work for authenticating an agent identity to dolt-sql-server, OR does this need a new credential type?

## Likely answer (to be confirmed in design)

**MySQL doesn't natively understand bridge certs.** Two options:

### Option A — Vault stores a MySQL user/password; notme out of scope

- Operator pre-provisions a MySQL user on the dolt-sql-server
- The password lands in cloister's vault via `wrangler secret put` or kek-source
- Cloister vault DO injects `Authorization: Basic ...` or sets MySQL `Cleartext Plugin` on the connection
- **Notme work**: NONE. The credential is an operator-managed secret; notme doesn't touch it.

### Option B — TLS client cert, minted per agent identity

- Dolt-sql-server configured with `--require-secure-transport` + a CA bundle from notme
- Each agent identity gets a short-lived TLS client cert minted by notme's `LocalCA` (same path as GHA bridge certs)
- Dolt verifies the cert chain; agent identity = client cert CN/SAN
- **Notme work**: extend `LocalCA.IssueBridgeCert` to support a new `--purpose mysql-client` flag that issues with `extKeyUsage = clientAuth` and an appropriate SAN namespace. Add `/exchange-mysql-cert` endpoint analogous to `/exchange-token`.

### Option C — DPoP-style proof in a MySQL `connect_attrs` field

- Agent sends DPoP-shaped proof in the MySQL connection's `connect_attrs` map at handshake time
- Dolt-sql-server validates via a custom auth plugin (requires bd patching — likely OUT of scope)

## Scope of this bead

**Design + decision, not implementation.** Output:

1. Audit which MySQL auth shape dolt-sql-server actually supports (cleartext password, native_password, caching_sha2_password, TLS client cert via mysql_clear_password, etc.)
2. Choose A / B / C based on (a) what dolt-sql-server supports, (b) which keeps the trust-root posture clean, (c) which preserves the "credential never leaves vault" property
3. If A: file follow-up notme beads as `none` (nothing to do)
4. If B: file follow-up notme bead for `LocalCA` extension + new `/exchange-mysql-cert` endpoint
5. If C: file follow-up notme bead PLUS a bd-side bead for the custom auth plugin

## Trust-boundary considerations

- Whichever credential shape: it MUST live in cloister's vault, never in plaintext on the wire INTO the Worker
- Per ADR-0019 sign-helper precedent: prefer short-lived credentials minted on-demand over long-lived static secrets
- TLS client certs (Option B) have the cleanest "short-lived + non-bearer" properties — match the bridge-cert precedent

## Dependencies

- **Blocks on**: cloister-9d19e3 (cloister ADR-0033 for the dolt-sql-server binding) — that ADR picks the credential shape from the cloister side; this bead implements notme's side of that choice.
- **Indirectly blocks on**: rosary ADR-0012 rewrite (the substrate adoption decision)

## Tracking

- Parent: cloister-9d19e3 (cloister ADR-0033 design)
- Related notme work: existing GHA-OIDC bridge cert path (`pkg/oidc`, `LocalCA.IssueBridgeCert`, `/exchange-token`), bead `signet-882eca` (bridge cert with capability X.509 extensions — relevant precedent)
- Threat model touchpoint: notme adds a new attack-surface row for the mysql-client cert mint if Option B

comments.text:
Update from cloister ADR-0033 draft (cloister/docs/adr/0033-bd-substrate-binding.md, 2026-06-23):

**The cloister-side picked Option A** (vault-stored bearer token), not Option B (TLS client cert via LocalCA). Rationale per ADR-0033 D4:

- bd MCP server runs UDS-only inside the cluster trust boundary
- Filesystem ACL on `/run/cloister-uds/` is the perimeter (same as mache + llo)
- Bearer token mediated by vault per ADR-0024 cred-iso/v1 — same shape as mache/llo's tokens
- Mirrors LLO's ADR-0022 shared-secret precedent

**This eliminates notme's piece for the bearer-token path.** No LocalCA extension required; no new `/exchange-mysql-cert` endpoint; no bridge cert capability extension for MySQL auth.

**Status options:**

1. **Close as invalid** — bearer-token path is the picked direction; no notme work required.
2. **Keep open as Option B (future)** — if a deployment later wants TLS client cert auth (e.g. for an external multi-tenant bd-as-a-service shape), the design discussion here stays valid. Park it as P3 / future-consideration.

Recommend (2): keep open at P3 as a parked design discussion for the deferred TLS-client-cert option. If/when that direction surfaces, this bead has the prior thinking documented.

This is a design bead for minting bd Dolt sql-server MySQL creds; the implementation surface is the TS worker (worker/src/auth/ token issuance) and/or the vault Worker (vault/src/), not Go. Exact module to be settled in the design itself; file scope set to worker/src/auth/ + vault/src/ + docs/design/ as placeholders.

title: Service bindings: split notme into public + private surfaces
description:
Use CF service bindings to eliminate public attack surface for internal operations.

Public surface (reachable via internet):
- Passkey login/register
- GHA OIDC cert exchange (/cert/gha)
- CA bundle (/.well-known/*)
- DPoP token endpoint (/token)

Private surface (only via service binding, no public URL):
- cert minting (mintBridgeCert RPC)
- admin operations (epoch rotation, bootstrap reset)
- principal management
- scope grants

Consuming Workers bind to notme-bot in wrangler.toml:
```toml
[[services]]
binding = "AUTH"
service = "notme-bot"
```

Then call directly: `env.AUTH.mintBridgeCert(subject, pubkey)`

This eliminates for internal calls:
- CORS (same-process)
- Audience validation (binding IS the auth)
- Rate limiting (trust the caller)
- Token/header auth (not needed)
- Public attack surface (endpoints not reachable)

The public surface still needs all the hardening. But the internal surface becomes zero-trust-by-architecture.

title: notme-proxy: UDS listen support (unix:/path/to/sock)
description:
Parent: rosary-ac0518.

The proxy's --listen flag already advertises 'unix:/run/notme/mtls.sock' in its docstring (proxy/src/main.rs:11) but the parser only handles SocketAddr (line 184). Implement the unix:PATH variant so cloister can route through notme-proxy via UDS without a TCP hop.

Required:
- Detect unix: prefix in --listen; bind UnixListener instead of TcpListener
- Strip socket file on shutdown + on stale leftover
- Optionally surface AF_UNIX peer creds (SO_PEERCRED on linux, getpeereid on macOS) so downstream attestation can pin uid/pid/cmd
- Update README + Taskfile examples

Out of scope: changing the upstream forwarding (still HTTPS+mTLS via bridge cert).

comments.text:
Implemented UDS listen support in proxy/src/main.rs (uncommitted on main):

**Changes:**
- New `ListenAddr` enum (Tcp/Unix) + `parse_listen_addr()` helper
- `unix:` prefix routes to `tokio::net::UnixListener`; everything else falls through to `SocketAddr`/`TcpListener` (existing behavior preserved)
- Stale socket file removed before bind (`fs::remove_file` ignored if absent)
- Accept loop extracted to generic `spawn_conn<S>` over `AsyncRead+AsyncWrite+Send` so both `TcpStream` and `UnixStream` reuse the hyper http1 service path
- No new deps; UnixListener comes from `tokio` "full" feature already in Cargo.toml

**Tests added (6, all passing):**
- `parses_tcp_listen_addr` — `127.0.0.1:1080` → Tcp variant
- `parses_unix_listen_addr` — `unix:/run/notme/mtls.sock` → Unix variant
- `parses_unix_relative_path` — `unix:./mtls.sock` accepted
- `rejects_empty_unix_path` — `unix:` errors with helpful message
- `rejects_invalid_tcp_addr` — non-addr strings error
- `binds_unix_listener_and_removes_stale_socket` (tokio::test) — pre-creates a stale file, confirms removal+bind succeed, round-trips a byte through the accepted stream

`cargo build`, `cargo test` (6/6), `cargo clippy --tests` all clean.

**Not yet committed** — leaving for review before commit/close. Permissions on the socket are left to umask (matches the rest of the crate's minimal style); tighten later if workerd's group setup demands it.
comments.author: rsry-mcp

comments.text:
Committed as 5bfcd63 on main.

**Security review additions over the initial draft:**
1. Socket file chmod'd to 0600 right after bind. The bridge cert means anyone with `connect()` access can fetch as the bridge identity — owner-only is the right default for the local-trust model from the parent epic. There's a microsecond chmod-after-bind race window; mitigated by directory perms (operator concern). A future hardening could set umask before bind for a tighter guarantee, but that needs a libc dep so it's out of scope here.
2. `try_remove_socket_file()` refuses to remove non-socket paths and uses `symlink_metadata` (not `metadata`) so a symlink at the path is treated as "not a socket" rather than followed. This protects against operator typos like `unix:./bridge-cert.pem` silently deleting the cert, and against an attacker placing a symlink at the target before startup.

**Validated 2x:**
- Pass 1: `cargo build` + `cargo test` (11/11) + `cargo clippy --tests -- -D warnings` — all clean
- Pass 2: `cargo clean` then `cargo test --release` (11/11) — all clean from a fresh build

**Test coverage (11 tests):**
- Parser: tcp / unix-absolute / unix-relative / empty-unix / invalid-tcp
- Stale removal: noop-when-absent / refuses-regular-file / refuses-symlink-to-regular-file
- Bind helper: sets 0600 perms / replaces stale socket / round-trip accept

Closing.

description:
`worker/src/__tests__/cert-gha.test.ts:29`: TypeScript reports `Property 'windowStart' does not exist on type 'never'`. This is a pre-existing error (file exists on main branch with the same pattern). The issue is that the test uses a local `rl` variable typed as `null` initially; after the `if (!rl || ...)` branch TypeScript narrows `rl` to `never` in the truthy branch body, so `.windowStart` is inaccessible via the type system.

The test logic is correct but the type annotation is wrong. Fix: give `rl` an explicit type: `let rl: { count: number; windowStart: number } | null = null;` (already there on line 28) — the actual root cause is the TypeScript 6.x narrowing being stricter than 5.x. The type annotation exists but the if-condition narrows to never incorrectly. Needs: change the null check or restructure the variable initialization.

title: cert-authority.ts + signing-authority.ts + token.ts — Ed25519 as any casts are a WebCrypto TS lib gap, document it
description:
Multiple security-critical crypto call sites use `{ name: "Ed25519" } as any` or `"Ed25519" as any` to work around a missing Ed25519 entry in the TypeScript DOM lib's `SubtleCrypto` overloads:

- `worker/src/cert-authority.ts`: lines 48, 65, 115
- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts`: lines 126, 136, 155, 192, 279, 311, 423
- `worker/src/auth/token.ts`: lines 78, 113

These `as any` casts suppress type-checking on the algorithm parameter passed to `crypto.subtle.generateKey/importKey/sign/verify`. If the string is wrong (e.g., typo or wrong casing), the runtime throws but TS doesn't catch it at compile time.

The casts are structurally necessary given the current `@cloudflare/workers-types` lib — Ed25519 was added to the spec but typings lag. However they are undocumented, making them look like lazy workarounds.

Fix options:
1. Add a top-of-file comment in each affected file explaining the cast is a DOM lib gap, not a type escape hatch. (docs)
2. Define a typed `const ED25519_ALGO = { name: "Ed25519" } as AlgorithmIdentifier` constant and use it throughout — narrows the surface of `as any` to one declaration. (tidy)

The `cert-exchange.ts:142` cast `(body.proof as any).type` in the exhaustive-check branch is a TypeScript narrowing artifact — correct but worth a comment.
The `auth/principals.ts:106,176` `.toArray() as any[]` casts hide SQLite row shapes — these are medium risk as they bypass row type checking on security-relevant principal lookups.

Severity: medium for Ed25519 casts (documented gap, low bug risk), medium for principals.ts (hides shape mismatches on security queries).
Action: docs + tidy
Labels: perspective:dev, action:docs, action:tidy

comments.text: Test comment — checking MCP write path after schema migration.

comments.text:
Fixed in commit dd8794d (pushed to origin/main).

All 6 production-code `"Ed25519" as any` cast sites now import the single `ED25519` constant from worker/src/platform.ts:
- worker/worker.ts (2 sites)
- worker/src/auth/token.ts (2 sites)
- worker/src/signing-authority.ts (2 sites)
- worker/src/cert-exchange.ts (1 site)

The platform.ts comment was expanded to be the canonical reference for the workaround (CF Workers' WebCrypto fully implements Ed25519, but the TS lib types don't enumerate it). SECURITY.md gained an "Ed25519 typing workaround" section under "Known Limitations" explaining the gap, the convention, and what changes when the lib types catch up.

Tests retain their own inline casts because they construct distinct algorithm-object shapes per call (importKey wants the object form, sign accepts the string form) — that's documented in the platform.ts header so future contributors don't get confused.

title: notme: M1 — OIDC audience pin "notme.bot" incoherent for Google ID tokens
description:
Found by code-review sub-agent (aa0878f69907df240).

Multiple OIDC-accepting routes pin `expectedAudience: "notme.bot"`:
- worker/worker.ts:1307 (/connections POST)
- worker/worker.ts:1422 (/join)
- worker/worker.ts:1465 (/auth/oidc/login)
- worker/src/cert-exchange.ts:106

But Google ID tokens carry `aud = <google-client-id>.apps.googleusercontent.com`, never `notme.bot`. So either:
(a) These OIDC paths cannot accept Google tokens at all today (audience check always fails),
(b) The audience pin is wrong and should be the Google client ID from env, OR
(c) The OIDC paths are dead code that should be removed.

`TRUSTED_ISSUERS` in verify-proof.ts:43 includes `https://accounts.google.com`, suggesting (a)/(c) — Google was meant to work.

The synthetic test at gen/ts/__tests__/dpop-verifier.test.ts uses `iss: https://accounts.google.com` + `aud: notme.bot` — a configuration that can't exist in practice.

**First-principles options:**
1. Remove the Google branch from TRUSTED_ISSUERS until the audience plumbing is right
2. Add per-issuer audience expectations: `Map<issuer, expected-audience>` keyed by env config (e.g. `OIDC_CLIENT_ID_GOOGLE`)
3. Drop the OIDC paths entirely if they're not used

The structural fix landed in 5639328 (audience required at type level) is correct — this is just about the VALUE being pinned looking wrong for some issuers.

title: notme: M2 — passkey_challenges grows unbounded (no sweeper)
description:
Found by code-review sub-agent.

worker/src/auth/passkey.ts:248 — the new lookup `WHERE challenge = ? AND created_at > datetime('now', '-5 minutes')` filters expired rows at READ time but never deletes them.

The 0c5e296 fix (challenge per-flow binding) made each row keyed by its random challenge value. Successful flows DELETE their row at line 277-280. Abandoned flows (user closes browser, network failure) leave dead rows forever.

Fix options:
1. Periodic sweeper in DO alarm: `DELETE FROM passkey_challenges WHERE created_at < datetime('now', '-1 hour')`
2. Sweep on every authenticationOptions/registrationOptions call (cheap if indexed; happens before INSERT)
3. Use a TTL-aware storage (e.g. KV with expirationTtl) instead of SQLite

Option 2 is simplest and bounds growth at the rate of new auth attempts.

The test at passkey.test.ts:269-279 just asserts no-throw when the table is empty — it doesn't actually exercise the two-concurrent-flows scenario the bead was about.

The check `expectedChallenge === clientData.challenge` inside SimpleWebAuthn is now tautological because both come from the same source (clientDataJSON). The real binding moved to the SQL row-existence check, but no test pins the two-flow case.
