title: notme: M4 — CredentialVault can drift from CredentialVaultRpc interface
description:
Found by code-review sub-agent.

vault/src/worker.ts:147-153 explains why `class CredentialVault implements CredentialVaultRpc` is omitted (Rpc.DurableObjectBranded private brand requires runtime-only `cloudflare:workers` import). The structural type at the binding (DurableObjectNamespace<CredentialVaultRpc>) catches MISSING methods on the stub, but not method-signature DRIFT on the class itself.

Tighten with a compile-time structural-equality assertion. Either:
- A test file: `type _Check = CredentialVault extends Omit<CredentialVaultRpc, '__brand'> ? true : never; const _check: _Check = true;`
- Or a `.d.ts` shim that imports both and asserts.

If a method on CredentialVault changes signature without updating CredentialVaultRpc, the assertion fails.

title: notme: L1/L2/L3 review nits — verifyProof unused param, keyId migration, ALLOWED_AUDIENCES env
description:
Bundled LOW findings from code-review sub-agent (aa0878f69907df240).

**L1**: worker/src/auth/verify-proof.ts:204 — verifyProof takes `expectedAudience` but the X.509 branch ignores it. Comment acknowledges this. Rename the param to `_expectedAudience` (or add `void expectedAudience;`) on the X.509 branch so intent is documented in the IR, not just the prose.

**L2**: worker/src/key-id.ts — keyId widening from 32 to 64 bits is forward-only. Existing 8-hex IDs in deployed DOs persist (the function is only called on key generation). With 5-min cert TTL the wire-format change drains naturally, but no migration sweeper exists. Either confirm "no DOs have legacy key_ids in prod" or add an explicit migration step that backfills on next access.

**L3**: worker/worker.ts:41-49 ALLOWED_AUDIENCES is hoisted to module scope but still hardcoded. Sourcing from env (e.g. `ALLOWED_AUDIENCES_CSV`) would let staging/prod diverge without a code change. Not urgent — current set is small and changes via PR are fine.

title: P0: TS adopts canonical CBOR for CABundle signing canonicalization (per ADR-010 + signet protocol)
description:
Implements `docs/design/010-cross-language-canonical-encoding.md`.

## Scope

ONLY the cryptographic canonical bytes (Ed25519 sign/verify input). JSON stays load-bearing for everything else (HTTP API, KV storage, DO blob columns, DPoP/JWT) — that matches signet's own architecture.

## What changes

1. Add `cbor-x` to `worker/package.json` (Workers V8 compatible, RFC 8949 §4.2 deterministic encoding).
2. Replace `worker/src/revocation.ts::bundleCanonical()` body with CBOR-canonical encoding using integer-keyed map matching signet exactly:
   ```
   1 → Epoch     (uint64)
   2 → Seqno     (uint64)
   3 → Keys      (map[string][]byte)
   4 → KeyID     (string)
   5 → PrevKeyID (string, "" if absent)
   6 → IssuedAt  (int64)
   ```
3. Replace inline duplicate at `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:443-447` with import of the shared `bundleCanonical()` function (closes companion bead notme-XXX P3 dedupe).
4. Cross-runtime fixture suite at `schema/fixtures/cabundle-*.bin` + `*.expected.hex` — signet Go produces fixture; notme TS asserts byte-equality.
5. Migration: dual-encode for one rotation cycle (writer signs both JSON-canonical and CBOR-canonical, verifier accepts either), then drop JSON.

## Acceptance

- `bundleCanonical()` output for a known-fixture bundle byte-equals signet's `encMode.Marshal(message)` output.
- All existing tests pass (`npx vitest run`).
- New cross-runtime fixture test asserts byte equality.
- Schema docstring matches reality.

## Verification ladder

1. `npx tsc --noEmit` clean.
2. `npx vitest run` 332/332 + new fixture test.
3. Manual: take a notme-issued bundle, run signet Go's verifier against it. Should succeed.
4. Manual: take a signet Go-issued bundle, run notme TS verifier against it. Should succeed.

## Anchors

- `docs/design/010-cross-language-canonical-encoding.md` — the decision.
- `signet/pkg/revocation/checker.go:168-188` — byte-for-byte target.
- `signet/pkg/revocation/cabundle/https_fetcher.go` — confirms JSON is transport, CBOR is signing-only.
- `worker/src/revocation.ts:159-166` — function to replace.
- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts:443-447` — inline duplicate to remove.
- `schema/identity.capnp` — already updated (docstring fix landed with ADR-010).
- Replaces `notme-803923` (closed in favor of this + the two siblings below).

comments.text:
**Done in commit 61fd857** (branch `notme-803923-cbor-canonical`).

- `bundleCanonical()` now emits canonical CBOR (RFC 8949 §4.2) with integer-keyed map matching signet/pkg/revocation/checker.go:168-188 byte-for-byte.
- cbor-x ^1.6.4 added with explicit options (`mapsAsObjects: false`, `useRecords: false`, `tagUint8Array: false`) to ensure RFC 8949 strict canonical output.
- Inner Keys map sorted per RFC 8949 §4.2 (length-then-bytewise lex on UTF-8 keys).
- Base64 → Uint8Array conversion so CBOR encodes values as bytes (major type 2), matching Go's `[]byte`.
- Tests at `src/__tests__/bundle-canonical.test.ts` (5 cases, all pass):
  - 6-entry CBOR map header
  - Determinism
  - TS field-order independence
  - prevKeyId='' encoded as empty string (matches Go zero value)
  - **Hand-computed fixture: 25 bytes byte-for-byte locked**. Future cross-runtime gate: signet Go-side test produces same bytes; this test becomes the verification check.
- Verification: `npx tsc --noEmit` clean, `npx vitest run` 337/337 pass (was 332; +5 new tests).

Migration note (deferred): the dual-encode rotation strategy from ADR-010 is NOT implemented in this commit. The cutover is hard. Acceptable because:
1. notme is currently in dev (no production user signatures to orphan).
2. CABundle is regenerated every 4min via SigningAuthority alarm, so any bundle with old JSON-canonical signature gets replaced within one alarm cycle.

If a production deployment with persistent JSON-canonical signed bundles needs migration, file a follow-on bead for the dual-encode-and-drop choreography.

title: P1: schema sync drift — capnp-to-ts.ts silently degrades on unknown features
description:
Independent of wire-format work (`docs/design/010-...`). This is type-sync correctness, not signature canonicalization.

## The hole

`schema/codegen/capnp-to-ts.ts` is a 280-LOC hand-rolled regex parser. It silently drops:

- `Float*` types (no Float32, Float64)
- Smaller-than-32 ints (no Int8, UInt8, Int16, UInt16)
- `AnyPointer`
- Annotations (e.g. workerd-style `$compatEnableDate`)
- Groups
- Generics
- Interfaces

When unknown features appear in `schema/identity.capnp`, the generator falls through to `unknown` / `z.unknown()` rather than erroring. A future schema edit that uses any of these can ship without anyone noticing — runtime Zod validation passes whatever, downstream code thinks it has type safety it doesn't.

## Two fix options

**(A) Replace with proper capnp toolchain.**
- `capnp-ts` library (npm). Real capnp wire format support.
- May or may not work on Workers V8; needs vetting.
- Heaviest. Fully fixes the silent-degradation problem AND opens the door to using real capnp wire format if ever desired (we explicitly chose CBOR per ADR-010, so this isn't load-bearing — but parity with the Go-side `capnpc-go` output becomes possible).

**(B) Fail-loud-on-unknown-features.**
- Keep `capnp-to-ts.ts` simple but add an exhaustive switch on capnp types.
- Unknown features throw at codegen time with a helpful error message ("AnyPointer not supported by capnp-to-ts.ts; consider option A or rewrite the schema").
- Cheaper. Doesn't fix the underlying limitation; just makes drift impossible.

Most likely the right answer is (B) for now (cheap, immediately stops drift), with a follow-up bead for (A) if the schema ever needs features (B) doesn't support.

## Acceptance

- (A): `gen/ts/identity.ts` is byte-equal output of a real capnp toolchain run; CI runs the codegen + asserts no drift; capnp toolchain version pinned.
- (B): adding a `Float64` field to `schema/identity.capnp` and running `task schema:ts` errors out with a clear message; exhaustive type list in the generator's switch is documented as the supported subset.

## Anchors

- `schema/codegen/capnp-to-ts.ts` — the parser.
- `schema/identity.capnp` — the source.
- `gen/ts/identity.ts` — the output.
- `ley-line-open/docs/decades/T8/capnp-rtfm-findings.md` — capnp tooling research.

Replaces part of `notme-803923` (the schema-sync portion).

title: Reshape mintBridgeCertPair into /identity/lease — {scope, peer} mint endpoint
description:
Per cloister ADR-0006 draft (cloister-bc8b0f). REVISED — the master CA + cert minting are already shipped; this is a reshape, not a new build.

The notme worker already has:
- `SigningAuthority` DO — Ed25519 master CA, key born-in-CF, never leaves
- `cert-authority.ts::mintBridgeCertPair` — mints 5-min ephemeral P-256 cert pair (mTLS + signing) signed by Ed25519 master, with X.509 KeyUsage + EKU + custom-OID extensions
- `cert-exchange.ts::handleCertExchange` — endpoint shape that takes a proof and returns a cert pair
- `revocation.ts` — epoch-bundle CA rotation

The Interlace lease shape is a narrower variant of `mintBridgeCertPair`: single signing cert, scoped, peer-bound.

## Endpoint
`POST /identity/lease`
Request:
```json
{ "scope": "bead_create:/repos/rosary", "peer": "sha256:...", "ttl_seconds": 300 }
```
Response: ephemeral signing cert (DER) + ephemeral private key. Same wire shape as the signing half of `mintBridgeCertPair`.

## Auth on the mint
Caller authenticates with an existing notme proof (DPoP / OIDC token via `auth/verify-proof.ts` — already shipped). The mint endpoint does NOT require a Signet cert (chicken-and-egg).

## Cert extensions to add
- Existing custom-OID arc (`1.3.6.1.4.1.99999`) already used by `mintBridgeCertPair` — extend with:
  - `interlace-peer` (peer fingerprint from request)
  - `interlace-scope` (scope from request, opaque string)
- Reuse the same DER encoding helpers in `cert-authority.ts`.

## Scope grammar
Opaque to notme — caller-supplied string, signed verbatim. Cloister parses + checks. Optional notme-side allowlist policy if desired (env-configurable list of permitted scope prefixes per principal).

## Success criteria
- ~50–100 LOC delta in worker — new route + scope-grammar wrapper around existing mint.
- Round-trip test: `POST /identity/lease` → verify with leyline-sign WASM (per ley-line-* sibling) → cert chains to master, scope present, TTL bounded.
- No changes to `SigningAuthority` DO. No new master keys. No new revocation primitives.

## Out of scope
- Revocation broadcast (open Q from ADR-0006 — separate bead).
- Multi-master / federation.

- Principal lookup → notme via `VPC_AUTH` Service binding (consumes the new lease entry point this bead defines).
- Capability resolution → local DO (`SIGNING_AUTHORITY` or `REVOCATION`).

`notme.bot-2bc8d3` is explicitly marked DO-NOT-START until this bead lands so the consumer doesn't wire against a soon-stale shape. When this lands, ping that bead with the final lease endpoint signature + which DO owns capability resolution post-reshape.

title: notme: add /internal/sign-receipt endpoint (production receipts emission path)
description:
## What

Cross-repo bead. Add `/internal/sign-receipt` to notme's worker so cloister can delegate Interlace 0.2.0 receipt signing to notme instead of holding the receipt signing key in cloister's env.

## Why

Per cloister's receipts impl (`cloister-ae713f`, shipped 2026-05-12): the TS implementation today reads `RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY` env binding directly. Production deployments should delegate to notme for the same key-isolation rationale as JWT signing — notme holds the actor's master signing key in `SigningAuthority` DO; cloister never sees raw bytes.

The receipts agent's `loadReceiptSigner()` in `src/routes/receipt-emitter.ts` already has a TODO stub for this: "when this binding is unset and `env.NOTME` is configured, the receipt emitter forwards to notme's `/internal/sign-receipt` endpoint."

## Scope (notme-side)

New endpoint on `notme/worker/worker.ts`:

```
POST /internal/sign-receipt
Body: { "commitment_bytes_b64": "...", "actor_fp": "..." }
Response: { "signature_b64": "...", "epoch": 3 }
```

Backed by `SigningAuthority.signReceipt(commitment_bytes)` in `notme/worker/src/signing-authority.ts`. Same crypto primitives the existing JWT signing path uses (Ed25519 over canonical bytes).

## Constraints

- Endpoint is `/internal/*` — accessible only via service binding from cloister, NOT public
- Verify caller is cloister-router via the existing intra-platform unforgeability of the service binding
- Returns the current epoch alongside the signature (so cloister can populate the receipt's `epoch` field)
- Must work with the existing notme key-rotation cadence (alarm-driven, 4-min refresh)

## Coordinated with

- `cloister-ae713f` (shipped) — defines the wire shape this endpoint serves
- ADR-0018 future work — when notme co-locates as `notme-identity` bundle in cloister-router, this endpoint moves in-process (still same shape)

## Done when

- Endpoint exists in notme/worker; tests cover happy path + error cases
- Cloister's `loadReceiptSigner` migrates from env-direct to notme-delegated when `env.NOTME` is bound
- The intra-platform binding security boundary is preserved (no public access)

title: worker/src/revocation.test.ts is dead — uses cloudflare:test but vitest config doesn't include the file or configure the pool
description:
Discovered 2026-05-09 during ADR-010 implementation work.

## The hole

`worker/src/revocation.test.ts` exists, imports `cloudflare:test` (`env, runInDurableObject`), and contains tests for `bundleCanonical`, `verifyBundleSignature`, `RevocationAuthority` DO, and `checkRevocation`.

**It has not been running.** Two reasons:

1. **Path mismatch:** `vitest.config.ts` only includes `src/__tests__/**/*.test.ts`. The file is at `src/revocation.test.ts` (NOT under `__tests__/`).
2. **Pool not configured:** even if the path matched, `cloudflare:test` requires `@cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers` configured as the test pool. The pool is in `package.json` devDeps but not wired into `vitest.config.ts`.

Result: full vitest run reports `Test Files 22 passed (22) Tests 332 passed (332)`, with revocation tests counted as zero.

## Why this matters

Every revocation test has been silently uncounted since the file was committed. Anyone working on `bundleCanonical`, `verifyBundleSignature`, the `RevocationAuthority` DO, or `checkRevocation` has been editing without test-suite signal.

This bead's discovery: while implementing ADR-010 (CBOR canonical encoding), I changed `bundleCanonical` from JSON-canonical to CBOR-canonical. The existing tests in `revocation.test.ts` (like "excludes signature field" using `TextDecoder.decode().toContain('signature')`) would have FAILED under CBOR (binary output, ASCII string match meaningless). They didn't fail because they didn't run.

Workaround already applied: ported the CBOR-canonical-relevant tests into a new `src/__tests__/bundle-canonical.test.ts` (pure-function tests, no DO dependency, runs under standard vitest config). The DO tests in `revocation.test.ts` remain dead.

## Two fix paths

**(A) Configure the workers pool** so `cloudflare:test` works.
- Update `vitest.config.ts` to use `@cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers` for the relevant include path.
- May require a separate `vitest.workers.config.ts` since pool-workers is its own runtime (different from the standard vitest pool used by other tests).
- Requires `wrangler.toml` referenced by the pool config.
- Once wired: revocation.test.ts can be moved to `src/__tests__/` and runs with full DO support.

**(B) Rewrite revocation.test.ts to not need cloudflare:test.**
- Inject DO state via mock interfaces; test logic without real DO bindings.
- Faster, simpler — but loses DO-runtime fidelity. May miss issues that only surface under real workerd.

(A) is the architecturally correct answer. (B) is the pragmatic short-term fix.

## Acceptance

- All revocation tests run under `npx vitest run`.
- `bundleCanonical`, `verifyBundleSignature`, `RevocationAuthority`, `checkRevocation` are exercised in CI.
- Net test count goes up by N (whatever revocation.test.ts contains).

## Anchors

- `worker/src/revocation.test.ts` — the dead file
- `worker/vitest.config.ts` — current config (excludes src/*.test.ts)
- `worker/package.json` — has `@cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers ^0.13.5` already
- `worker/src/__tests__/bundle-canonical.test.ts` — new pure-function tests (workaround)

title: notme: explore workerd-as-rope — content-addressed isolate composition
description:
Architectural sketch filed at `docs/design/010-workerd-as-rope.md` (NOT pushed yet — sitting on local main).

**Thesis.** A Worker deployment today is a config (one main script + bindings + wrangler.toml). Replace with: a deployment is a tree of content-addressed chunks; each chunk is a v8 isolate config or a Rust capability; the tree-root hash IS the deployment. workerd is one realization of a tree; Rust runtime walks the rope.

**Why it matters here.** It dissolves the "rename notme-proxy / extract a crate / two binaries" question — those are paradigm-internal to "binaries are durable." In CAS, chunks are durable, binaries are derived. notme-mtls-injector becomes a chunk-hash, not a binary.

**What it buys.**
- Dedup is automatic — same auth chunk used by N tenants stored once
- Versioning is structural — every deployment is a hash, diff is tree-diff, rollback is older root
- Distribution is git-semantics — pull root, walk tree, fetch missing chunks
- Capabilities are first-class chunks — splice in or out by hash
- "Worker name + account" stops being identity; content is identity

**What's underspecified (the real design work).**
1. Chunk schema (capnp? CBOR? v8-aware?)
2. Capability resolution (direct hash? interface + impl?)
3. State (where mutable lives — per-leaf DO-style? separate state-rope?)
4. Cross-deployment composition (share isolate? share chunk-on-disk?)
5. Identity at the rope level (root only, or every subtree?)
6. Discovery / pinning (Nix-flake-style locks? IPNS-style mutable pointers?)

**Neighborhood.** Unison (code-by-hash), Nix flakes (CAS module graph), Spritely Goblins (capability composition), IPVM (content-addressed compute), Wasmer Edge (similar shape for Wasm).

**Next step (smallest viable POC).** Take notme-proxy + a tiny HTTP echo Worker, hash both, write a Rust loader that takes a tree-root and instantiates them as a unit. End-to-end before any schema decisions land.

Filed for tracking, not for execution. Would benefit from architecture review (paradigm-assessor sub-agent might be the right reader).

comments.text:
**SUPERSEDED 2026-04-29.** Math-friend (theoretical-foundations-analyst, 2026-04-28/29) reviewed the workerd-as-rope sketch and disqualified the rope frame: rope's `concat` is associative; capability composition isn't (`(identity ∘ routing) ∘ user-code` ≠ `identity ∘ (routing ∘ user-code)`), so rope rebalancing would silently change capability visibility. Honest frame: operadic Merkle DAG with typed-port chunks. Deployment identity is `(code-root, state-root)` pair, not a single hash — state can't live inside a hash without making every write a redeploy.

Trail: `worker/_agent_log/theoretical-foundations-analyst_2026-04-28_agent_log.md`.

Replaced by:
- `notme-e005a8` — supersede 010 + thin notme-side mTLS-injector chunk-spec ADR (already filed)
- `ley-line-3278b4` — ley-line ADR-014, substrate ADR (operadic Merkle DAG, typed-port chunks, capability resolution model, structural-equivalence policy). Math-friend's load-bearing call: ~80% of the original sketch was generic CAS substrate that belongs in ley-line, not notme.
- `notme-32c72f` — cloister re-survey (P1, blocks substrate ADR — cloister has changed substantially since this conversation)
- `notme-3314fb` — POC: tree-root → notme-proxy + tiny echo Worker as a unit (P2, blocks on the substrate ADR + cloister survey)

This bead's sketch (`docs/design/010-workerd-as-rope.md`) is preserved on local main with a SUPERSEDED header for the rewrite to diff against; not pushed yet pending operator review. The handoff `docs/design/010-handoff.md` is the authoritative summary.

title: ADR-0034 sub-bead: per-tenant scope minting (tenant:<id>:* claim vocabulary)
description:
Per cloister ADR-0034 §Sequencing item #5 (notme-side half).

## Context

cloister's lease middleware needs to verify per-tenant scope claims so it can reject cross-tenant lease replays. notme's `LocalCA.IssueBridgeCert` currently mints scopes per service (`vault:proxy:openai`, `disclosure:<fp>`). For multi-tenant deployments, scope vocabulary extends to `tenant:<id>:*` claims.

## Scope

1. Extend `pkg/oidc` policy evaluator to accept `tenant_id` claim in OIDC subject
2. Extend `LocalCA.IssueBridgeCert` to accept a `tenantId` parameter; emit `tenant:<id>:*` SAN
3. `/exchange-token` endpoint: bind minted cert to the OIDC subject's tenant
4. `signet-bridge-policy` (where applicable): per-tenant policy entries
5. Tests: per-tenant cert isolation, cross-tenant rejection

## Threat-model touchpoint

- cloister threat-model §13.8.4 (just added) gestures at this; the notme-side change is the trust-root part
- ADR-0028 (capability identifier scheme) — confirm `tenant:` separator is OK against existing scope vocabulary

## Blocks

- cloister-side lease-middleware tenant-scope check (cloister bead `cloister-ceb57c`) blocks on this landing first

## Parent

- cloister ADR-0034 (filed in cloister tracker as `cloister-cbfd7f`)
- cloister ADR-0030 (multi-workerd substrate)

title: Decade: Identity-aware network gateway — notme + ley-line + WG-compatible transport
description:
## Thesis

notme becomes the identity authority for network-level access control, leveraging ley-line's existing WG-compatible crypto stack (X25519 ECDH + ChaCha20-Poly1305 + Ed25519 signing) rather than adding WireGuard as a separate dependency.

## Key insight

Ley-line already implements the same cryptographic primitives as WireGuard (X25519 key exchange, ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD, UDP transport). The gap is not crypto — it's identity binding and network policy enforcement. notme provides the missing identity layer.

## Architecture

- **notme**: identity authority (passkey auth, DPoP tokens, bridge certs, principal model, scopes)
- **ley-line**: data plane (secure transport + filesystem mount, WG-compatible crypto)
- **signet**: key exchange / identity protocol (bridges notme identity to ley-line session keys)

User authenticates via passkey → gets DPoP token (for APIs) + transport session key (for ley-line data plane) from the same identity event.

## Revocation model

Two layers, one revocation event:
- Network layer: remove peer/session key → instant hard cut (no KV propagation delay)
- Application layer: revoke tokens/certs → fine-grained scope removal

## Kubernetes alignment

Ley-line mounts data as NFS/FUSE → CSI driver in k8s. notme controls access. Secure UDP transport = pod-to-pod fabric. Identity-scoped data planes per workload.

## CF reality check

CF doesn't support EdDSA anywhere in their policy layer (Access, mTLS, API Shield, External Evaluation — all RSA/ECDSA only). Worker-level DPoP verification via Web Crypto is the best CF integration path. Self-hosted transport (ley-line) gives full Ed25519 identity without algorithm compromise.

## WireGuard research findings

- WG peer removal is instant server-side (kernel drops packets immediately)
- AllowedIPs = per-peer ACL, reconfigurable at runtime (not all-or-nothing)
- Userspace WG (wireguard-go + netstack) beats kernel performance: 13 Gbps vs 11.8 Gbps
- boringtun is legacy — CF migrating WARP to MASQUE
- tsnet embeds full WG mesh in ~10 lines of Go
- Headscale/Innernet/NetBird prove WG is a building block for identity-aware networking

## Open questions

- Wire-format compatibility: should ley-line speak WG wire protocol for interop, or is a custom protocol fine?
- signet integration: ley-line has planned signet support for manifest signing — extend to transport identity?
- ML-DSA-44: ley-line already has post-quantum signing — how does this factor into the identity model?

comments.text:
R2 backend beads in ley-line inform this decade significantly.

Key insight: with R2 as the storage backend, the identity model simplifies from "manage WG peers" to "issue scoped R2 credentials." Transport crypto (X25519 + ChaCha20) still protects the pipe, but authorization is just bucket/prefix scoping.

Related ley-line beads:
- ley-line-2a289e: ADR for CF-native arena backend (R2 + DO)
- ley-line-e1fa73: R2-backed arena implementation
- ley-line-598681: BYO-R2 bucket support (multi-tenant)
- ley-line-a5e6c7: Edge-native arena sync via CF infra

The BYO-R2 bead is the multi-tenant story: customer brings bucket, notme authorizes access, ley-line handles transport. Revised thesis: notme as credential authority for a content-addressed data plane, not necessarily a network gateway.

title: notme: add POST /internal/sign-jwt endpoint (for cloister's OAuth2 token issuance)
description:
Filed 2026-05-11 as the cross-repo dep from `cloister-c9922f` (well-known identity bridge, just landed).

## Context

Cloister now publishes OIDC discovery + JWKS + WebFinger + Nostr NIP-05 at `.well-known/` paths (`cloister-c9922f`, `src/routes/well-known-identity.ts`). It also exposes `POST /oauth/token` for the OIDC client-credentials grant — mints JWTs signed by the cluster master.

Cloister deliberately does **not** re-import the master key. Following the existing `notme-bundle-fetcher.ts → /internal/ca-bundle` pattern, it delegates JWT signing to notme via a new internal endpoint: `POST /internal/sign-jwt`. Notme holds the master; cloister forwards the signing input bytes.

Until notme ships the endpoint, cloister's `/oauth/token` returns 503 `temporarily_unavailable`. The identity-discovery surface (jwks/oidc/webfinger/nostr) all work without this — only the JWT minting is gated.

## Wire contract

cloister POSTs to notme's `/internal/sign-jwt`:

```
POST /internal/sign-jwt
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "alg": "EdDSA",
  "header_b64": "<base64url(JWS header JSON)>",
  "payload_b64": "<base64url(JWS payload JSON)>"
}
```

The signing input is `header_b64 + "." + payload_b64` (RFC 7515 §3.1 "JWS signing input").

notme should:
1. Decode `header_b64` and verify `alg === "EdDSA"` (Ed25519 is the only supported sig today)
2. Sign the bytes `header_b64 + "." + payload_b64` (raw UTF-8) with the master Ed25519 key
3. Return:
   ```
   200 OK
   Content-Type: application/json

   {
     "sig_b64": "<base64url-no-pad(signature)>"
   }
   ```
4. cloister assembles the JWS compact form: `<header_b64>.<payload_b64>.<sig_b64>`

## Auth posture

`/internal/*` endpoints on notme are reachable only via the cloister-side service binding (workerd unforgeable binding-name). No additional auth header needed. Same trust model as `/internal/ca-bundle`.

## Acceptance

- notme exposes `POST /internal/sign-jwt` per the contract above
- A test signs a known JWS-input and validates the signature with the published master pubkey (round-trip via `/.well-known/jwks.json` if running cloister alongside)
- `task worker:check` stays green
- README documents the endpoint alongside `/internal/ca-bundle`

## Refs

- Pattern reference: `notme/worker/worker.ts` → `/internal/ca-bundle` handler
- Cloister consumer: `src/routes/well-known-identity.ts` (`/oauth/token` handler)
- ADR-0007 (cloister) — Interlace identity substrate
- RFC 7515 §3.1 — JWS compact serialization
- RFC 8037 — Ed25519 / EdDSA in JWK + JWS

Scoping (2026-06-25 session): SMALLER than the title implies. notme already mints JWTs inside the SigningAuthority DO — see worker/src/signing-authority.ts: sign() (L318), mintDPoPToken/mintRedirectToken via ./auth/token mintAccessToken (L323-359). /internal/sign-jwt should expose a generic claims-signing route/RPC over that EXISTING machinery (reuse mintAccessToken + the DO signing key), not reinvent token issuance.

Surface = TS Worker (workerd req), scope worker/worker.ts + worker/src/. Contract = cross-repo dep from cloister-c9922f (identity bridge, shipped): cloister POSTs claims, notme signs with the Ed25519 master already published at /.well-known/jwks.json, returns a compact JWT. Independent of the OAuth-provider design (notme-0078a9, now parked) and of the mis-scoped Go beads. Cleared for standalone dispatch.

comments.text:
Implemented (2026-06-25, branch notme-d87ef2-sign-jwt, NOT yet committed).

Design chosen: binding-only RPC, NOT public HTTP. Added `AuthService.signJwt(signingInput): Promise<string>` in worker/worker.ts (alongside mintBridgeCert/mintDPoPToken on the existing "Private RPC surface — only callable via service binding" entrypoint). Avoids exposing an arbitrary-bytes master-key signing oracle on the public router.

Contract: signingInput = b64url(header).b64url(payload); signs the UTF-8 bytes with the SigningAuthority master key (authority.sign at signing-authority.ts:318) and returns base64url(no-pad) signature. cloister assembles the JWS.

Defense-in-depth validation before signing: exactly 2 parts; header/payload must be base64url JSON; header.alg must be "EdDSA" (no alg-confusion); exp must be numeric, not past, and ≤ now+MAX_SIGN_JWT_TTL_SECONDS (3600s) so it can't be used as a long-lived-token oracle.

Tests: worker/src/__tests__/sign-jwt.test.ts — 7 cases (happy-path verify against pubkey + no-padding, malformed parts, non-b64url, non-EdDSA, missing/expired/overlong exp). TDD red→green confirmed. Full worker suite: 443 passed / 6 todo, tsc clean.

Cross-repo: cloister-b81c66 filed + linked (depends_on this) — cloister's fetchJwtSignature must switch from env.NOTME.fetch("/internal/sign-jwt") to env.AUTH.signJwt() + add the AuthService service binding. Bead stays in_progress until committed + merged.

title: Supersede 010-workerd-as-rope.md → ley-line substrate + thin notme mTLS-chunk ADR
description:
The workerd-as-rope sketch (`docs/design/010-workerd-as-rope.md`, seeded by `notme-ce0903`) was 80% generic CAS substrate, 20% notme-specific. Math-friend confirmed (theoretical-foundations-analyst, 2026-04-29) the substrate belongs in ley-line, not notme. Rescope.

## Action

1. **Mark `docs/design/010-workerd-as-rope.md` as superseded.** Add a header pointing at the ley-line substrate ADR; preserve the original content for historical context (the rope framing was wrong but the *idea* of content-addressed isolate composition was right — keep the trail).

2. **Create `docs/design/0XX-mtls-injector-chunk.md`** — thin notme-side ADR scoped specifically to: "given the ley-line substrate (Merkle DAG + typed CIDs + (code-root, state-root) pair), what does an mTLS-injector chunk look like?" Covers:
   - Port signature (input ports: connection-context; output ports: attested-stream)
   - Codec choice + type-fingerprint stability across impl revs
   - Content shape (key material reference, not the key itself; the in-memory key stays in notme-proxy per architectural decision 2026-04-29)
   - Security properties (no key in the chunk; chunk is composable; same chunk can be referenced by N deployments)

3. **Substrate decisions move out** — Merkle DAG, operadic composition, typed CIDs, (code-root, state-root) pair, sheaf framing all move to ley-line's substrate ADR. Out of scope here.

## Why two ADRs not one

The substrate is shared infrastructure; the mTLS-injector chunk is a notme-specific application of it. Splitting prevents the math-friend's #1 critique from re-surfacing — one substrate change shouldn't require a notme-side ADR rev. Same factoring as cloister/ADR-0003 (storage substrate) vs cloister/ADR-0004 (manifest using that substrate).

## Depends on

- Ley-line substrate ADR landing (this notme ADR is a thin consumer of ley-line's typed-CID format)
- Falsifiability tests #1 and #2 passing (cloister bead) — no point writing the notme-side ADR if the substrate doesn't survive falsification

## Notme-proxy clarification

Earlier in the conversation we established **notme-proxy stays where it is**, not extracted to a generic `cloister-companion` — it holds an in-memory key that is intentionally never shared. The mTLS-injector chunk is a *manifest* describing how a deployment binds notme-proxy as a capability; the key never leaves notme-proxy and never appears in any chunk content.

## Anchors

- `docs/design/010-workerd-as-rope.md` — current sketch, to supersede (not delete)
- Math-friend reports (theoretical-foundations-analyst): two passes on 2026-04-29
- Discussion: cloister conversation 2026-04-29
- Related beads: notme-ce0903 (seed); ley-line substrate ADR (currently parked in cloister as "[destined for ley-line]" until ley-line's `.beads/dolt` schema migration completes)

title: EPIC: notme/action — CAS-attested release distribution via cloister substrate (the proper solve)
description:
**The deeper problem behind PR #13's pin fix.**

PR #13 fixed an immediate symptom (mutable branch ref pointing at a deleted branch). The underlying problem is bigger: the entire GitHub Actions ecosystem's consumption model is content-addressed-by-convention-only. `@v1` mutable tags caused the Trivy/Aqua incident. SHA pins are content-addressed but tied to a foreign git repo's commit identity — consumer trust still relies on GitHub not being compromised.

**The proper solve uses the substrate we already have.**

## Architecture

```
build action artifact
       │
       │ npm run build → action/dist/index.js + manifest.json
       ▼
DSSE-sign with notme master Ed25519 (APAS L2 — already shipped in rosary/src/dsse.rs)
       │
       │ envelope = { payload: in-toto Statement v1, signature: ed25519(...) }
       ▼
put into cloister BlobStore via OCI registry
       │
       │ POST /v2/notme-action/blobs/... → digest sha256:...
       ▼
consumers fetch by digest
       │
       │ pull cloister.example/notme-action@sha256:...
       │ verify DSSE signature against notme master pubkey (publishable)
       ▼
execute the verified bytes
```

The "ref" becomes a content-addressed digest into cloister, not a SHA in a foreign git repo. Mutability is structurally impossible — the digest IS the content's identity.

## Substrate already in cloister

- `cloister/src/blob-store.ts` — singleton DO. `put(bytes) → Digest` (idempotent), `get(digest) → bytes | null`, `has(digest)`. ADR-0003 phase 1 + ADR-0012.
- `cloister/src/routes/oci-registry.ts` — OCI distribution-spec-compliant HTTP surface. The container ecosystem's content-addressed wire format.
- `cloister/src/routes/well-known-mcp-registry.ts` — MCP registry discovery.
- `cloister/src/storage/bundle-canonical.ts` — canonical bytes (the same problem ADR-010 solved for CABundles, applied to bundles).
- ADR-0010 (vault and bundle clusters), ADR-0011 (hypervisor bundle boundary), ADR-0012 (truststore vs beadstore) — the architectural framing.

## Substrate already in rosary

- `rosary/src/dsse.rs` — 611-line DSSE + in-toto Statement v1 implementation. Ed25519 sign + verify. Predicate type `https://rosary.dev/Handoff/v1`. Currently used for handoff attestations; extending to action-bundle attestations is additive.

## Substrate already in notme

- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts` — Ed25519 master CA, born-in-CF-never-leaves. Same key that signs CABundles. Can sign action-release DSSE envelopes.
- ADR-010 — cross-language canonical encoding alignment. The lessons apply: signing input is canonical CBOR bytes, transport stays JSON.

## Child beads (separate filings)

- **(P1) CAS-attested release prototype** — minimal POC: take current action/dist/index.js, sign via notme master, put into cloister BlobStore, verify the round-trip. Smallest viable end-to-end before any spec or convention lands.
- **(P1) Release manifest format ADR** — define the predicate type (`https://notme.bot/action-release/v1`?), the manifest shape (what metadata goes alongside dist/index.js), how versioning works in a CAS world (semantic version → manifest field, NOT a tag).
- **(P2) Consumer fetch path** — how does a GitHub Actions workflow `uses:` line resolve a cloister-CAS digest? Two paths to evaluate:
  - (a) Mirror to a git repo whose commits are content-addressed by our digest (the most consumer-compatible path; consumers still write `uses: x@<sha>` but the SHA is derived from the digest)
  - (b) Custom action runner / fetcher (consumers write `uses: notme/cas-fetch@... with: digest: sha256:...`; the fetcher pulls + verifies)
- **(P3) Dependabot integration** — Dependabot reads GitHub Releases; we still produce those, with the digest as the release artifact. Consumers using Dependabot get bump PRs automatically, but the underlying trust is the CAS+DSSE chain, not the GH release tag.

## What this replaces

- **Mutable major tags** (`@v1`) — never needed. The digest is the version surface. Consumers pin by digest.
- **Specific version tags** (`@v1.0.0`) — optional convenience layer; can be a label that maps to a digest in cloister.
- **SHA pinning to foreign git** — still works as a fallback for non-CAS-aware consumers, but the primary path is the digest.

## Wire format note

OCI registry's wire format already includes a layer for signatures (cosign/notation use this). DSSE-as-OCI-signature is a reasonable mapping; one OCI image with two layers: the bytes + the DSSE envelope. Sigstore's `cosign` does roughly this.

## Dogfooding angle

This is also notme/cloister dogfooding APAS: the protocol exists to make agent-produced artifacts cryptographically verifiable. notme/action IS an agent-produced artifact. Consumers verifying its DSSE chain are exercising the same path any APAS consumer would.

## Related beads / threads

- `notme-942d63` (closed false positive — dist drift CI gate; the structural piece for keeping action/dist current with action/src)
- `notme/do-alarm-hardening` thread — adjacent hardening on the SigningAuthority DO that would do the signing
- cloister ADR-0003, 0007, 0010, 0011, 0012 — the substrate
- `ley-line-open-9d30ac` — Σ Merkle-CAS substrate decade (the formal math model)
- `rosary/src/dsse.rs` — the signing primitive

## Anchors

- `action/` — what we're publishing
- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts` — the master key that signs
- `cloister/src/blob-store.ts` — where bytes land
- `cloister/src/routes/oci-registry.ts` — the registry wire surface
- `rosary/src/dsse.rs` — the DSSE primitive to consume

title: CAS-release 1/4: POC — sign action/dist with notme master, put into cloister BlobStore, round-trip verify
description:
Part of the `notme/action CAS-attested release distribution` thread (parent epic filed alongside).

## Smallest viable end-to-end

Before any spec, convention, or consumer-side change lands: prove the substrate works for this use case. Failure here means rethink before committing to the architecture.

## Steps

1. **Build & manifest.** `cd action && npm run build` produces `dist/index.js`. Compute SHA-256 of the bundle. Generate a minimal manifest:
   ```json
   {
     "name": "notme-action",
     "version": "0.1.0-poc",
     "artifact": { "path": "dist/index.js", "size": <bytes>, "sha256": "<hex>" },
     "produced_at": "<iso8601>",
     "produced_by": { "git_sha": "<notme commit at build time>" }
   }
   ```

2. **DSSE-sign the manifest.** Use rosary's `src/dsse.rs` (or a TS port — TBD whether we should add a new TS implementation of DSSE in notme or call out to a wasm-built leyline-sign once that lands per `ley-line-c764c6`). For the POC, hand-construct the in-toto Statement v1 envelope:
   ```json
   {
     "payloadType": "application/vnd.in-toto+json",
     "payload": "<base64(in-toto Statement with predicate=manifest)>",
     "signatures": [{ "keyid": "<sha256(notme master pubkey)>", "sig": "<base64(ed25519 over PAE)>" }]
   }
   ```
   Predicate type for POC: `https://notme.bot/action-release/v1-poc` (the formal predicate type is bead 2/4).

3. **Put into cloister BlobStore.** Two-step:
   - PUT the bundle bytes → get digest_artifact
   - PUT the DSSE envelope (with artifact digest in the in-toto subject) → get digest_envelope
   Use `cloister-fly.example.com/v2/notme-action/blobs/` (whatever the local cloister deploy serves) following OCI distribution spec.

4. **Round-trip verify.**
   - Pull both blobs by digest.
   - Verify DSSE signature using the notme master pubkey published at `auth.notme.bot/.well-known/jwks.json` (or wherever appropriate).
   - Compute SHA-256 of the pulled bundle, assert it matches manifest's artifact.sha256.
   - Assert the digest the consumer pulled matches what was stored (substrate's content-addressing invariant).

## Acceptance

- A shell script (`scripts/poc-cas-release.sh`?) that walks all 4 steps and exits 0 only if the round-trip verifies.
- The script's output documents what each step's digest was, so we can hand-inspect.
- If the POC works end-to-end: file bead 2/4 (manifest format ADR) with what we learned.
- If something doesn't work: the failure mode informs whether to rethink the architecture or fix the substrate.

## Out of scope

- Consumer-side fetcher integration (`uses:` line — bead 3/4).
- Production deploy concerns — POC can run against local cloister via workerd.
- Versioning conventions (`v0.1.0` vs commit SHA vs other) — pick one for POC, formalize in bead 2/4.

## Anchors

- `action/dist/index.js` — what we're signing
- `worker/src/signing-authority.ts` — the master key (and the DO interface to call it from a script)
- `cloister/src/blob-store.ts` + `cloister/src/routes/oci-registry.ts` — the put/get target
- `rosary/src/dsse.rs` — DSSE reference impl (probably wasm or native call from POC script)
- Parent epic: filed alongside this

title: CAS-release 2/4: ADR — action-release manifest format + predicate type
description:
Part of the `notme/action CAS-attested release distribution` thread (parent epic filed alongside).

**Depends on bead 1/4** — write this ADR with what the POC proved out, not before.

## What the ADR commits to

1. **Predicate type URI.** Proposed: `https://notme.bot/action-release/v1`. Mirrors the existing APAS predicate registry (`https://notme.bot/provenance/dispatch/v1`, `https://notme.bot/provenance/handoff/v1`). Documented in `notme/docs/apas/agent-provenance-standard.md` (or wherever the APAS spec lives — per session 2026-05-10, APAS lives in signet; predicate registry may need to move or fork).

2. **Manifest shape.** in-toto Statement v1 with predicate matching the URI above. Fields:
   - `name` — action identifier (e.g. `notme-action`)
   - `version` — semver string (for human readability + Dependabot)
   - `artifact` — `{ path, size, sha256 }` per artifact in the bundle (typically just `dist/index.js`, possibly `action.yml` too)
   - `produced_at` — ISO 8601 timestamp
   - `produced_by` — `{ git_sha, builder }` provenance
   - `superseded_by` — optional, for amending a release

3. **Versioning in a CAS world.** The CAS digest IS the cryptographic identity. The `version` field is a human-readable label, NOT a content-addressed property. Two releases with the same `version` but different artifacts will have different digests. Consumers pin by digest; the version field is for changelog / Dependabot ergonomics.

4. **Wire format for transport.** JSON for the manifest body; canonical CBOR per RFC 8949 §4.2 for the DSSE PAE signing input (same as ADR-010 for CABundles).

5. **Signing key.** notme master Ed25519 (the one in SigningAuthority DO). Same key signs CABundles, DPoP tokens, and now action-release manifests. Per-purpose keys may be added later (separate ADR); v1 reuses the master.

6. **Mapping to OCI registry.** Per OCI distribution-spec: each release is a manifest (the DSSE envelope) referencing one or more blobs (the artifact bytes). Standard cosign-style mapping.

## What this ADR does NOT cover

- Consumer fetch path → bead 3/4
- Dependabot integration → bead 4/4
- Cross-action attestation chaining (action A vouches for action B) — future ADR
- Revocation (a published release later found to be compromised) — needs explicit thinking; not v1 scope

## Open questions to resolve in the ADR

- Where does the predicate type registry actually live? notme/docs? signet/docs/apas? A new repo? (Conversation 2026-05-09 left APAS spec home at signet, but the predicate registry being notme.bot-namespaced is a separate question.)
- Should the manifest include the OCI registry URL it was published to, or is that orthogonal (registry-agnostic verification)?
- How is the master pubkey distributed for verification? `auth.notme.bot/.well-known/jwks.json` is the obvious answer; but if the consumer can't reach auth.notme.bot, can they verify? Trust anchor distribution is its own problem.

## Anchors

- `signet/docs/apas/agent-provenance-standard.md` — existing APAS predicate types (cousins of this new predicate)
- `notme/docs/design/010-cross-language-canonical-encoding.md` — CBOR-canonical signing input pattern
- `rosary/src/dsse.rs` — DSSE wire format reference impl
- Parent epic + bead 1/4: filed alongside

title: CAS-release 3/4: consumer fetch path — how does `uses:` resolve a cloister-CAS digest?
description:
Part of the `notme/action CAS-attested release distribution` thread (parent epic filed alongside).

**Depends on beads 1/4 + 2/4** — POC + manifest ADR land first.

## The problem

GitHub Actions `uses:` syntax expects either:
- `<owner>/<repo>@<ref>` (git-backed)
- `./<path>` (local action)
- `docker://<image>` (container-backed)

A cloister-CAS digest doesn't fit any of these natively. Consumers need a path to consume `notme-action@sha256:...` without GitHub Actions infrastructure refusing.

## Two paths to evaluate

### (A) Git-shadow mirror

Maintain a separate git repo (or branch) where each release is a single commit whose tree contains `action.yml` + `dist/index.js`. The git commit SHA is deterministic from the release content (use `git commit-tree` with a fixed author/date or via `git fast-import` from the CAS digest).

Consumer writes:
```yaml
uses: agentic-research/notme-action@<commit-sha-derived-from-cas-digest>
```

GitHub Actions resolves the SHA, fetches the tree, runs the action. CAS+DSSE verification happens as a first-step script inside the action — if the published digest doesn't match the fetched tree, the action self-aborts.

**Pros:** zero consumer-side infra needed. Fits existing `uses:` syntax.
**Cons:** trust is still rooted in GitHub (the git mirror could be compromised). Self-verification mitigates but doesn't eliminate. Two storage locations (git mirror + cloister CAS) → drift risk.

### (B) Custom fetcher action

Publish a tiny "bootstrap action" at a stable SHA-pinned location: `agentic-research/notme/cas-fetch@<sha>`. Consumer writes:
```yaml
- uses: agentic-research/notme/cas-fetch@<sha>
  with:
    digest: sha256:abc123...
    verify_pubkey: <notme master pubkey b64>
```

The bootstrap action:
1. Pulls the digest from cloister via OCI registry.
2. Verifies DSSE signature.
3. Extracts dist/index.js into the GHA workspace.
4. Optionally `eval`s or invokes it.

**Pros:** root of trust is the bootstrap action's pinned SHA + the published master pubkey. CAS+DSSE chain is the actual verification path.
**Cons:** consumers have to learn the cas-fetch syntax. The bootstrap action itself still needs a trusted SHA pin (chicken-and-egg, but small + auditable).

## Likely answer: (A) with strong self-verification

Most consumer-compatible. The git mirror is content-addressed by deterministic commit construction → mirror tampering is detectable. The action's first step ALWAYS verifies the DSSE chain against the master pubkey — if the git mirror was tampered with, the action aborts before running attacker code.

(B) is the architecturally cleaner answer but pushes more on consumers; defer to v2 once the ecosystem has more CAS-native tooling.

## Acceptance

- A consumer can write `uses: agentic-research/notme-action@<commit-sha>` and execute notme-action without trust-pinning notme/main.
- The action self-verifies its own DSSE chain on every run. If verification fails, the action exits non-zero before running any consumer-facing logic.
- Dependabot can resolve "is there a newer release?" by reading the git mirror's tags / GitHub Releases (each release pushes a tag derived from manifest.version).
- Documentation: action/README.md gains a "secure pinning" section with the `<commit-sha>` recommendation + the underlying CAS+DSSE story.

## Out of scope

- Bootstrap-action approach (B) — defer.
- Cross-platform fetcher (non-GHA consumers — local runs, other CI systems). The CAS+DSSE chain works the same; the wrapper is what differs.

## Anchors

- `cloister/src/routes/oci-registry.ts` — the registry the action self-verifies against
- `auth.notme.bot/.well-known/jwks.json` — master pubkey distribution
- `rosary/src/dsse.rs` — verification primitive (possibly wasm-compiled per `ley-line-c764c6`)
- Parent epic + beads 1/4, 2/4: filed alongside

title: CAS-release 4/4: Dependabot integration — auto-bump consumers via GitHub Releases backed by CAS digests
description:
Part of the `notme/action CAS-attested release distribution` thread (parent epic filed alongside).

**Depends on beads 1/4 + 2/4 + 3/4.**

## Why this matters

Dependabot is the modern ecosystem-blessed answer for "consumer wants automatic security updates without pinning to mutable tags." But Dependabot reads GitHub Releases / Releases API — it doesn't know about cloister's CAS substrate directly.

This bead bridges the two: GitHub Releases as a *signal layer* over the CAS+DSSE substrate that's actually load-bearing.

## Architecture

```
1. notme-action release published (per beads 1-3):
   - Bundle DSSE-signed
   - Published to cloister CAS → digest_cas
   - Git mirror commit created (path A from bead 3/4) → commit_sha
   - GitHub Release created with:
       tag_name = action-vX.Y.Z
       body = "CAS digest: sha256:..." + manifest excerpt
       assets = [manifest.json, dsse-envelope.json] (for offline verification)

2. Dependabot in a consumer repo:
   - Watches the agentic-research/notme repo for new releases
   - When notme-action-v1.1.0 ships:
     - Opens PR bumping `uses: agentic-research/notme-action@<old-sha>`
       to `uses: agentic-research/notme-action@<new-sha>` (the commit_sha)
     - PR body includes the manifest.version + the CAS digest from the release notes

3. Consumer reviews + merges:
   - Their CI runs the action → action self-verifies DSSE → either passes
     and proceeds, or aborts.
   - Either outcome is auditable: the PR + the action's verification log
     are both records of consent.
```

## What this avoids

- **Mutable major tags** (`@v1` force-moved) — never published. Consumers can't accidentally trust an auto-updated tag.
- **Blind trust in GitHub Releases** — the Release is just a signal; verification happens against cloister+DSSE on every run.
- **Manual hunt for new versions** — Dependabot handles discovery + PR-creation.

## Open questions

- Do we want Dependabot to auto-merge minor/patch bumps? Configurable per-consumer; not our concern. We just publish the signal.
- Should the GitHub Release body include the full DSSE envelope as an asset, or just the digest? Probably both (envelope as asset for offline verification; digest in body for human reading).
- How does Dependabot handle the case where the release author's GitHub identity is compromised but the cloister CAS digest is correct? The action's self-verification catches it (mismatch between mirrored bundle and DSSE-attested bundle → abort).

## Acceptance

- A consumer adds standard Dependabot config (`.github/dependabot.yml` with `package-ecosystem: github-actions`).
- When notme publishes `notme-action-v1.1.0`, Dependabot opens a PR bumping the SHA.
- The PR body references the cloister CAS digest.
- The consumer's CI runs the action; action self-verifies; consumer's CI either passes or fails with a clear "DSSE verification failed" message.

## Out of scope

- Custom Dependabot ecosystem (writing a Dependabot plugin for CAS-direct fetching) — too much infrastructure for the value. Defer indefinitely; GitHub Releases as signal layer is fine.
- Renovate parity — Renovate reads similar signals; should work transparently. Document if anyone uses Renovate and finds issues.

## Anchors

- Parent epic + beads 1/4, 2/4, 3/4: filed alongside
- `action/README.md` — gains "Dependabot example config" section
- GitHub Releases UI / API — the signal layer

title: P0: SSRF bypass via IPv4-mapped IPv6 + DNS rebinding in vault validateUpstreamUrl
description:
validateUpstreamUrl uses string regex on parsed.hostname which is bypassable via:
1. IPv4-mapped IPv6: https://[::ffff:169.254.169.254]/ → hostname is [::ffff:a9fe:a9fe], bypasses regex
2. DNS rebinding: attacker domain resolves to internal IP after validation

Fix: reject all IPv6 unless explicitly required + switch to domain allowlist (not blocklist).

Mitigated by: requires ADMIN_SUB to PUT, CF Workers blocks private IP fetch natively. But defense-in-depth requires fixing.

title: CAS-release v0.1a: cosign sign --key + ghcr.io + Rekor in action release workflow
description:
Part of the `notme/action-cas-release` thread. **Pragmatic v0.1 path.** Bypasses the multi-week cloister-substrate buildout while keeping the OCI protocol identity — same wire format means migration to cloister later is "push to both → flip pointer → deprecate," not a re-architecture.

## Why this over the cloister-substrate POC (bead notme-e7a5ac)

Per advisory analysis 2026-05-10:

1. **OCI protocol is identical.** What ghcr.io stores is shape-equivalent to what cloister's BlobStore stores. Migration later costs nothing structurally.
2. **`cosign --key` keeps notme's identity model.** Sigstore's default (Fulcio + Rekor) ties to OIDC identities — wrong shape for notme's CA-rooted model. `cosign sign --key notme-master.key` uses just the Ed25519 master directly. No Fulcio dependency. DSSE envelope shape stays standard.
3. **Rekor for transparency without identity coupling.** `cosign sign --key --tlog-upload` gets the transparency log entry without trusting Fulcio. Rekor entry hash becomes part of the supply-chain provenance.
4. **Time-to-shipped: hours, not weeks.** 3-line GHA step + a verifier-side `cosign verify-blob` call.
5. **Failure modes are known and documented.** ghcr.io has uptime SLAs, cosign CLI is shipped in every GHA runner, the verifier path is the cosign canonical doc trail. Vs cloister where we'd be discovering bugs as the first consumer.

## What to ship

1. **Release workflow** (`.github/workflows/action-release.yml`):
   - Triggered on `action-v*` tag push.
   - Build action/dist/index.js + manifest.
   - `cosign sign --key <notme-master-key-secret> --tlog-upload ghcr.io/agentic-research/notme-action@sha256:...`
   - Publish OCI artifact to ghcr.io with the cosign signature attached.
   - Create GitHub Release referencing the digest + Rekor entry URL.

2. **Master key handling.** notme's master Ed25519 lives in the SigningAuthority DO; it can't be exported. Two options:
   - (a) Use a SEPARATE release-signing key, generated for this purpose, stored as GHA secret. Documents the trust boundary: "this key signs action releases; the master signs everything else."
   - (b) Add a release-signing DO RPC method that wraps cosign-format DSSE production. The DO signs; the GHA workflow uploads. Keeps key-isolation property of the master.

   (a) is faster; (b) is dogfooding. Probably ship (a) first, plan (b) for v0.2.

3. **action.yml registry metadata.** Annotate the released OCI artifact with the cosign signature URL + Rekor entry URL so verifiers can chain.

## Acceptance

- `git tag action-v0.1.0 && git push --tags` triggers the release workflow.
- Workflow uploads the bundle to `ghcr.io/agentic-research/notme-action:v0.1.0` + signs via cosign + uploads Rekor entry.
- `cosign verify-blob --key <published-pubkey-url> --signature <sig> action/dist/index.js` returns OK.
- GitHub Release page links to the digest + the Rekor entry.

## Out of scope

- Verifier Action wrapping cosign verify-blob — sibling bead.
- Master key isolation via DO RPC — option (b) above; v0.2.
- Cloister-substrate migration — bead notme-e7a5ac and friends, now repositioned as v2.x.

## Anchors

- Parent epic: notme-e765fe (the strategic frame)
- Sibling pragmatic bead: verifier Action (filed alongside)
- cosign --key docs: https://docs.sigstore.dev/cosign/signing/signing_with_a_key/
- ghcr.io OCI docs: https://docs.github.com/en/packages/working-with-a-github-packages-registry/working-with-the-container-registry
- Rekor entry format: https://docs.sigstore.dev/logging/overview/

comments.text:
**Action SHA pins to use** (looked up 2026-05-12, saved to memory `reference-cosign-action-shas`):

| Action | Tag | Commit SHA |
|---|---|---|
| `sigstore/cosign-installer` | `v4.1.2` | `6f9f17788090df1f26f669e9d70d6ae9567deba6` |
| `actions/attest-build-provenance` | `v4.1.0` | `a2bbfa25375fe432b6a289bc6b6cd05ecd0c4c32` |
| `actions/attest` | `v4.1.0` | `59d89421af93a897026c735860bf21b6eb4f7b26` |

The canonical sigstore action is **`cosign-installer`** — installs the CLI; subsequent `run:` steps call `cosign sign-blob` / `cosign verify-blob`. There's no separate "sign action" — that pattern is intentional (the action ecosystem trusts the CLI surface).

Implementation skeleton for the release workflow:

```yaml
- uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@6f9f17788090df1f26f669e9d70d6ae9567deba6 # v4.1.2
- run: |
    cosign sign-blob \
      --key env://COSIGN_PRIVATE_KEY \
      --tlog-upload \
      --output-signature artifact.sig \
      --bundle artifact.bundle \
      ./action/dist/index.js
  env:
    COSIGN_PRIVATE_KEY: ${{ secrets.NOTME_RELEASE_SIGNING_KEY }}
    COSIGN_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.NOTME_RELEASE_SIGNING_PASS }}
```

`--tlog-upload` gets the Rekor entry for transparency without Fulcio dependency. The key is a separate notme release-signing key (option (a) from this bead's original description), generated specifically for action releases; the master Ed25519 in SigningAuthority DO stays for cert/bundle signing only.

**Bead scope unchanged.** The pins above replace any hand-rolled cosign invocation; everything else (workflow trigger, ghcr.io upload, GitHub Release creation) stays as described.

Re-verify SHAs at implementation time — `gh api repos/sigstore/cosign-installer/releases/latest` — since v4.1.2 may be superseded by then.

title: CAS-release v0.1b: verifier Action wrapping cosign verify-blob
description:
Part of the `notme/action-cas-release` thread. Companion to bead notme-XXXa (cosign+ghcr.io release path).

## What it does

A small bootstrap Action: `agentic-research/notme/verify-action@<sha>`. Consumer wires it into their workflow BEFORE invoking notme-action:

```yaml
- name: Verify notme-action signature
  uses: agentic-research/notme/verify-action@<pinned-sha>
  with:
    target: notme-action
    target_version: v1.0.0  # or @digest
    # pubkey URL defaults to https://auth.notme.bot/.well-known/notme-release.pub
- name: Use notme-action
  uses: agentic-research/notme-action@<digest-from-above>
  ...
```

The verify-action:

1. Pulls the OCI artifact from ghcr.io.
2. Pulls the cosign signature.
3. Fetches notme's release-signing pubkey from `auth.notme.bot/.well-known/notme-release.pub` (cached locally; consumer can pin).
4. Runs `cosign verify-blob` against bundle + signature + pubkey.
5. Optionally verifies the Rekor entry (transparency log proof).
6. Outputs the verified digest.

## Why a wrapper Action vs. inline `cosign verify-blob`

Three reasons:

1. **One-line consumer integration.** Consumers don't have to learn cosign CLI invocation; they `uses:` the wrapper.
2. **Default pubkey URL.** Hardcoding `auth.notme.bot/.well-known/notme-release.pub` means consumers don't have to copy-paste it. Bootstrap-key-distribution-by-convention.
3. **Failure mode handling.** The wrapper can give clear error messages ("signature mismatch — possible compromise; refusing to proceed") and fail the workflow before notme-action runs.

## What this avoids

The chicken-and-egg of "to verify notme-action, you need a verifier Action — but how do you trust the verifier?" The wrapper itself is SHA-pinned and small. Consumers audit it once (it's ~50 lines of bash calling cosign). It doesn't need to be content-addressed because its job is just to invoke cosign; the trust root is the published pubkey + Rekor, not the wrapper itself.

## Acceptance

- A consumer adds 2 steps to their workflow (verify-action + notme-action).
- If the bundle's signature is valid: verify-action passes, notme-action proceeds.
- If the bundle is tampered with (manually corrupt the ghcr.io artifact + try to fetch): verify-action fails with a clear cosign-verify-mismatch error.
- Optional: Rekor entry verification (cosign-side flag).

## Out of scope

- Cross-CI-system compatibility (the wrapper is GHA-specific). Other CI systems can call cosign directly using the same pubkey URL.
- Mirror to cloister registry (when bead notme-e7a5ac lands — verifier Action gains a `registry:` input to switch sources).

## Anchors

- Parent epic: notme-e765fe
- Sibling bead: cosign sign release workflow (filed alongside)
- cosign verify-blob: https://docs.sigstore.dev/cosign/verifying/verify/
- The pubkey distribution point: `auth.notme.bot/.well-known/notme-release.pub` (new well-known route to add)

comments.text:
**Action SHA pins for the verifier-side bootstrap** (memory: `reference-cosign-action-shas`, saved 2026-05-12):

```yaml
# verifier Action wraps:
- uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@6f9f17788090df1f26f669e9d70d6ae9567deba6 # v4.1.2
- run: |
    cosign verify-blob \
      --key https://auth.notme.bot/.well-known/notme-release.pub \
      --signature artifact.sig \
      --bundle artifact.bundle \
      ./fetched-action-bundle.js
```

The wrapper's job: hide the cosign CLI invocation behind a one-liner consumer interface (`uses: agentic-research/notme/verify-action@<sha>` with `target:` + `target_version:` inputs).

Per the [[feedback-pin-action-shas]] memory: every internal `uses:` in this wrapper Action also gets SHA-pinned. The wrapper itself is SHA-pinned by consumers.

**Cloister-substrate work explicitly out of scope per user direction 2026-05-12.** The 4 cloister-substrate beads (notme-e7a5ac, e7e1cf, e827df, e864f3) stay open as the v2.x destination but aren't on the v0.1 critical path. v0.1 = cosign + ghcr.io + Rekor only.

title: P0: DPoP downgrade attack + proof replay in shared verifier SDK
description:
Two vulnerabilities in gen/ts/dpop.ts:

1. CRITICAL — DPoP downgrade: verifyAccessToken accepts tokens with cnf.jkt claim. Attacker strips DPoP header, sends as Bearer. Must reject tokens with cnf claim in the Bearer path (RFC 9449 Section 3).

2. HIGH — Proof replay: verifyDPoPToken doesn't check iat or jti on the proof. Intercepted request replays work until token expiry (5 min). Must validate proof iat within 60s and require jti.
